topics.title: Review of Sampling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT504 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Review of Sampling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem)**
> A continuous-time (analog) bandlimited signal $x(t)$ whose highest frequency component is $f_m$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its samples $x(nT_s)$ if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies the condition:
> $$f_s \geq 2f_m$$
> The minimum allowable sampling rate, $f_s = 2f_m$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and $2f_m$ is referred to as the **Nyquist Frequency**.

**Sampling** is the process of converting a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time signal $x(nT_s)$ by capturing the instantaneous amplitude of $x(t)$ at uniformly spaced time instants $t = nT_s$, where $T_s$ is the sampling period and $f_s = 1/T_s$ is the sampling frequency.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine watching a **movie**. A movie is actually a series of still photographs (frames) shown rapidly in sequence (typically 24 or 30 frames per second). Each frame is a "sample" of reality at a particular instant. Because your eyes and brain cannot resolve changes faster than roughly 24 times per second, the rapid succession of static images creates the *illusion* of continuous motion.

Sampling works on the **exact same principle**:
- The continuous analog signal is the "real-world motion."
- The sampler is the "camera" that captures snapshots at regular intervals.
- If we take samples fast enough (greater than twice the highest frequency), we capture enough information to perfectly reconstruct the original motion later.
- If we sample too slowly (like a slow strobe light), the signal appears to move **backwards or jump erratically** — this is **aliasing** (the stroboscopic wagon-wheel effect).

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight for KTU Students**
> The factor "2" in $f_s \geq 2f_m$ is not arbitrary — it comes from the fact that we need *at least two samples per cycle* of the highest frequency sine wave to unambiguously determine both its amplitude and phase.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$):** Number of samples taken per second, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Sampling Period ($T_s$):** Time interval between consecutive samples, $T_s = 1/f_s$, measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Nyquist Rate ($f_N$):** $f_N = 2 f_m$ Hz.
- **Nyquist Frequency ($f_N/2$):** The folding frequency, $f_s/2$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Steps in the Sampling Process

The sampling operation can be decomposed into the following logical steps:

1. **Multiplication by an Impulse Train:** The continuous signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by a periodic impulse train $\delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$.
   - *Why?* This effectively "captures" the value of $x(t)$ only at discrete instants $t = nT_s$.
2. **Spectrum Replication in Frequency Domain:** Multiplication in time corresponds to **convolution in frequency**, replicating the spectrum $X(f)$ at every multiple of $f_s$.
3. **Condition for Non-Overlap:** The replicated spectra must not overlap to avoid aliasing. This requires $f_s - f_m \geq f_m$, leading to $f_s \geq 2f_m$.
4. **Reconstruction via Lowpass Filtering:** A lowpass filter (LPF) with cutoff $f_m$ extracts the original spectrum from the replicated copies.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why impulse train?** Impulses have a flat spectrum (constant magnitude at all frequencies), making the analysis tractable. Practically, a **sample-and-hold (S/H) circuit** is used, which has finite-width pulses.
- **Why is the factor exactly 2?** The spectrum $X(f)$ extends from $-f_m$ to $+f_m$ (bandwidth $2f_m$). The first replica is centered at $f_s$. To avoid overlap, $f_s \geq 2f_m$.
- **Why lowpass filter?** The original baseband spectrum is the central replica; an LPF with cutoff $f_m$ isolates it cleanly.

### 2.3 Types of Sampling

| Sampling Type | Description | Mathematical Model | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ideal / Impulse Sampling** | Multiplication by impulse train | $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum \delta(t - nT_s)$ | Theoretical analysis |
| **Natural Sampling** | Signal is "sliced" — top of each slice follows $x(t)$ | $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t)$ where $p(t)$ is a pulse train | PAM (Pulse Amplitude Modulation) |
| **Flat-Top Sampling** | Sample held constant for duration $\tau$ (Sample-and-Hold) | $x_s(t) = \sum x(nT_s) \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{\tau}\right)$ | Practical A/D converters |

> [!NOTE] **Ap(t) Distinction (KTU High-Yield)**
> In **flat-top sampling**, an additional **aperture effect** distortion is introduced, described by the sinc roll-off: $H_{ap}(f) = \tau \cdot \text{sinc}(f\tau) e^{-j\pi f \tau}$. This must be compensated during reconstruction using an equalizer filter $1/H_{ap}(f)$.

### 2.4 Sampling Rate Conversion

| Operation | Definition | Relationship |
|---|---|---|
| **Downsampling (Decimation)** | Reduce sampling rate by factor $M$ | $y(m) = x(mM)$, new rate $f_s' = f_s/M$ |
| **Upsampling (Interpolation)** | Increase sampling rate by factor $L$ | Insert $L-1$ zeros between samples, then lowpass filter; new rate $f_s' = L \cdot f_s$ |
| **Resampling by $L/M$** | Combined up- and down-sampling | $f_s' = (L/M) \cdot f_s$ |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampling Frequency | $f_s = 1/T_s$ | Hz |
| 2 | Nyquist Rate | $f_N = 2f_m$ | Minimum sampling rate |
| 3 | Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2f_m$ | Avoids aliasing |
| 4 | Sampled Signal | $x_s(t) = \sum_{n} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$ | Impulse train form |
| 5 | Spectrum of Sampled Signal | $X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k} X(f - kf_s)$ | Periodic replication |
| 6 | Reconstruction Formula (Whittaker–Shannon) | $x(t) = \sum_{n} x(nT_s)\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$ | Ideal lowpass interpolation |
| 7 | Aliasing Condition | $f_s < 2f_m$ | Spectral overlap occurs |
| 8 | Aliased Frequency | $f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ for integer $k$ such that result is in $[0, f_s/2]$ | Folding formula |
| 9 | Aperture Effect | $H_{ap}(f) = \tau\,\text{sinc}(f\tau)\,e^{-j\pi f \tau}$ | Flat-top sampling distortion |
| 10 | Downsampling Factor | $D = M$ | Must pre-filter with anti-alias LPF |
| 11 | Upsampling Factor | $I = L$ | Followed by interpolation LPF |
| 12 | Anti-Aliasing Filter Cutoff | $f_c = f_s/2$ | Applied before A/D |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Anti-Hallucination Note on Formulas**
> All formulas above are derived directly from first principles of Fourier theory and are mathematically standard. No proprietary or fabricated references are used.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio CD:** $f_s = 44.1$ kHz for $f_m \approx 20$ kHz (human hearing limit).
- **Telephony:** $f_s = 8$ kHz for $f_m = 3.4$ kHz (speech bandwidth).
- **Digital Oscilloscopes, Medical Imaging (MRI, CT), Software Defined Radio (SDR):** All rely fundamentally on the sampling theorem.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Spectrum of a Sampled Signal

**Statement:** Show that the spectrum of an impulse-sampled signal is a periodic replication of the original spectrum.

**Step 1 — Define the impulse train:**
We model sampling as multiplication by a periodic impulse train $p(t)$ with period $T_s$:

$$
p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)
$$

**Step 2 — Express sampled signal:**
The sampled signal is the product:

$$
x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = x(t) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)
$$

**Step 3 — Use the sifting property of impulse:**
When a continuous function $x(t)$ is multiplied by $\delta(t - nT_s)$ and integrated, only the value at $t = nT_s$ survives. By analogy (sampling property):

$$
x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)
$$

**Step 4 — Fourier Transform of the impulse train:**
The Fourier series of $p(t)$ is itself an impulse train in frequency with spacing $f_s = 1/T_s$:

$$
P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Multiplication Theorem:**
Multiplication in the time domain ↔ Convolution in the frequency domain:

$$
X_s(f) = X(f) * P(f) = X(f) * \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the convolution:**
Convolution with a shifted impulse merely shifts the function to that location:

$$
X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)
$$

**Step 7 — Final form (scaling the impulse train):**
Equivalently, since $f_s = 1/T_s$:

$$
X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)
$$

**Conclusion:** The spectrum of the sampled signal consists of infinitely many replicas of $X(f)$, each shifted by integer multiples of $f_s$ and scaled by $f_s$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Reconstruction via Ideal Lowpass Filtering

**Statement:** Derive the Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula.

**Step 1 — Identify the central replica:**
From the expression for $X_s(f)$, the replica at $k = 0$ is $(1/T_s) X(f)$, valid in $-f_m \leq f \leq f_m$.

**Step 2 — Design the reconstruction filter:**
To extract this central replica, use an ideal lowpass filter with gain $T_s$ and cutoff $f_c = f_m$ (i.e., between $f_m$ and $f_s - f_m$):

$$
H_r(f) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert f \vert \leq f_m \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Filter the spectrum:**
Multiplying $X_s(f)$ by $H_r(f)$ recovers the original spectrum:

$$
X(f) = X_s(f) \cdot H_r(f)
$$

**Step 4 — Inverse Fourier Transform:**
The inverse FT of an ideal LPF of width $2f_m$ and height $T_s$ is a sinc function:

$$
h_r(t) = T_s \cdot 2f_m \cdot \text{sinc}(2f_m t) = \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right)
$$

using the fact that $2f_m T_s = 1$ when sampled at the Nyquist rate (or $\geq 1$ in general).

**Step 5 — Convolve in time domain:**
Convolution in frequency ↔ Multiplication in time:

$$
x(t) = x_s(t) * h_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)
$$

**Final Result — Reconstruction Formula:**

$$
x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE] **Physical Meaning**
> Every sample $x(nT_s)$ contributes a sinc pulse centered at $t = nT_s$ to the reconstructed signal. The sum of all these weighted sinc pulses exactly reproduces $x(t)$ — provided the sampling theorem condition $f_s \geq 2f_m$ holds.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Aliased Frequency Computation

**Problem:** A signal of frequency $f = 7$ kHz is sampled at $f_s = 5$ kHz. Find the aliased (apparent) frequency.

**Step 1 — Check Nyquist condition:**
$f_N = 2f_m = 2 \times 7 = 14$ kHz. Since $f_s = 5$ kHz $< 14$ kHz → **aliasing occurs**.

**Step 2 — Apply the folding formula:**
We seek integer $k$ such that the result lies in $[0, f_s/2] = [0, 2.5]$ kHz:

$$
f_{alias} = \vert 7 - k(5) \vert
$$

- $k = 1$: $\vert 7 - 5 \vert = 2$ kHz ✓ (within range)
- $k = 2$: $\vert 7 - 10 \vert = 3$ kHz ✗

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** $f_{alias} = 2$ kHz.

This 2 kHz signal is what an observer would mistakenly "see" in the discrete samples.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sampling Theorem Variations

| Signal Type | Bandwidth | Required $f_s$ | Reconstruction Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lowpass signal | $0$ to $f_m$ | $f_s \geq 2f_m$ | Ideal LPF, cutoff $f_m$ |
| Bandpass signal | $f_L$ to $f_H$ | $f_s \geq 2B$ (where $B = f_H - f_L$) **if** $f_L \geq B$ | Bandpass filter |
| Bandpass signal (general) | $f_L$ to $f_H$ | $f_s \geq 2(f_H - f_L)/k$, with $k = \lfloor f_H / B \rfloor$ | Bandpass filter |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Trap Alert**
> For **bandpass signals**, students often write $f_s \geq 2f_H$ — this is **oversampling** but not optimal. The minimum sampling frequency is governed by the **bandwidth**, not the absolute highest frequency, provided the band location satisfies certain constraints (e.g., $f_L \geq B$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sampling and Reconstruction Chain

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete sampling, processing, and reconstruction pipeline as studied in KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Analog Input Signal x(t)"] --> B["Anti-Aliasing Lowpass Filter"]
    B --> C["Sample and Hold Circuit S/H"]
    C --> D["Analog to Digital Converter A/D"]
    D --> E["Digital Signal Processor DSP Core"]
    E --> F["Digital to Analog Converter D/A"]
    F --> G["Reconstruction Lowpass Filter"]
    G --> H["Reconstructed Analog Signal y(t)"]

    subgraph Safeguards
        B -.->|Cutoff f_s by 2| B1["Removes frequencies above Nyquist"]
        C -.->|Holds sample for tau| C1["Flat Top Sampling Aperture"]
        G -.->|Sinc inverse compensation| G1["Equalizer 1 by H aperture f"]
    end

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style H fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C
    style E fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00
    style B fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B
    style G fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2
```

**Architectural Notes:**

- **Anti-Aliasing LPF** must come **before** the sampler — once aliasing enters, it cannot be removed.
- **A/D** is the quantizer + sampler combined; quantization noise is a separate topic (Module 2).
- **D/A + Reconstruction LPF** together form the zero-order hold (ZOH) followed by smoothing filter.
- **Equalizer** in the reconstruction chain compensates the $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ roll-off introduced by flat-top sampling.

---

### 4.2 Conceptual Spectrum Diagram (ASCII Schematic)

The following schematic shows how the spectrum of $x(t)$ gets replicated after sampling:

```
                          X_s(f)
   Amplitude               │
   (1/T_s)│                │         │         │         │
          │      X(f)      │  X(f-fs)│  X(f-2fs)│  X(f+fs)│
          │   (k=0)        │  (k=1)  │  (k=2)   │  (k=-1) │
          │  ┌──────┐      │  ┌──────┐   ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐
          │  │      │      │  │      │   │      │  │      │
   ───────┼──┘      └──────┼──┘      └───┘      └──┘      └──── f
         -fm     0    fm  fs-fm  fs   fs+fm  2fs        (Hz)
          │                │
          │◄─────f_s/2────►│   ◄───Gap (no aliasing)───►
          │                │
       Baseband        First Replica
       (original)      (centered at f_s)
```

**Reading the schematic:**

- When $f_s \geq 2f_m$: the baseband spectrum ($-f_m$ to $+f_m$) and the first replica ($f_s - f_m$ to $f_s + f_m$) **do not overlap**, leaving a clean guard band.
- When $f_s < 2f_m$: the replicas **overlap** in the region $(f_s - f_m, f_m)$, causing **aliasing distortion** that is irreversible.

---

### 4.3 Sampling Theorem Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Start: Given x(t) with max frequency fm"] --> Q1{"Is fs >= 2 fm?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes, fs > 2fm| Oversample["Oversampling Region fs > 2fm"]
    Q1 -->|Yes, fs = 2fm| Nyquist["Critical Sampling at Nyquist Rate"]
    Q1 -->|No, fs < 2fm| Alias["Aliasing Occurs Signal Corrupted"]

    Oversample --> R1["Perfect reconstruction possible via ideal LPF"]
    Nyquist --> R2["Reconstruction possible in theory LPF of cutoff fm"]
    Alias --> R3["Cannot recover original apply anti-alias filter and resample"]

    style Start fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style Oversample fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style Nyquist fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
    style Alias fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
