## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q: State and explain the Nyquist sampling theorem. Define Nyquist rate and Nyquist frequency.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The Nyquist sampling theorem states that a bandlimited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ having maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its discrete samples if the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2f_m$. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Nyquist Rate:** The minimum sampling rate required for distortion-free reconstruction, given by $f_N = 2f_m$. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Nyquist Frequency:** Half of the sampling frequency, $f_s/2$, which is the highest baseband frequency that can be unambiguously represented. It also equals the folding frequency. **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q: What is aliasing? How can it be prevented?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Aliasing:** Aliasing is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of a signal appear as false lower frequencies in the sampled signal due to insufficient sampling rate ($f_s < 2f_m$). The overlapping of spectral replicas causes irreversible distortion. **(1.5 marks)**
>
> **Prevention Methods:** **(1.5 marks)**
> 1. Sample at a rate $f_s \geq 2f_m$ (satisfy Nyquist criterion).
> 2. Use an **anti-aliasing lowpass filter** with cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$ before the sampler to bandlimit the input.
> 3. Increase sampling rate using oversampling A/D converters.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q3]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**a)** State and prove the sampling theorem for lowpass signals. Derive the expression for the spectrum of the sampled signal. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the different types of sampling (natural, flat-top, ideal). Compare their spectra and state the aperture effect in flat-top sampling. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1 — Statement:** *(1 mark)*
A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples $x(nT_s)$ if $f_s \geq 2f_m$.

**Step 2 — Sampling Model:** *(1 mark)*
Multiplication by impulse train:

$$
x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t), \quad p(t) = \sum_{n} \delta(t - nT_s)
$$

**Step 3 — Use Sifting Property:** *(1 mark)*

$$
x_s(t) = \sum_{n} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)
$$

**Step 4 — Fourier Transform of Impulse Train:** *(1 mark)*

$$
P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k} \delta(f - kf_s)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Multiplication Theorem:** *(1 mark)*

$$
X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s)
$$

**Step 6 — Reconstruction via LPF:** *(1 mark)*
An ideal LPF with gain $T_s$ and cutoff $f_m$ extracts the $k = 0$ term, recovering $X(f)$, hence $x(t)$.

**Step 7 — Nyquist Condition:** *(1 mark)*
For no overlap of replicas: $f_s - f_m \geq f_m \Rightarrow f_s \geq 2f_m$. ∎

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

| Type | Time-Domain Operation | Spectrum | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ideal (Impulse)** | Multiply by $\delta_{T_s}(t)$ | $f_s \sum X(f - kf_s)$ | Theoretical baseline; flat envelope |
| **Natural** | Multiply by pulse train $p(t)$ of width $\tau$ | $\frac{\tau}{T_s} \sum \text{sinc}(k f_s \tau) e^{-j\pi k f_s \tau} X(f - kf_s)$ | Pulse top follows $x(t)$ |
| **Flat-Top (S/H)** | Hold sample for $\tau$ | $\frac{\tau}{T_s} \sum \text{sinc}(k f_s \tau) e^{-j\pi k f_s \tau} X(f - kf_s)$ | Pulse held constant; introduces **aperture effect** $H_{ap}(f) = \tau \text{sinc}(f\tau) e^{-j\pi f \tau}$ |

**Aperture Effect (3 marks):** In flat-top sampling, holding each sample for duration $\tau$ acts as a convolution with a rectangular pulse, equivalent in frequency to multiplication by $\tau \text{sinc}(f\tau) e^{-j\pi f\tau}$. This causes a **sinc-shaped roll-off** (amplitude distortion) and **linear phase** (delay). The effect is compensated by an equalizer $1/H_{ap}(f)$ in the reconstruction stage.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**a)** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ has maximum frequency $f_m = 4$ kHz. **(7 marks)**
   (i) Determine the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing.
   (ii) If the signal is sampled at $f_s = 6$ kHz, find the range of frequencies in the baseband that will be alias-free.
   (iii) A 10 kHz component exists in the signal. Find its apparent (aliased) frequency at $f_s = 6$ kHz.

**b)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the complete process of sampling, quantization, and reconstruction of a signal. Explain the role of anti-aliasing and reconstruction filters. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**(i) Minimum Sampling Rate:** *(2 marks)*

$$
f_s^{\min} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(ii) Alias-Free Baseband at $f_s = 6$ kHz:** *(2 marks)*
Since $f_s = 6$ kHz, the Nyquist frequency is $f_s/2 = 3$ kHz. The baseband spectrum from $0$ to $3$ kHz will be alias-free; components from $3$ to $4$ kHz will be **aliased** (fold back into $2$ to $3$ kHz).

**(iii) Aliased Frequency of 10 kHz at $f_s = 6$ kHz:** *(3 marks)*
Apply folding formula with $k = 2$ (try nearest multiple of 6 kHz):

$$
f_{alias} = \vert 10 - (2)(6) \vert = \vert 10 - 12 \vert = 2 \text{ kHz}
$$

(Verify: $\vert 10 - 1 \cdot 6 \vert = 4$ kHz — outside $[0, 3]$ kHz, so $k=2$ is correct.)

**Apparent frequency = 2 kHz.**

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Block Diagram of Sampling & Reconstruction (4 marks):**

```
x(t) → [Anti-Alias LPF] → [Sampler] → [Quantizer + Coder] 
        → [Transmission/Storage] → [Decoder] → [Reconstruction LPF] → y(t) ≈ x(t)
```

**Roles (3 marks):**

- **Anti-Aliasing Filter (Pre-Filter):** A lowpass filter placed *before* the sampler to bandlimit the input signal to $f_s/2$. This ensures that no frequency above the Nyquist frequency enters the sampler, preventing aliasing.
- **Sampler:** Converts continuous-time signal to discrete-time by capturing values at $t = nT_s$.
- **Quantizer + Coder:** Rounds each sample to the nearest level and encodes it in binary (introduces quantization noise).
- **Reconstruction LPF (Post-Filter):** After D/A conversion, this lowpass filter with cutoff $f_s/2$ smooths the staircase-like output to recover the original analog waveform.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse the **Nyquist Rate** ($2f_m$) with the **Nyquist Frequency** ($f_s/2$). Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In the aliased frequency problem, students forget to choose the integer $k$ such that the result lies in $[0, f_s/2]$. Always verify the folded frequency falls in this interval.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In flat-top sampling, students write the spectrum formula but forget the **aperture effect equalizer** requirement — this costs 2 marks in reconstruction problems.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In bandpass signal sampling, blindly using $f_s \geq 2f_H$ instead of the bandwidth-based formula. Examiners *expect* the optimal $f_s$ formula for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Review of Sampling"**

- [x] **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2f_m$ is the necessary and sufficient condition for perfect reconstruction of a bandlimited signal.
- [x] **Nyquist Rate:** $f_N = 2f_m$ (minimum sampling rate).
- [x] **Nyquist Frequency:** $f_s/2$ (half of sampling rate; also called the folding frequency).
- [x] **Aliasing:** Occurs when $f_s < 2f_m$. High frequencies fold into low frequencies — irreversible. **Prevention:** Anti-aliasing LPF before sampler.
- [x] **Sampled Spectrum:** $X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k} X(f - kf_s)$ — periodic replication of $X(f)$ with period $f_s$.
- [x] **Reconstruction Formula (Whittaker–Shannon):** $x(t) = \sum_{n} x(nT_s)\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$.
- [x] **Three Sampling Types:** Ideal (impulse), Natural (pulse-top tracks signal), Flat-Top (S/H — constant top).
- [x] **Aperture Effect:** $H_{ap}(f) = \tau \text{sinc}(f\tau) e^{-j\pi f \tau}$ — distortion unique to flat-top sampling; corrected by an equalizer.
- [x] **Downsampling by $M$:** Must apply anti-alias LPF at $f_s/(2M)$ *before* decimation.
- [x] **Upsampling by $L$:** Insert $L-1$ zeros, then apply interpolation LPF at $f_s'/2$.
- [x] **Audio CD Standard:** $f_s = 44.1$ kHz for $f_m \approx 20$ kHz.
- [x] **Telephony Standard:** $f_s = 8$ kHz for speech band of $3.4$ kHz.
- [x] **Bandpass Sampling:** Minimum $f_s$ depends on signal bandwidth $B = f_H - f_L$, not $f_H$ alone, when $f_L \geq B$.

> **End of Module 1 — Review of Sampling Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Z-Transform and DTFT; The Discrete Fourier Transform - DFT as a Linear Transformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT504 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Z-Transform and DTFT; The Discrete Fourier Transform - DFT as a Linear Transformation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 The Z-Transform

The **Z-Transform** of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is formally defined as the infinite power series:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n}$$

where $z$ is a complex variable. This transform is the discrete-time counterpart of the continuous-time Laplace Transform. It converts a discrete sequence in the time domain into a complex-valued function in the z-domain, thereby exposing the algebraic structure (polynomials/rational functions) of the signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Z-Transform is a fundamental tool for the analysis and representation of discrete-time signals and linear time-invariant (LTI) systems. It is the discrete equivalent of the Laplace Transform and provides a unified framework for stability, causality, and frequency-response analysis.

**Region of Convergence (ROC):** The set of all values of $z$ in the complex plane for which the infinite sum $X(z)$ converges (i.e., $|X(z)| < \infty$).

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Recipe Decoder":**
Imagine you have a smoothie (your digital signal $x[n]$) made by blending a sequence of fruits, one after another. Each fruit is added in a specific order and at a specific time. The Z-Transform is like a special "recipe decoder" that, instead of telling you *when* each fruit went in, tells you the **cumulative weight** of the recipe when stretched or compressed by a complex stretching factor $z^{-1}$ at every step. The ROC is the set of stretching factors for which this cumulative weight actually makes sense (converges to a finite number) — like the range of blender speeds for which the smoothie doesn't fly out of the jar.

### 1.2 The Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)

The **DTFT** of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is defined as:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

The DTFT is essentially a special case of the Z-Transform evaluated **on the unit circle** of the z-plane, i.e., at $z = e^{j\omega}$. It provides the frequency-domain representation of an aperiodic discrete-time sequence.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Prism for Digital Sound":**
Think of a digital audio signal as white light — a mixture of many different pure tones (frequencies) added together. The DTFT acts like a **mathematical prism**: it separates $x[n]$ into its constituent pure-frequency "colors" $X(e^{j\omega})$ and tells you *exactly* how much of each frequency is present. The variable $\omega$ (radians/sample) is the angular position around the prism where each color emerges.

### 1.3 The Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) as a Linear Transformation

The **DFT** of a finite-length sequence $x[n]$ of length $N$ is defined as:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

Because this equation is a **linear combination** of the input samples $x[n]$ (with complex exponential weights), the DFT can be written compactly as a matrix-vector product:

$$\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W} \cdot \mathbf{x}$$

where $\mathbf{W}$ is the $N \times N$ **DFT matrix** whose $(k, n)$-th entry is $W_N^{kn} = e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$, and $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{X}$ are the $N \times 1$ input and output column vectors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Linear Transformation"?** A linear transformation $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$ must satisfy $T(\alpha\mathbf{x}_1 + \beta\mathbf{x}_2) = \alpha T(\mathbf{x}_1) + \beta T(\mathbf{x}_2)$. Since the DFT sum is linear in $x[n]$ and the exponential weights are constants (do not depend on $x$), the DFT qualifies as a linear map. Recognizing it as such unlocks the powerful language of linear algebra (eigenvectors, matrix inverses, etc.) for spectrum analysis.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Audio Equalizer Grid":**
Picture a sound equalizer with $N$ sliders, each controlling one discrete frequency band. The DFT treats the time-domain signal $x[n]$ as $N$ numbers fed into the grid. The matrix $\mathbf{W}$ is a *fixed set of instructions* that says, "Add up all the input samples, but rotate each one by a different precise angle before adding." The result, $\mathbf{X}$, is the equalizer's display: $N$ frequency-band magnitudes. The matrix view reveals that **all frequency analyses are just a single, fixed linear recipe applied to the data**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Z-Transform: Structural Mechanics

The Z-Transform $X(z)$ operates on $x[n]$ as a **bilinear** generating function. Its core structure reveals:

- **One-sided vs Two-sided**: The one-sided Z-Transform starts summation at $n=0$ (used for causal systems with initial conditions). The two-sided form runs over all integers.
- **ROC Properties (KTU High-Yield)**:
  * The ROC is a **ring** (annular region) in the z-plane: $r_2 < \vert z \vert < r_1$.
  * ROC cannot contain any pole of $X(z)$.
  * If $x[n]$ is **finite-duration**, the ROC is the entire z-plane except possibly $z=0$ and/or $z=\infty$.
  * If $x[n]$ is **right-sided**, the ROC extends outward from the outermost pole to $\infty$.
  * If $x[n]$ is **left-sided**, the ROC extends inward from the innermost pole to $0$.
  * If $x[n]$ is **two-sided**, the ROC is a ring bounded by poles.
  * A **stable & causal** LTI system has ROC that **includes the unit circle** and extends outward to $\infty$.

- **Poles and Zeros**: Poles (roots of denominator) and zeros (roots of numerator) of $X(z)$ completely characterize the system in the z-domain.

### 2.2 DTFT: Relationship with Z-Transform

The DTFT is the Z-Transform **sampled on the unit circle**:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z)\Big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$$

This holds **only if the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ lies within the ROC**. The DTFT is **periodic in $\omega$ with period $2\pi$**, reflecting the inherent aliasing of discrete-time frequency content.

**Existence of DTFT:** The DTFT converges (in the uniform sense) if and only if $x[n]$ is **absolutely summable**:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$$

### 2.3 DFT as a Linear Transformation: Structural Mechanics

The DFT can be viewed as three nested concepts:

1. **Sampled DTFT**: The DFT is the DTFT evaluated at $N$ uniformly spaced frequencies $\omega_k = 2\pi k / N$, $k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$.
2. **Linear Map**: Each output sample $X[k]$ is a linear combination of the input samples $x[n]$.
3. **Matrix Equation**: $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{x}$, where

$$
W_N = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & W_N & W_N^2 & \cdots & W_N^{N-1} \\
1 & W_N^2 & W_N^4 & \cdots & W_N^{2(N-1)} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
1 & W_N^{N-1} & W_N^{2(N-1)} & \cdots & W_N^{(N-1)^2}
\end{bmatrix}, \quad W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}
$$

**Key Properties of $\mathbf{W}_N$:**
- $\mathbf{W}_N$ is a **Vandermonde** matrix.
- $\mathbf{W}_N$ is **symmetric** (not Hermitian).
- $\mathbf{W}_N^N = \mathbf{I}$ (because $W_N^N = e^{-j2\pi} = 1$), so $\mathbf{W}_N$ is a **root-of-unity** matrix.
- The IDFT is given by the inverse matrix: $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{W}_N^{-1} \mathbf{X}$, where $\mathbf{W}_N^{-1} = \frac{1}{N}\mathbf{W}_N^*$.
- The matrix is **unitary up to scaling** ($\mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{W}_N^* = N \mathbf{I}$).

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Domain | Application of Z-Transform | Application of DTFT | Application of DFT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Speech Processing** | Pole-zero modeling of vocal tract | Spectral envelope estimation | Spectral display in vocoders |
| **Image Processing** | Recursive 2-D filters | Frequency-domain filtering | JPEG block transform |
| **Communications** | Channel equalizer design | Matched filtering | OFDM modulator/demodulator |
| **Biomedical** | ECG/EEG denoising | Heart-rate spectral analysis | Heart-rate variability FFT |
| **Control Systems** | Discrete controller design | Bode plot generation | Discrete spectrum analysis |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Z-Transform | $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, z^{-n}$ | Bilateral form |
| 2 | One-sided Z-Transform | $X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n]\, z^{-n}$ | For causal sequences |
| 3 | DTFT | $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$ | Periodic in $\omega$, period $2\pi$ |
| 4 | DTFT $\leftrightarrow$ Z-Transform | $X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z)\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$ | Only if unit circle $\subset$ ROC |
| 5 | DFT (Analysis) | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$ | $k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$ |
| 6 | IDFT (Synthesis) | $x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] e^{j2\pi kn/N}$ | $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$ |
| 7 | Twiddle Factor | $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ | Fundamental $N$-th root of unity |
| 8 | DFT as Matrix | $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{x}$ | Linear transformation |
| 9 | IDFT Matrix | $\mathbf{W}_N^{-1} = \frac{1}{N}\mathbf{W}_N^*$ | Conjugate transpose divided by $N$ |
| 10 | Twiddle Periodicity | $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^k$ | $W_N^N = 1$ |
| 11 | Z-Transform of $\delta[n]$ | $1$ | ROC: entire z-plane |
| 12 | Z-Transform of $u[n]$ | $\frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z-1}$ | ROC: $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| 13 | Z-Transform of $a^n u[n]$ | $\frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z-a}$ | ROC: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| 14 | DTFT Existence | $\sum \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$ | Absolute summability condition |
| 15 | DFT Matrix Property | $\mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{W}_N^* = N \mathbf{I}$ | Unitary (up to $\sqrt{N}$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In exam answers, always state the ROC explicitly when writing a Z-Transform. Marks are routinely docked when ROC is missing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: From Z-Transform to DTFT

**Starting point:** The bilateral Z-Transform is

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, z^{-n}$$

**Step 1 — Restrict $z$ to the unit circle.** Substitute $z = r e^{j\omega}$ where $r$ is the magnitude and $\omega$ is the angle. Then

$$z^{-n} = r^{-n} e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2 — Set $r = 1$ (place on unit circle).** With $r=1$, we have $z^{-n} = e^{-j\omega n}$, so

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 3 — Convergence condition.** This substitution is only valid if the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ lies within the ROC of $X(z)$. This requires the existence condition

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$$

If $x[n]$ is absolutely summable, the DTFT exists uniformly and equals the Z-Transform on the unit circle.

**Step 4 — Periodicity.** Since $e^{-j(\omega + 2\pi)n} = e^{-j\omega n}$, we have

$$X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})$$

confirming $2\pi$-periodicity.

### 3.2 Derivation: From DTFT to DFT (Sampling in Frequency)

**Starting point:** DTFT of a finite-length $N$-point sequence $x[n]$, $0 \le n \le N-1$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 1 — Sample $\omega$ uniformly.** Choose $N$ equally spaced frequencies in one period $[0, 2\pi)$:

$$\omega_k = \frac{2\pi k}{N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.** Replace $\omega$ with $\omega_k$:

$$X[k] = X(e^{j\omega_k}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega_k n} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}$$

This is precisely the **DFT**.

**Step 3 — Define twiddle factor.** Let $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$. Then

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, W_N^{kn}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: DFT as a Linear Transformation (Matrix Form)

**Step 1 — Write out the $N$ DFT equations explicitly.** For $k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X[0] &= x[0] \cdot 1 + x[1] \cdot 1 + \cdots + x[N-1] \cdot 1 \\
X[1] &= x[0] \cdot 1 + x[1] \cdot W_N + x[2] \cdot W_N^2 + \cdots + x[N-1] \cdot W_N^{N-1} \\
X[2] &= x[0] \cdot 1 + x[1] \cdot W_N^2 + x[2] \cdot W_N^4 + \cdots + x[N-1] \cdot W_N^{2(N-1)} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
X[k] &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, W_N^{kn}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the linear system.** Each $X[k]$ is a **linear combination** of $\{x[0], x[1], \dots, x[N-1]\}$ with **constant** complex weights $W_N^{kn}$. Hence the entire DFT can be packed into a single matrix equation:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X[0] \\ X[1] \\ \vdots \\ X[N-1] \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\ 1 & W_N & W_N^2 & \cdots & W_N^{N-1} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & W_N^{N-1} & W_N^{2(N-1)} & \cdots & W_N^{(N-1)^2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x[0] \\ x[1] \\ \vdots \\ x[N-1] \end{bmatrix}$$

In compact notation: $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{x}$.

**Step 3 — Verify linearity.** Let $\mathbf{y} = \alpha \mathbf{x}_1 + \beta \mathbf{x}_2$. Then

$$\mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{y} = \alpha \mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{x}_1 + \beta \mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{x}_2$$

which is precisely the linearity condition. The DFT is a **linear operator**.

### 3.4 Worked Example: 4-Point DFT as Matrix Multiplication

Let $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$. Compute $X[k]$ using the matrix form.

**Step 1 — Compute the twiddle factors.** For $N=4$:

$$W_4 = e^{-j2\pi/4} = e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$$

So the powers of $W_4$ cycle as: $W_4^0 = 1$, $W_4^1 = -j$, $W_4^2 = -1$, $W_4^3 = j$, $W_4^4 = 1$, etc.

**Step 2 — Construct the 4×4 DFT matrix.**

$$\mathbf{W}_4 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -j & -1 & j \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & j & -1 & -j \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Perform the matrix-vector multiplication.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X[0] &= 1(1) + 1(2) + 1(3) + 1(4) = 10 \\
X[1] &= 1(1) + (-j)(2) + (-1)(3) + (j)(4) = -2 + 2j \\
X[2] &= 1(1) + (-1)(2) + 1(3) + (-1)(4) = -2 \\
X[3] &= 1(1) + (j)(2) + (-1)(3) + (-j)(4) = -2 - 2j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final DFT vector.**

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ -2 + 2j \\ -2 \\ -2 - 2j \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Note:** The DFT is conjugate-symmetric ($X[3] = X[1]^*$) because the input $x[n]$ is real. This is a powerful KTU-style sanity check.

### 3.5 Inverse DFT via Matrix Inversion

Since $\mathbf{W}_N^{-1} = \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{W}_N^*$, we have $\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{W}_N^* \mathbf{X}$.

**Verify for the worked example** by reconstructing $x[0]$:

$$
x[0] = \frac{1}{4}\left[1 \cdot X[0] + 1 \cdot X[1] + 1 \cdot X[2] + 1 \cdot X[3]\right] = \frac{1}{4}(10 - 2 + 2j - 2 - 2 - 2j) = \frac{4}{4} = 1 \;\checkmark
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Hierarchical Relationship: Z-Transform → DTFT → DFT

This diagram illustrates the conceptual hierarchy: the Z-Transform is the most general; the DTFT is its evaluation on the unit circle; the DFT is a frequency-sampled, finite-length version of the DTFT.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Z Transform X of z"] -->|"Evaluate on unit circle z equals e to j omega"| B["DTFT X of e to j omega"]
    B -->|"Sample N frequencies uniformly in 0 to 2 pi"| C["DFT X of k"]
    A -->|"Finite length N point sequence"| C
    C -->|"Write as matrix vector product"| D["Linear Transformation X equals W sub N times x"]
    B -->|"Periodic with period 2 pi in omega"| E["DTFT is 2 pi periodic"]
    C -->|"Periodic with period N in k"| F["DFT is N periodic"]
    A -->|"ROC determines existence region"| G["Region of Convergence in z plane"]
```

### 4.2 DFT as Linear Transformation: Block Functional Architecture

This block diagram describes the data flow when the DFT is implemented as a matrix-vector multiplication.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Time Domain Vector x of n"]
        i0["x of 0"]
        i1["x of 1"]
        i2["x of 2"]
        iN["x of N minus 1"]
    end
    subgraph WMATRIX["DFT Matrix W sub N of size N by N"]
        m00["W sub N to power 0 0 equals 1"]
        mkk["W sub N to power k n equals e to minus j 2 pi k n by N"]
        mNN["W sub N to power N minus 1 squared"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Frequency Domain Vector X of k"]
        o0["X of 0 DC component"]
        o1["X of 1 First harmonic"]
        oK["X of k General bin"]
        oN["X of N minus 1 Highest bin"]
    end
    i0 --> m00
    i1 --> mkk
    i2 --> mkk
    iN --> mNN
    m00 --> o0
    mkk --> oK
    mNN --> oN
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow: Z-Transform Analysis Pipeline

This flowchart depicts how a digital signal is analyzed step by step using the Z-Transform, DTFT, and DFT in sequence.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S["Discrete time signal x of n"] --> P1{"Is sequence absolutely summable?"}
    P1 -->|"Yes"| Z1["Compute X of z using Z Transform"]
    P1 -->|"No for DTFT directly"| Z2["Use Z Transform with ROC analysis"]
    Z1 --> Q1{"Does unit circle lie in ROC?"}
    Z2 --> Q1
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| DT["Evaluate X of e to j omega to get DTFT"]
    Q1 -->|"No"| NA["DTFT does not exist in classical sense"]
    DT --> R1["Sample N frequencies omega k equals 2 pi k by N"]
    R1 --> DFT["Compute X of k equals sum of x of n e to minus j 2 pi k n by N"]
    DFT --> MAT["Write as matrix equation X equals W sub N times x"]
    MAT --> OUT["Frequency domain representation obtained"]
```

### 4.4 ROC Anatomy in the z-Plane

This conceptual diagram shows the typical ROC configuration for a right-sided causal sequence.

```mermaid
graph TD
    ZP["Z plane"] --> ORIGIN["Origin at z equals 0"]
    ZP --> UC["Unit circle at z absolute value equals 1"]
    ZP --> P["Pole at z equals a"]
    P -->|"Boundary of ROC"| R["ROC: z absolute value greater than a extends to infinity"]
    UC -->|"If a less than 1, unit circle is inside ROC"| R
    R --> STABLE["Stable system: ROC includes unit circle"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]** Define the Z-Transform of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$. What is the Region of Convergence (ROC)?

**Model Answer:**
The bilateral Z-Transform of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is defined as
$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, z^{-n}$$
where $z$ is a complex variable. The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** is the set of all values of $z$ in the complex plane for which this infinite sum converges to a finite value, i.e., for which $\vert X(z) \vert < \infty$. The ROC is essential because different signals can have identical $X(z)$ expressions but different ROCs.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for ROC definition, 1 for distinct-ROC remark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** State the relation between the DTFT and the Z-Transform. Under what condition does the DTFT exist?

**Model Answer:**
The DTFT is obtained by evaluating the Z-Transform of $x[n]$ on the unit circle $z = e^{j\omega}$:
$$X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z)\Big\vert_{z=e^{j\omega}} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$
The DTFT exists (in the uniform convergence sense) if and only if the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ lies within the ROC of $X(z)$, which is equivalent to the condition that $x[n]$ is absolutely summable:
$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$$

**(3 marks: 1 for relation, 1 for existence condition, 1 for absolute summability statement)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Define the Z-Transform and the DTFT. Derive the DTFT from the Z-Transform and explain the periodicity property. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the 4-point DFT of the sequence $x[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$ using the matrix formulation. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Z-Transform definition.**
$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, z^{-n}, \quad z \in \text{ROC}$$

**Step 2 — DTFT definition.**
$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 3 — Derivation.** Substitute $z = r e^{j\omega}$:
$$X(r e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, r^{-n} e^{-j\omega n}$$
On the unit circle, $r = 1$, so $z^{-n} = e^{-j\omega n}$, giving the DTFT.

**Step 4 — Periodicity.** Because $e^{-j(\omega + 2\pi)n} = e^{-j\omega n}$ for integer $n$, we get
$$X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})$$
Hence DTFT is $2\pi$-periodic in $\omega$. This periodicity is a consequence of sampling in time; the unit circle is traversed every $2\pi$ radians.

**Step 5 — Convergence condition.** DTFT exists if $\sum \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$ (sufficient) or under weaker square-summability conditions.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Twiddle factor.** $W_4 = e^{-j2\pi/4} = -j$.

**Step 2 — DFT matrix.**
$$\mathbf{W}_4 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -j & -1 & j \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & j & -1 & -j \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Input vector.**
$$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Matrix multiplication.**
$$X[0] = 1+1+1+1 = 4$$
$$X[1] = 1 + (-j)(1) + (-1)(1) + (j)(1) = 0$$
$$X[2] = 1 - 1 + 1 - 1 = 0$$
$$X[3] = 1 + j - 1 - j = 0$$

**Step 5 — Result.**
$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Comment:** All frequency components except the DC bin ($k=0$) are zero, which is expected for a constant (DC) input signal. The DFT correctly identifies that $x[n] = 1$ is a pure DC waveform of magnitude 4.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** State and prove the linearity property of the Z-Transform. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Express the 4-point DFT as a matrix equation. Verify the conjugate symmetry property for a real input sequence $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Statement:** If $x_1[n] \leftrightarrow X_1(z)$ with ROC $R_1$ and $x_2[n] \leftrightarrow X_2(z)$ with ROC $R_2$, then for any constants $a, b$,
$$a x_1[n] + b x_2[n] \;\leftrightarrow\; a X_1(z) + b X_2(z)$$
with ROC containing $R_1 \cap R_2$.

**Proof:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{Z}\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} &= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\}\, z^{-n} \\
&= a \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_1[n]\, z^{-n} + b \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_2[n]\, z^{-n} \\
&= a X_1(z) + b X_2(z)
\end{aligned}
$$

The interchange of summation and finite linear combination is valid inside the intersection of the two ROCs (where both sums converge absolutely).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — DFT matrix for $N=4$:**
$$\mathbf{W}_4 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & W_4 & W_4^2 & W_4^3 \\ 1 & W_4^2 & W_4^4 & W_4^6 \\ 1 & W_4^3 & W_4^6 & W_4^9 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -j & -1 & j \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & j & -1 & -j \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Matrix equation:** $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_4 \mathbf{x}$ with $\mathbf{x} = [1, 2, 3, 4]^T$.

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{X}$:**
$$X[0] = 1+2+3+4 = 10$$
$$X[1] = 1 + (-j)(2) + (-1)(3) + (j)(4) = -2 + 2j$$
$$X[2] = 1 - 2 + 3 - 4 = -2$$
$$X[3] = 1 + (j)(2) + (-1)(3) + (-j)(4) = -2 - 2j$$

**Step 4 — Verify conjugate symmetry.** For real $x[n]$, DFT satisfies $X[N-k] = X[k]^*$:
- $X[1] = -2 + 2j$ and $X[3] = -2 - 2j = (X[1])^*$. ✓
- $X[2] = -2$ is real, and $X[2] = X[2]^*$. ✓

The conjugate symmetry property is verified for this real sequence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ROC:** Many students write $X(z)$ without specifying the ROC. The ROC is **mandatory** for full marks — it disambiguates signals with identical algebraic $X(z)$.
> 2. **Sign error in twiddle factor:** The DFT uses $e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$ (with a **minus** sign). The IDFT uses $e^{+j2\pi kn/N}$ (with a **plus** sign). Confusing the two is the most common mistake.
> 3. **Off-by-one indexing:** DFT indices run from $0$ to $N-1$, not $1$ to $N$.
> 4. **Missing conjugate symmetry check:** When $x[n]$ is real, $X[N-k] = X[k]^*$. Failure to verify wastes easy marks.
> 5. **Confusing DTFT with DFT:** The DTFT is continuous and $2\pi$-periodic; the DFT is discrete and $N$-periodic. They are **not** the same.
> 6. **Wrong inverse matrix:** The IDFT matrix is $\frac{1}{N}\mathbf{W}_N^*$, not $\mathbf{W}_N^{-1}$ in some ad-hoc form. The $1/N$ factor is essential.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Z-Transform** $X(z) = \sum x[n]\, z^{-n}$ converts a discrete sequence to a complex-plane function; ROC is the convergence region, always excluding poles.
- **DTFT** is the Z-Transform evaluated on the unit circle $z = e^{j\omega}$; it is **continuous** in $\omega$ and **$2\pi$-periodic**.
- **DFT** is the DTFT sampled at $N$ uniformly spaced frequencies $\omega_k = 2\pi k/N$; it operates on finite-length $N$-point sequences.
- The **DFT is a linear transformation**: it can be written as $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{x}$, where $\mathbf{W}_N$ is the $N \times N$ DFT matrix with entries $W_N^{kn} = e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$.
- The DFT matrix $\mathbf{W}_N$ satisfies $\mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{W}_N^* = N \mathbf{I}$, making it **unitary up to scaling**.
- The IDFT matrix is $\mathbf{W}_N^{-1} = \frac{1}{N}\mathbf{W}_N^*$.
- **Twiddle factor**: $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$, satisfying $W_N^N = 1$ and $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^k$.
- **DTFT existence condition:** $\sum \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$ (absolute summability), or unit circle inside ROC.
- **DFT conjugate symmetry:** For real $x[n]$, $X[N-k] = X[k]^*$.
- **DFT periodicity:** $X[k+N] = X[k]$ (a direct consequence of time-domain sampling).
- **Stable & causal LTI system:** All poles inside the unit circle; ROC is $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert_{\max}$ and includes the unit circle.
- **Canonical Z-transforms to memorize:** $\delta[n] \leftrightarrow 1$; $u[n] \leftrightarrow \frac{z}{z-1}$; $a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \frac{z}{z-a}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IDFT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT504 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IDFT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (IDFT)** is the mathematical operation that recovers the time-domain discrete sequence $x(n)$ from its frequency-domain representation $X(k)$ (the Discrete Fourier Transform coefficients). It is the formal inverse operator of the DFT, satisfying a perfect reconstruction property when the sequence is finite and periodic.

For an N-point sequence, the IDFT is rigorously defined as:

$$
x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{\,j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1
$$

Equivalently, using the **twiddle factor** $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$, we can write:

$$
x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, W_N^{-kn}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The IDFT is the exact dual of the DFT. The DFT converts time → frequency, while the IDFT converts frequency → time. The normalizing factor $\frac{1}{N}$ is what fundamentally distinguishes the IDFT from the DFT equation (which has no such divisor).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Think of the **DFT as a prism** that takes a mixed musical chord and splits it into individual pure sine waves of different frequencies. The **IDFT is the reverse prism** — it takes those individual pure frequencies and combines them back into the original mixed chord.

> **Real-World Analogy — The Blender Analogy:**
> Imagine a fruit smoothie. The DFT is the act of **separating** the smoothie back into identifiable chunks of banana, mango, and ice (the frequency components). The IDFT is the act of **re-blending** those chunks back into the exact same smoothie. The blender (IDFT) needs the recipe coefficient $1/N$ to know how much to re-mix.

- The chord (signal) is **decomposed** by the DFT into a spectrum.
- The IDFT **synthesizes** the spectrum back into the original signal.
- Without the IDFT, we could analyze a signal but never reconstruct it — making it useless for any practical audio/image/communication application.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Physical Constants

- **N** — Length of the finite-duration sequence (number of samples). Standard values in DSP textbooks: $N = 4, 8, 16, \ldots, 1024$.
- **$W_N$** — Twiddle factor, defined as $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$, with the property $W_N^{N} = 1$.
- **Periodicity of IDFT:** The reconstructed sequence $x(n)$ is implicitly **periodic with period N**, even though we typically plot it only for $n = 0$ to $N-1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always remember that the IDFT output is *fundamentally periodic* with period N. When the question asks for $x(n)$ for $n \ge N$, you must wrap around using modulo-N arithmetic: $x(n) = x(n \mod N)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The IDFT computation proceeds in the following structured steps:

1. **Acquire frequency samples** $X(k)$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$.
2. **Form the complex exponentials** $e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$ (these are the basis vectors of the inverse transform — orthogonal unit vectors in the complex plane).
3. **Multiply** each $X(k)$ by its corresponding complex exponential $e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$.
4. **Accumulate (sum)** all the $N$ products for a fixed $n$.
5. **Divide** the accumulated sum by $N$ to obtain the original sample $x(n)$.
6. **Repeat** steps 2 to 5 for each desired $n$ in the range $0 \le n \le N-1$.

> **The "Why" Behind the $\frac{1}{N}$ Factor:**
> The DFT expands a signal as a linear combination of N orthogonal basis vectors. Because the basis vectors are *not* unit-normalized (they have magnitude $\sqrt{N}$), the forward transform accumulates the magnitudes. The inverse transform must therefore "undo" this amplification by dividing by $N$ to recover the original amplitude.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for IDFT-based questions in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| Formula / Property | Mathematical Expression | Condition / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| IDFT (basic) | $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$ | $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ |
| IDFT (twiddle form) | $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, W_N^{-kn}$ | $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$ |
| DFT (for reference) | $X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$ | $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ |
| Parseval's Theorem | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n) \vert^{2} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X(k) \vert^{2}$ | Energy conservation |
| Conjugate Property | $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \text{DFT} \{ X^{*}(k) \} \right]^{*}$ | Compute IDFT using DFT |
| Periodicity | $x(n + N) = x(n)$ | Implicit in DFT/IDFT pair |
| Linearity | $\text{IDFT}\{a X_1(k) + b X_2(k)\} = a x_1(n) + b x_2(n)$ | $a, b$ are complex constants |
| Time Reversal | $\text{IDFT}\{X((-k))_N\} = x((-n))_N$ | Modulo-N reversal |
| Circular Shift (Freq) | $\text{IDFT}\{X((k-l))_N\} = x(n) e^{j 2\pi l n / N}$ | Multiplication by complex exponential |
| Duality | $\text{DFT}\{x(n)\} = X(k) \implies \text{DFT}\{X(n)\} = N \, x((-k))_N$ | Crucial for IFFT using FFT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Pitfall:** When computing IDFT via the conjugate property, students often forget to take the **conjugate twice** (once on $X(k)$ before DFT, and once on the DFT output). The result is missing a complex conjugate, leading to wrong answers in numerical problems.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

The IDFT is the workhorse of nearly every modern signal processing pipeline:

- **OFDM Communication (4G/5G/Wi-Fi):** IDFT is used at the transmitter to modulate data onto orthogonal subcarriers. The receiver applies the DFT to demodulate.
- **Audio Coding (MP3, AAC):** The encoder uses a Modified DCT/DFT, and the decoder uses the corresponding inverse to reconstruct the audio waveform.
- **Image Compression (JPEG):** The 2D-IDFT reconstructs the spatial pixel image from the frequency-domain DCT coefficients.
- **Spectrum Analyzers:** After the front-end analog hardware captures a signal and the ADC digitizes it, the IDFT reconstructs the time-domain waveform from the analyzer's frequency bins.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI):** The reconstruction algorithm uses an inverse Fourier transform (often a 2D-IDFT) to convert raw k-space frequency data into viewable anatomical images.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the IDFT from the DFT Definition

We begin with the forward DFT definition:

$$
X(k) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) \, e^{-j 2\pi k m / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1
$$

**Step 1:** Multiply both sides by $e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$ (a complex exponential at frequency index $k$ corresponding to time index $n$):

$$
X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N} = \left[ \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) \, e^{-j 2\pi k m / N} \right] e^{j 2\pi k n / N}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum both sides over $k$ from $0$ to $N-1$:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) \, e^{j 2\pi k (n - m) / N}
$$

**Step 3:** Swap the order of summation (a valid operation because both sums are finite):

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N} = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k (n - m) / N} \right]
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the inner sum. This is a geometric series of the form $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} r^{k}$ with $r = e^{j 2\pi (n-m) / N}$.

Recall the geometric series result: $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} r^{k} = \frac{1 - r^{N}}{1 - r}$.

Since $r^{N} = e^{j 2\pi (n-m)} = 1$ (because $e^{j 2\pi M} = 1$ for any integer $M$), the numerator $1 - r^{N} = 0$.

If $n \neq m$ (mod $N$), then $r \neq 1$, so the denominator $1 - r \neq 0$, and the entire inner sum equals $0$.

If $n = m$, then $r = 1$, and the inner sum reduces to $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} 1 = N$.

**Step 5:** Therefore, the inner sum acts as a discrete Kronecker delta:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k (n - m) / N} = \begin{cases} N, & \text{if } n = m \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} = N \, \delta(n - m)
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute this back into the equation:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N} = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) \cdot N \, \delta(n - m) = N \, x(n)
$$

**Step 7:** Divide both sides by $N$ to obtain the IDFT:

$$
x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1
$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Conjugate Property

**Statement:** $\text{IDFT}\{X(k)\} = x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \text{DFT}\{X^{*}(k)\} \right]^{*}$

This is the property that allows IDFT to be computed using only DFT hardware/software.

**Step 1:** Start with the DFT of $X^{*}(k)$:

$$
\text{DFT}\{X^{*}(k)\} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X^{*}(k) \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply complex conjugation to the entire expression. Recall that $(AB)^{*} = A^{*} B^{*}$ and $(A + B)^{*} = A^{*} + B^{*}$:

$$
\left[ \text{DFT}\{X^{*}(k)\} \right]^{*} = \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X^{*}(k) \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N} \right]^{*}
$$

**Step 3:** Distribute the conjugate:

$$
\left[ \text{DFT}\{X^{*}(k)\} \right]^{*} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left[ X^{*}(k) \right]^{*} \, \left[ e^{-j 2\pi k n / N} \right]^{*}
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify using $(z^{*})^{*} = z$ and $(e^{j\theta})^{*} = e^{-j\theta}$:

$$
\left[ \text{DFT}\{X^{*}(k)\} \right]^{*} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}
$$

**Step 5:** Divide by $N$:

$$
\frac{1}{N} \left[ \text{DFT}\{X^{*}(k)\} \right]^{*} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N} = x(n)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \text{DFT}\{X^{*}(k)\} \right]^{*}}
$$

$\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step computational recipe for exam problems:**
> 1. Compute $X^{*}(k)$ — replace every $j$ with $-j$ in $X(k)$.
> 2. Take the DFT of $X^{*}(k)$ using the standard DFT formula (no conjugate, no $1/N$).
> 3. Conjugate the result of step 2.
> 4. Divide by $N$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: IDFT of a 4-Point Sequence

Let $X(k) = \{4, 0, 0, 0\}$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3$. Compute the IDFT $x(n)$.

**Setting up:** $N = 4$, so $W_4 = e^{-j 2\pi/4} = e^{-j \pi/2} = -j$.

**Step 1:** Compute $X^{*}(k)$:

$$
X^{*}(k) = \{4^{*}, 0^{*}, 0^{*}, 0^{*}\} = \{4, 0, 0, 0\}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the DFT of $X^{*}(k) = \{4, 0, 0, 0\}$.

Using the DFT formula $Y(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x(n) e^{-j 2\pi k n / 4}$:

$$
Y(k) = 4 \cdot e^{0} + 0 + 0 + 0 = 4
$$

So $Y(k) = \{4, 4, 4, 4\}$.

**Step 3:** Conjugate the result:

$$
Y^{*}(k) = \{4, 4, 4, 4\}
$$

**Step 4:** Divide by $N = 4$:

$$
x(n) = \frac{1}{4} \{4, 4, 4, 4\} = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}
$$

**Verification by direct IDFT formula:**

$$
x(0) = \frac{1}{4} \left[ 4 + 0 + 0 + 0 \right] = 1
$$

$$
x(1) = \frac{1}{4} \left[ 4 \cdot e^{0} + 0 \cdot e^{j \pi/2} + 0 \cdot e^{j \pi} + 0 \cdot e^{j 3\pi/2} \right] = \frac{4}{4} = 1
$$

By symmetry, $x(2) = x(3) = 1$. ✓

This matches the expected result: a constant DC sequence in time has all its energy concentrated at the $k=0$ frequency bin.

## 3.4 A Non-Trivial Numerical Example

Let $X(k) = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3$. Compute $x(n)$ using the conjugate property.

**Step 1:** $X^{*}(k) = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ (all values are real, so conjugation leaves them unchanged).

**Step 2:** Compute the DFT of $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$.

For $N = 4$, $e^{-j 2\pi/4} = e^{-j \pi/2} = -j$, so $e^{-j 2\pi k n/4} = (-j)^{kn}$.

The matrix form is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(k) &= \sum_{n=0}^{3} x(n) (-j)^{kn}
\end{aligned}
$$

Constructing the twiddle factor matrix $T$ where $T[k][n] = W_4^{kn} = (-j)^{kn}$:

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -j & -1 & j \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & j & -1 & -j \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiply $T$ with the column vector $[1, 2, 3, 4]^T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(0) &= (1)(1) + (1)(2) + (1)(3) + (1)(4) = 10 \\
Y(1) &= (1)(1) + (-j)(2) + (-1)(3) + (j)(4) = 1 - 2j - 3 + 4j = -2 + 2j \\
Y(2) &= (1)(1) + (-1)(2) + (1)(3) + (-1)(4) = 1 - 2 + 3 - 4 = -2 \\
Y(3) &= (1)(1) + (j)(2) + (-1)(3) + (-j)(4) = 1 + 2j - 3 - 4j = -2 - 2j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Conjugate $Y(k)$:

$$
Y^{*}(k) = \{10, -2 - 2j, -2, -2 + 2j\}
$$

**Step 4:** Divide by $N = 4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(0) &= \frac{10}{4} = 2.5 \\
x(1) &= \frac{-2 - 2j}{4} = -0.5 - 0.5j \\
x(2) &= \frac{-2}{4} = -0.5 \\
x(3) &= \frac{-2 + 2j}{4} = -0.5 + 0.5j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $x(n) = \{2.5, -0.5 - 0.5j, -0.5, -0.5 + 0.5j\}$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: DFT ↔ IDFT Transformation Pair

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the symmetric flow of the DFT-IDFT pair, including the conjugate property for indirect IDFT computation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InputA["x(n) Time Domain Signal"] --> DFTBlock["DFT Computation X(k) equals sum from n 0 to N-1 of x(n) e to power minus j 2 pi k n over N"]
    DFTBlock --> FreqDomain["X(k) Frequency Domain Spectrum k equals 0 to N-1"]
    FreqDomain --> IDFTBlock["IDFT Computation x(n) equals 1 over N times sum from k 0 to N-1 of X(k) e to power j 2 pi k n over N"]
    IDFTBlock --> OutputA["Reconstructed x(n)"]

    FreqDomain --> ConjugateOp["Conjugate Operation X star of k"]
    ConjugateOp --> DFTBlock2["Apply DFT Formula to X star of k"]
    DFTBlock2 --> ConjugateOp2["Conjugate the DFT Output"]
    ConjugateOp2 --> ScaleDiv["Divide by N"]
    ScaleDiv --> OutputB["Reconstructed x(n) via Conjugate Property"]

    DFTBlock -.->|Twiddle factor WN equals e to power minus j 2 pi over N| IDFTBlock
```

## 4.2 Algorithmic Flowchart: Computing IDFT via Conjugate Property

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Given Xk array of N samples]) --> Step1["Step 1: Compute X star of k by conjugating every element of Xk"]
    Step1 --> Step2["Step 2: Compute the DFT of X star of k using standard DFT formula Ym equals sum from k 0 to N-1 of X star of k times e to power minus j 2 pi k m over N"]
    Step2 --> Step3["Step 3: Take the complex conjugate of Ym to get Y star of m"]
    Step3 --> Step4["Step 4: Divide every element of Y star of m by N to obtain xn"]
    Step4 --> End([End: Output xn array length N])

    Step1 -.Check.-> Check1{All elements of Xk real?}
    Check1 -->|Yes| FastPath["Shortcut: No need to conjugate in Step 1"]
    Check1 -->|No| Step1
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Architecture: IDFT in a Typical OFDM Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BitStream["Input Bit Stream"] --> Mapper["Symbol Mapper QAM or PSK"]
    Mapper --> SerialToParallel["Serial to Parallel Converter"]
    SerialToParallel --> IDFTMod["N Point IDFT Modulator"]
    IDFTMod --> ParallelToSerial["Parallel to Serial Converter"]
    ParallelToSerial --> AddCP["Add Cyclic Prefix"]
    AddCP --> DAC["Digital to Analog Converter"]
    DAC --> RFChain["RF Front End Transmit"]

    IDFTMod -.Uses.-> Twiddle["Twiddle Factor Matrix WN to power minus kn"]
    IDFTMod -.Output.-> TimeSamples["Time Domain OFDM Samples xn"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each box represents a functional block; arrows show the data flow direction. The dashed arrows indicate auxiliary/parameter relationships rather than data flow. In the OFDM transmitter, the IDFT block is the heart of the modulator — it transforms parallel frequency-domain symbols (one per subcarrier) into a time-domain waveform that is then transmitted.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: IDFT Definition [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q:** Define the Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (IDFT) of a sequence $X(k)$ of length $N$. State the relationship between the DFT and IDFT in terms of twiddle factors.

**Model Answer:**

The IDFT of an N-point frequency-domain sequence $X(k)$ is defined as:

$$
x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1
$$

Using the twiddle factor $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$, this can be rewritten as:

$$
x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, W_N^{-kn}
$$

The relationship between DFT and IDFT is **dual** — the DFT transforms a time-domain signal to the frequency domain using the forward kernel $W_N^{kn} = e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$, while the IDFT transforms back using the reverse kernel $W_N^{-kn} = e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$ with a normalizing factor of $1/N$.

*(3 marks: 1 for IDFT formula, 1 for twiddle form, 1 for duality explanation)*

---

### Question 2: Conjugate Property [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q:** State and prove the conjugate property used to compute the IDFT using the DFT algorithm.

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** The IDFT of $X(k)$ can be computed using the DFT algorithm as:

$$
\text{IDFT}\{X(k)\} = x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \text{DFT}\{X^{*}(k)\} \right]^{*}
$$

**Proof Sketch:**

Apply the DFT formula to $X^{*}(k)$:

$$
\text{DFT}\{X^{*}(k)\} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X^{*}(k) \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}
$$

Conjugating both sides:

$$
\left[ \text{DFT}\{X^{*}(k)\} \right]^{*} = \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X^{*}(k) \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N} \right]^{*} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}
$$

Dividing by $N$:

$$
\frac{1}{N} \left[ \text{DFT}\{X^{*}(k)\} \right]^{*} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N} = x(n) = \text{IDFT}\{X(k)\}
$$

*(3 marks: 1 for statement, 2 for proof)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Comprehensive IDFT Problem [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q (a):** Derive the expression for the IDFT of an N-point sequence $X(k)$ starting from the DFT definition. Clearly state all steps and the role of the orthogonality principle.

**Model Answer:**

The IDFT derivation proceeds from the DFT definition:

$$
X(k) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) \, e^{-j 2\pi k m / N}
$$

**Step 1:** Multiply both sides by $e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$:

$$
X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N} = \left[ \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) \, e^{-j 2\pi k m / N} \right] e^{j 2\pi k n / N}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum both sides over $k$ from $0$ to $N-1$:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) \, e^{j 2\pi k (n - m) / N}
$$

**Step 3:** Swap the order of summation:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N} = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k (n - m) / N} \right]
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the orthogonality principle. The inner sum is a geometric series:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k (n - m) / N} = \begin{cases} N, & \text{if } n = m \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5:** The sum collapses to a single term (when $m = n$), giving $N \cdot x(n)$:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N} = N \cdot x(n)
$$

**Step 6:** Divide by $N$ to obtain the IDFT:

$$
x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}
$$

The orthogonality principle ensures that the cross-terms between different frequency components cancel out, leaving only the contribution at the matching time index $n = m$.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q (b):** Compute the 4-point IDFT of the sequence $X(k) = \{4, 1 + j, 0, 1 - j\}$ using the conjugate property. Show all matrix steps.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $N = 4$, $X(k) = \{4, 1 + j, 0, 1 - j\}$.

**Step 1:** Compute the conjugate of $X(k)$:

$$
X^{*}(k) = \{4, 1 - j, 0, 1 + j\}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the DFT of $X^{*}(k) = \{4, 1-j, 0, 1+j\}$ using $W_4 = -j$.

The twiddle factor matrix for N=4 is:

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -j & -1 & j \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & j & -1 & -j \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing each row by multiplying with $[4, 1-j, 0, 1+j]^T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(0) &= 1(4) + 1(1-j) + 1(0) + 1(1+j) = 4 + 1 - j + 0 + 1 + j = 6 \\
Y(1) &= 1(4) + (-j)(1-j) + (-1)(0) + (j)(1+j) \\
     &= 4 + (-j + j^{2}) + 0 + (j + j^{2}) \\
     &= 4 + (-j - 1) + (-1 + j) = 4 - j - 1 - 1 + j = 2 \\
Y(2) &= 1(4) + (-1)(1-j) + 1(0) + (-1)(1+j) = 4 - 1 + j + 0 - 1 - j = 2 \\
Y(3) &= 1(4) + (j)(1-j) + (-1)(0) + (-j)(1+j) \\
     &= 4 + (j - j^{2}) + 0 + (-j - j^{2}) \\
     &= 4 + (j + 1) + (-j + 1) = 4 + 1 + 1 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

So $Y(k) = \{6, 2, 2, 6\}$.

**Step 3:** Take the complex conjugate of $Y(k)$:

$$
Y^{*}(k) = \{6, 2, 2, 6\}
$$

(All values are real, so conjugation leaves them unchanged.)

**Step 4:** Divide by $N = 4$:

$$
x(n) = \frac{1}{4} \{6, 2, 2, 6\} = \{1.5, 0.5, 0.5, 1.5\}
$$

**Final Answer:** $x(n) = \{1.5, 0.5, 0.5, 1.5\}$

---

### Question B: Alternative Comprehensive IDFT Problem [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q (a):** Explain the relationship between DFT and IDFT with respect to the twiddle factor. Prove Parseval's theorem for the DFT-IDFT pair.

**Model Answer:**

**Twiddle Factor Relationship:**

Let $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$ be the N-point twiddle factor. The DFT uses $W_N^{kn}$ (positive exponent in the kernel of the forward transform, or equivalently $W_N^{+kn}$ if we write the IDFT kernel as $W_N^{-kn}$).

- **DFT:** $X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, W_N^{kn}$ with kernel exponent $+kn$
- **IDFT:** $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, W_N^{-kn}$ with kernel exponent $-kn$

The exponent sign reversal is the key mathematical duality.

**Parseval's Theorem Proof:**

**Statement:** $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n) \vert^{2} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X(k) \vert^{2}$

**Proof:**

Starting with the LHS, substitute $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, W_N^{-kn}$:

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n) \vert^{2} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, x^{*}(n) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, W_N^{-kn} \right] x^{*}(n)
$$

Swap the order of summation:

$$
= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^{*}(n) \, W_N^{-kn} \right]
$$

Note that $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^{*}(n) \, W_N^{-kn} = X^{*}(k)$ by the DFT definition (applying complex conjugation).

Therefore:

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n) \vert^{2} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, X^{*}(k) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X(k) \vert^{2}
$$

$\blacksquare$

Parseval's theorem states that the total energy in the time domain equals $1/N$ times the total energy in the frequency domain.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q (b):** An 8-point sequence has DFT coefficients $X(k) = \{8, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0\}$. Compute the IDFT manually and verify using the conjugate property. Comment on the physical interpretation.

**Model Answer:**

**Direct IDFT Computation:**

$$
x(n) = \frac{1}{8} \sum_{k=0}^{7} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / 8}
$$

Since $X(k) = 0$ for all $k \neq 0$ and $X(0) = 8$:

$$
x(n) = \frac{1}{8} \cdot 8 \cdot e^{0} = 1, \quad \text{for all } n
$$

So $x(n) = \{1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1\}$.

**Verification via Conjugate Property:**

**Step 1:** $X^{*}(k) = \{8, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0\}$

**Step 2:** DFT of $X^{*}(k)$:

For any $m$, $\text{DFT}\{X^{*}(k)\} = \sum_{k=0}^{7} X^{*}(k) \, e^{-j 2\pi k m / 8} = 8 \cdot e^{0} = 8$ (since only $k=0$ contributes).

So $Y(m) = 8$ for all $m$.

**Step 3:** $Y^{*}(m) = 8$ for all $m$ (real).

**Step 4:** Divide by 8: $x(n) = 1$ for all $n$. ✓

**Physical Interpretation:**

A constant DC signal (all samples equal to 1) in the time domain has all its spectral energy concentrated at the **zero-frequency (DC) bin** $k=0$ in the frequency domain. This is the most fundamental example of the DFT-IDFT duality: a pure DC signal is represented by a single non-zero frequency component.

This result is the discrete analog of the continuous-time Fourier transform pair: $x(t) = 1 \leftrightarrow X(f) = \delta(f)$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/N$ factor:** Many students write the IDFT as $\sum X(k) e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$ without dividing by $N$. This is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction in any IDFT question.
> 2. **Sign error in the twiddle factor exponent:** The IDFT uses $W_N^{-kn}$ (or equivalently $e^{+j 2\pi k n / N}$), **not** $W_N^{+kn}$. This is a very common error.
> 3. **Missing conjugation in the conjugate property:** When using the conjugate property, you must conjugate $X(k)$ **before** taking the DFT, and then conjugate the DFT output **after** — but only **divide by $N$ once**, at the end.
> 4. **Confusing DFT and IDFT outputs in numerical problems:** Always re-check by taking the DFT of your computed $x(n)$ to verify that you recover the original $X(k)$.
> 5. **Index range errors:** The IDFT is defined for $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$. Writing $n = 0$ to $N$ (one extra sample) loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Misinterpreting Parseval's theorem:** The factor $1/N$ belongs on the frequency-domain side, not the time-domain side. Writing $\sum \vert x(n) \vert^2 = \sum \vert X(k) \vert^2$ (without $1/N$) is wrong and is a frequent trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following is a high-density rapid-revision checklist for IDFT — review these points before every KTU exam.

- **Core IDFT Formula:** $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$ — must include the $1/N$ factor and the **positive** exponent in the complex exponential.
- **Twiddle Factor Convention:** $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$. DFT uses $W_N^{+kn}$; IDFT uses $W_N^{-kn}$.
- **Conjugate Property (most exam-relevant identity):** $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \text{DFT}\{X^{*}(k)\} \right]^{*}$ — allows IDFT to be computed using existing DFT hardware.
- **Duality Property:** $\text{DFT}\{X(n)\} = N \, x((-k))_N$ — useful for swapping domain roles.
- **Parseval's Theorem:** $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X(k) \vert^2$ — energy conservation between time and frequency domains.
- **Periodicity:** The IDFT output $x(n)$ is periodic with period $N$; for indices beyond $N-1$, apply modulo-N arithmetic.
- **Linearity:** IDFT is a linear operator — the IDFT of a weighted sum is the weighted sum of the IDFTs.
- **Circular Convolution Theorem:** $\text{IDFT}\{X_1(k) \cdot X_2(k)\} = x_1(n) \text{ (N)} x_2(n)$ — multiplication in frequency corresponds to circular convolution in time.
- **Numerical Recipe (for exam problems):**
  1. Identify $N$ and $X(k)$.
  2. Form the twiddle factor matrix $T[k][n] = W_N^{kn}$ or $W_N^{-kn}$ depending on direction.
  3. Multiply and sum according to the formula.
  4. Always verify by applying the forward DFT on the result.
- **Engineering Applications:** OFDM transmitters (4G/5G/Wi-Fi), JPEG image compression, MP3 audio decoding, MRI image reconstruction, spectrum analyzer waveform display.
- **Common Mistake Pattern:** A "missing" $1/N$ or a "missing" conjugation is the single most frequent error in KTU answer sheets. Always re-derive mentally before writing the final expression.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of DFT and Examples. Circular Convolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT504 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of DFT and Examples. Circular Convolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) — Formal Definition

For a finite-length sequence $x(n)$ of length $N$, the **N-point Discrete Fourier Transform** is defined as:

$$X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, W_N^{kn}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

and the **Inverse DFT (IDFT)** is:

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, W_N^{-kn}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

where the **twiddle factor** is $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$, so $W_N^{kn} = e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The DFT acts as a "frequency prism" for discrete-time signals. Just as a glass prism splits white light into its constituent colors (frequencies), the DFT splits a finite sequence into its N frequency components $X(0), X(1), \ldots, X(N-1)$. These samples are themselves periodic with period $N$ — i.e., $X(k+N) = X(k)$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sealed Ring"

Imagine your finite sequence $x(n)$ as a necklace of **N beads** (samples). In *linear* operations, the necklace has two free ends; in *circular* operations (DFT world), the two ends are **sealed together into a ring**.

- **Linear shift** = slide the beads along a straight ruler (zero-padding outside).
- **Circular shift** = rotate the beads around the sealed ring (what falls off one end reappears at the other end).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study properties of DFT?**
> - They are the algebraic **shortcuts** used in every KTU numerical problem.
> - They form the foundation of the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** algorithm (Module 2/3).
> - The **circular convolution** property is the bridge that lets us compute linear convolution using DFTs — the very heart of fast digital filtering (overlap-add, overlap-save).

## 1.3 The "Big Picture" List of DFT Properties

The properties we will study in this section are:

1. Periodicity
2. Linearity
3. Circular Time Shifting
4. Circular Frequency Shifting (Modulation)
5. Time Reversal
6. Circular Convolution
7. Circular Correlation
8. Multiplication in Time ↔ Circular Convolution in Frequency
9. Parseval's Theorem
10. Symmetry (Conjugate) properties for real sequences
11. Duality
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Framework — Why These Properties Hold

The DFT pair shares an elegant *symmetry*: the inverse formula is identical to the forward formula except for (a) the sign of the exponent and (b) a factor of $1/N$. This near-symmetry is precisely why every property in the time domain has a "twin" in the frequency domain (time-shift ↔ frequency-shift, convolution ↔ multiplication, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal recipe used to prove every property:**
> 1. Start from the definition $X(k) = \sum x(n) W_N^{kn}$.
> 2. Substitute the modified sequence (shifted, reversed, etc.).
> 3. Change the index of summation if needed.
> 4. Pull constant multipliers out of the sum.
> 5. Arrive at a factor times the original $X(k)$ (or a related spectrum).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — All Properties at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> Notation: $x(n) \xleftrightarrow{DFT} X(k)$. All shifts are *circular* modulo $N$. Operations use $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$.

| # | Property | Time Domain | Frequency Domain |
|---|----------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Periodicity** | $x(n+N) = x(n)$ | $X(k+N) = X(k)$ |
| 2 | **Linearity** | $a\,x_1(n) + b\,x_2(n)$ | $a\,X_1(k) + b\,X_2(k)$ |
| 3 | **Circular Time Shift** | $x((n-m))_N$ | $X(k)\,W_N^{km}$ |
| 4 | **Circular Freq. Shift** | $x(n)\,W_N^{-n\ell}$ | $X((k-\ell))_N$ |
| 5 | **Time Reversal** | $x((-n))_N$ | $X((-k))_N$ |
| 6 | **Circular Convolution** | $x_1(n) \circledast x_2(n)$ | $X_1(k) \cdot X_2(k)$ |
| 7 | **Circular Correlation** | $x_1(n) \circledast x_2^*((-n))_N$ | $X_1(k) \cdot X_2^*(k)$ |
| 8 | **Multiplication ↔ Cir. Conv.** | $x_1(n) \cdot x_2(n)$ | $\dfrac{1}{N}\,X_1(k) \circledast X_2(k)$ |
| 9 | **Parseval's Theorem** | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n)\vert^2$ | $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X(k)\vert^2$ |
| 10 | **Conjugate Symmetry (real $x$)** | $x(n)$ real | $X(k) = X^*((N-k))_N$ |
| 11 | **Duality** | $X(n)$ | $N \cdot x((-k))_N$ |

Where $((n-m))_N$ means $(n-m)$ taken modulo $N$, and $x_1(n) \circledast x_2(n)$ denotes **N-point circular convolution**.

## 2.3 Circular Convolution — The Star Property

### 2.3.1 Formal Definition

The **N-point circular convolution** of two length-$N$ sequences $x_1(n)$ and $x_2(n)$ is:

$$y(m) = x_1(m) \circledast x_2(m) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_1(n) \cdot x_2((m-n))_N, \quad m = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

Equivalently: $\quad y(m) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_2(n) \cdot x_1((m-n))_N$

### 2.3.2 Why It Matters in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fast Filtering Pipeline (used in every cell-phone baseband, audio equalizer, radar DSP):**
> Step 1: Compute $X_1(k) = \text{DFT}\{x_1(n)\}$ via FFT.
> Step 2: Compute $X_2(k) = \text{DFT}\{x_2(n)\}$ via FFT.
> Step 3: Point-wise multiply: $Y(k) = X_1(k) \cdot X_2(k)$.
> Step 4: Inverse FFT: $y(m) = \text{IDFT}\{Y(k)\}$.
> This converts a slow $O(N^2)$ convolution into a fast $O(N\log_2 N)$ operation.

### 2.3.3 Linear vs Circular Convolution — The Critical Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> Circular convolution is **NOT** the same as linear convolution unless the sequences are zero-padded to length $\geq L_1 + L_2 - 1$.
> **Time-aliasing** is what causes the difference: in circular convolution, the "tail" of the linear convolution that spills past index $N-1$ wraps around and adds to the early samples.

If $x_1$ has length $L_1$ and $x_2$ has length $L_2$, then:
- **Linear convolution** has length $L_1 + L_2 - 1$.
- **Circular convolution** has length $N = \max(L_1, L_2)$.
- If $N \geq L_1 + L_2 - 1$, the two are **identical**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of DFT properties |
|--------|-------------------------------|
| **Audio processing** | MP3/AAC codecs use MDCT (a DFT variant) with overlap-add |
| **Telecommunications** | OFDM in 4G/5G/Wi-Fi uses circular convolution via CP-prefixed blocks |
| **Biomedical** | ECG/EEG spectral analysis uses Parseval's theorem for total power |
| **Radar/Sonar** | Pulse compression uses matched filtering = correlation in time ↔ multiplication in frequency |
| **Image processing** | 2-D DFT of images, JPEG compression, de-blurring via Wiener filter |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Conventions used:** $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$; "mod $N$" is written as $((\cdot))_N$; "$\Sigma$" means $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}$. The DFT pair is $x(n) \xleftrightarrow{DFT} X(k)$.

## 3.1 Property 1 — Periodicity

**Statement:** If $x(n) \xleftrightarrow{DFT} X(k)$ over $N$ points, then $X(k+N) = X(k)$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(k+N) &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, W_N^{(k+N)n} \\
       &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, W_N^{kn} \cdot W_N^{Nn} \\
       &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, W_N^{kn} \cdot (e^{-j2\pi})^{n} \\
       &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, W_N^{kn} \cdot 1^{n} \\
       &= X(k)
\end{aligned}
$$

(Step 2 splits the exponent; Step 3 substitutes $W_N^N = e^{-j2\pi} = 1$.)

## 3.2 Property 2 — Linearity

**Statement:** $a\,x_1(n) + b\,x_2(n) \xleftrightarrow{DFT} a\,X_1(k) + b\,X_2(k)$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DFT}\{a\,x_1(n) + b\,x_2(n)\} &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \bigl[a\,x_1(n) + b\,x_2(n)\bigr] W_N^{kn} \\
&= a\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_1(n) W_N^{kn} + b\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_2(n) W_N^{kn} \\
&= a\,X_1(k) + b\,X_2(k)
\end{aligned}
$$

(Step 2 distributes the sum; Step 3 uses the linearity of summation.)

## 3.3 Property 3 — Circular Time Shift

**Statement:** $x((n-m))_N \xleftrightarrow{DFT} X(k) \cdot W_N^{km}$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DFT}\{x((n-m))_N\} &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x((n-m))_N \, W_N^{kn}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $r = (n-m)_N$, so $n = (r+m)_N$. When $n$ runs over $0, 1, \ldots, N-1$, $r$ also runs over $0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ (it is just a permutation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{r=0}^{N-1} x(r) \, W_N^{k(r+m)} \\
&= W_N^{km} \sum_{r=0}^{N-1} x(r) W_N^{kr} \\
&= W_N^{km} \cdot X(k)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Property 5 — Time Reversal

**Statement:** $x((-n))_N \xleftrightarrow{DFT} X((-k))_N$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DFT}\{x((-n))_N\} &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x((-n))_N \, W_N^{kn}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $r = (-n)_N$, so $n = (-r)_N$. As $n$ runs over $0, 1, \ldots, N-1$, $r$ also runs over $0, 1, \ldots, N-1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{r=0}^{N-1} x(r) \, W_N^{k(-r)} \\
&= \sum_{r=0}^{N-1} x(r) \, W_N^{(-k)r} \\
&= X((-k))_N
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Property 6 — Circular Convolution (The Star Property) ⭐

**Statement:** $x_1(n) \circledast x_2(n) \xleftrightarrow{DFT} X_1(k) \cdot X_2(k)$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DFT}\{x_1(n) \circledast x_2(n)\} &= \sum_{m=0}^{N-1}\left[\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_1(n)\,x_2((m-n))_N\right] W_N^{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

Swap the order of summation (allowed because both sums are finite):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_1(n) \left[\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_2((m-n))_N \, W_N^{km}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Now look at the inner sum. Let $r = (m-n)_N$, so $m = (r+n)_N$, and as $m$ runs from $0$ to $N-1$, $r$ also runs from $0$ to $N-1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inner sum} &= \sum_{r=0}^{N-1} x_2(r) \, W_N^{k(r+n)} \\
                 &= W_N^{kn} \sum_{r=0}^{N-1} x_2(r) W_N^{kr} = W_N^{kn} \cdot X_2(k)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DFT}\{x_1 \circledast x_2\} &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_1(n) \, W_N^{kn} \cdot X_2(k) \\
&= X_2(k) \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_1(n) W_N^{kn} \\
&= X_1(k) \cdot X_2(k) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Property 9 — Parseval's Theorem (Energy Conservation)

**Statement:** $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n)\vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X(k)\vert^2$.

**Derivation Outline:** Start from the RHS, substitute the IDFT $x(n) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k} X(k) W_N^{-kn}$ into the LHS, and simplify using the orthogonality relation $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} W_N^{n(k-\ell)} = N\,\delta(k-\ell)$. This is the key identity:

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} W_N^{n(k-\ell)} = 
\begin{cases} N, & k = \ell \pmod{N} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Examples — Circular Convolution

### Example A — Simple 4-Point Circular Convolution

> **Problem:** Compute the 4-point circular convolution $y(n) = x_1(n) \circledast x_2(n)$ for
> $x_1(n) = \{1,\,2,\,3,\,4\}$, $\;x_2(n) = \{1,\,1,\,1,\,1\}$.

**Method 1 — Direct Formula:**

Formula: $\displaystyle y(m) = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x_1(n) \cdot x_2((m-n)\bmod 4)$

- **m = 0:** $y(0) = x_1(0)x_2(0) + x_1(1)x_2(-1) + x_1(2)x_2(-2) + x_1(3)x_2(-3)$
   $= 1\cdot 1 + 2\cdot x_2(3) + 3\cdot x_2(2) + 4\cdot x_2(1)$
   $= 1 + 2(1) + 3(1) + 4(1) = 10$
- **m = 1:** $y(1) = x_1(0)x_2(1) + x_1(1)x_2(0) + x_1(2)x_2(-1) + x_1(3)x_2(-2)$
   $= 1\cdot 1 + 2\cdot 1 + 3\cdot x_2(3) + 4\cdot x_2(2)$
   $= 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10$
- **m = 2:** $y(2) = x_1(0)x_2(2) + x_1(1)x_2(1) + x_1(2)x_2(0) + x_1(3)x_2(-1)$
   $= 1 + 2 + 3 + 4\cdot x_2(3) = 1+2+3+4 = 10$
- **m = 3:** $y(3) = x_1(0)x_2(3) + x_1(1)x_2(2) + x_1(2)x_2(1) + x_1(3)x_2(0)$
   $= 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10$

**Answer:** $y(n) = \{10,\,10,\,10,\,10\}$

> [!NOTE]
> *Verification via DFT property:* All-ones sequence has DFT $\{4, 0, 0, 0\}$. So $Y(k) = X_1(k) \cdot 4$ at $k=0$ and $Y(k) = 0$ for $k=1,2,3$. Then $y(n) = \text{IDFT}\{Y(k)\} = \{4\cdot 4/4, 0, 0, 0\}$... wait, let me redo: $Y(0) = 10\cdot 4 = 40$, and $Y(k) = 0$ for $k=1,2,3$. So $y(n) = \{40/4, 0, 0, 0\} = \{10, 0, 0, 0\}$ — this is *not* matching! The error is in the DFT of $x_2 = \{1,1,1,1\}$: $X_2(0) = 4$, $X_2(k) = 0$ for $k=1,2,3$. So $Y(0) = X_1(0)\cdot X_2(0) = (1+2+3+4)\cdot 4 = 10\cdot 4 = 40$. Then $y(n) = \{40/4,\,0,\,0,\,0\} = \{10,0,0,0\}$.
>
> **Reconciliation:** The direct-formula method above used the same calculation but I made an arithmetic slip in the DFT cross-check. The *direct formula* gives $y(n) = \{10,10,10,10\}$, but the DFT cross-check gives $\{10,0,0,0\}$. This is a **classic student pitfall** — see the warning in §5. (The truth: $y(0) = 1\cdot 1 + 2\cdot 1 + 3\cdot 1 + 4\cdot 1 = 10$; for $m=1$ we need $x_2(0-1\bmod 4) = x_2(3) = 1$, so $y(1) = 1\cdot 1 + 2\cdot 1 + 3\cdot 1 + 4\cdot 1 = 10$. So the direct answer is indeed $\{10,10,10,10\}$. The DFT cross-check should be $Y(k) = X_1(k) X_2(k) = 0$ for $k=1,2,3$, meaning $y(n)$ should be a constant. Indeed the IDFT of $\{40,0,0,0\}$ is $\{10,10,10,10\}$. ✓ The constant $40/N$? No — IDFT formula is $\frac{1}{N}\sum$, so $y(n) = \frac{1}{4}\cdot 40 \cdot e^{0} + 0 = 10$. Yes, correct.)

### Example B — Concentric-Circle Method (4-Point)

For $x_1(n) = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$ and $x_2(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$:

| m | Computation | $y(m)$ |
|---|-------------|--------|
| 0 | $1\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 4 + 1\cdot 3 + 1\cdot 2$ | $10$ |
| 1 | $1\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 4 + 1\cdot 3$ | $10$ |
| 2 | $1\cdot 3 + 1\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 4$ | $10$ |
| 3 | $1\cdot 4 + 1\cdot 3 + 1\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 1$ | $10$ |

**Answer:** $y(n) = \{10,\,10,\,10,\,10\}$.

### Example C — Linear vs Circular Convolution (Crucial Comparison)

> **Problem:** $x_1(n) = \{1,\,2,\,3\}$ ($L_1=3$), $x_2(n) = \{1,\,1,\,1\}$ ($L_2=3$). Compute both 3-point circular and the linear convolution.

**Linear Convolution (length = 5):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_L(0) &= 1\cdot 1 = 1 \\
y_L(1) &= 1\cdot 1 + 2\cdot 1 = 3 \\
y_L(2) &= 1\cdot 1 + 2\cdot 1 + 3\cdot 1 = 6 \\
y_L(3) &= 2\cdot 1 + 3\cdot 1 = 5 \\
y_L(4) &= 3\cdot 1 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Linear result:** $y_L(n) = \{1,\,3,\,6,\,5,\,3\}$ (length 5).

**3-Point Circular Convolution:** $y_C(m) = \sum_{n=0}^{2} x_1(n) \cdot x_2((m-n)\bmod 3)$.

The linear convolution tail wraps around:
- $y_C(0) = y_L(0) + y_L(3) = 1 + 5 = 6$
- $y_C(1) = y_L(1) + y_L(4) = 3 + 3 = 6$
- $y_C(2) = y_L(2) = 6$ (nothing wraps onto index 2)

**Circular result:** $y_C(n) = \{6,\,6,\,6\}$ (length 3).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule:** To make circular convolution equal to linear convolution, **zero-pad both sequences to length $N \geq L_1 + L_2 - 1$** before computing. For our example, pad to $N=5$:
> $x_1 = \{1,2,3,0,0\}$, $x_2 = \{1,1,1,0,0\}$.
> Then the 5-point circular convolution **equals** the 5-point linear convolution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — DFT Property Application Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input sequence x1 of length N"] --> B["Compute X1 of k via DFT"]
    C["Input sequence x2 of length N"] --> D["Compute X2 of k via DFT"]
    B --> E["Pointwise multiplication Y of k = X1 of k times X2 of k"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Apply IDFT to Y of k"]
    F --> G["Output circular convolution y of m"]
    H["DFT property circular convolution theorem"] --> E
    I["Twiddle factor W_N to the kn"] --> B
    I --> D
```

## 4.2 Concentric-Circle Method for Circular Convolution

```mermaid

    subgraph Outer["Outer ring x2 of n = 1, 2, 3, 4"]
        O1["Index 0: 1"]
        O2["Index 1: 2"]
        O3["Index 2: 3"]
        O4["Index 3: 4"]
    end
    subgraph Inner["Inner ring x1 of n = 1, 1, 1, 1"]
        I1["Index 0: 1"]
        I2["Index 1: 1"]
        I3["Index 2: 1"]
        I4["Index 3: 1"]
    end
    O1 --> R1["Rotate inner ring by m=0, dot product, y of 0 = 10"]
    I1 --> R1
    O2 --> R2["Rotate inner ring by m=1, dot product, y of 1 = 10"]
    I2 --> R2
    O3 --> R3["Rotate inner ring by m=2, dot product, y of 2 = 10"]
    I3 --> R3
    O4 --> R4["Rotate inner ring by m=3, dot product, y of 3 = 10"]
    I4 --> R4
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        T1["Circular Time Shift x of n minus m"]
        T2["Circular Convolution x1 N x2"]
        T3["Multiplication x1 of n times x2 of n"]
        T4["Time Reversal x of minus n"]
    end
    subgraph Freq["Frequency Domain Result"]
        F1["Multiply by W_N to the km"]

        F4["Spectral Reversal X of minus k"]
    end
    T1 <-->|DFT| F1
    T2 <-->|DFT| F2

```

    C["x2 of n time domain"] --> D["FFT of x2 of n equals X2 of k"]
    B --> E["Element wise multiply Y of k = X1 of k dot X2 of k"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["IFFT of Y of k equals y of n"]
