## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Overlap–Save Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input x(n) Long Sequence"] --> B["Segment Splitter with M-1 Sample Overlap"]
    B --> C["N-point FFT of x_i(n)"]
    D["FIR Filter h(n) Length M"] --> E["Zero-pad to N = L + M - 1"]
    E --> F["Pre-computed N-point FFT H(k)"]
    C --> G["Complex Multiplier X_i(k) x H(k)"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["N-point IFFT yields y_i(n)"]
    H --> I{"Discard first M-1 samples of y_i(n)"}
    I --> J["Save remaining L samples to Output Buffer y(n)"]
    J --> K{"More input samples remain?"}
    K -- Yes --> B
    K -- No --> L["Final Output y(n) Linear Convolution Result"]
    I -- Discarded samples contain wrap-around aliasing --> M["Sample-Discarded Region"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Overlap–Add Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input x(n) Long Sequence"] --> B["Segment Splitter Non-overlapping blocks of length L"]
    B --> C["Append M-1 zeros to each block zero-padding to N = L + M - 1"]
    C --> D["N-point FFT of x_i(n)"]
    E["FIR Filter h(n) Length M"] --> F["Zero-pad to N = L + M - 1"]
    F --> G["Pre-computed N-point FFT H(k)"]
    D --> H["Complex Multiplier X_i(k) x H(k)"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["N-point IFFT yields y_i(n) Length N"]
    I --> J{"Is this the last block?"}
    J -- No --> K["Overlap last M-1 samples with next block and ADD algebraically"]
    K --> L["Append first L samples of y_i(n) to Output"]
    L --> B
    J -- Yes --> M["Append all N samples of y_i(n) to Output"]
    M --> N["Final Output y(n) Linear Convolution Result"]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram — Frequency Analysis via DFT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Time-Domain Signal x(n) Length N"] --> B{"Apply Windowing Function to reduce leakage"}
    B -- Optional but recommended --> C["Windowed Signal w(n) x x(n)"]
    B -- Skip for raw DFT --> D["Zero-pad if higher spectral density required"]
    D --> E["Compute N-point DFT X(k)"]
    C --> E
    E --> F["Magnitude Spectrum |X(k)|"]
    E --> G["Phase Spectrum angle X(k)"]
    E --> H["Power Spectrum |X(k)| squared"]
    F --> I["Identify Dominant Frequencies"]
    G --> J["Determine Phase Alignment"]
    H --> K["Compute Signal Energy Parseval Theorem"]
    I --> L["Frequency-Domain Interpretation"]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

### 4.4 Comparative Decision Flow — When to Use OLS vs OLA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Choose Block Convolution Method"] --> B{"Is the input data streaming or fixed-length?"}
    B -- Streaming / Real-time --> C["Use Overlap-Save Method"]
    B -- Fixed-length / Offline --> D["Use Overlap-Add Method"]
    C --> E["Overlap by M-1 samples and discard corrupted edges"]
    D --> F["Zero-pad blocks and add overlapping tails"]
    E --> G["Both yield identical linear convolution results"]
    F --> G
```

### 4.5 Sample Data-Flow Illustration — OLS Discard Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Block0["Block 0 input x_0"]
        A0["x(0)"] --- A1["x(1)"] --- A2["x(2)"] --- A3["x(3)"] --- A4["0"] --- A5["0"]
    end
    subgraph Block0Out["Block 0 output y_0"]
        B0["DISCARD"] --- B1["DISCARD"] --- B2["VALID y(0)"] --- B3["VALID y(1)"] --- B4["VALID y(2)"] --- B5["VALID y(3)"]
    end
    subgraph Block1["Block 1 input x_1 overlapped by 2"]
        C0["x(2)"] --- C1["x(3)"] --- C2["x(4)"] --- C3["x(5)"] --- C4["x(6)"] --- C5["x(7)"]
    end
    subgraph Block1Out["Block 1 output y_1"]
        D0["DISCARD"] --- D1["DISCARD"] --- D2["VALID y(4)"] --- D3["VALID y(5)"] --- D4["VALID y(6)"] --- D5["VALID y(7)"]
    end
    Block0 --> Block0Out
    Block1 --> Block1Out
    B2 --> Y["Final Output y(n) y(0) y(1) y(2) y(3) y(4) y(5) y(6) y(7) ..."]
    B3 --> Y
    B4 --> Y
    B5 --> Y
    D2 --> Y
    D3 --> Y
    D4 --> Y
    D5 --> Y
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q1. What is the Overlap–Save method? Why do we discard the first $M-1$ samples of each output block?**

**Model Answer:**

The Overlap–Save (OLS) method is a block convolution technique used to perform the **linear convolution** of a long sequence $x(n)$ with a short FIR filter $h(n)$ of length $M$ using the **DFT/FFT**. The long input is segmented into blocks of length $N = L+M-1$, where each block overlaps the previous one by $M-1$ samples. Each block is processed via $N$-point DFT, multiplied by the pre-computed filter DFT $H(k)$, and transformed back via IDFT.

We **discard the first $M-1$ samples** of each output block because the $N$-point DFT implements **circular convolution**, not linear convolution. The first $M-1$ samples of the circular convolution result are **contaminated by time-domain aliasing** (wrap-around of the filter tail). The remaining $L = N - (M-1)$ samples are free from aliasing and match the true linear convolution output for that block.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition 1.5 marks + Justification 1.5 marks.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q2. Compare Overlap–Save and Overlap–Add methods of block convolution in terms of overlap structure and post-processing of output blocks.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Overlap–Save | Overlap–Add |
|---------|--------------|-------------|
| Input block overlap | Yes, blocks overlap by $M-1$ samples | No overlap, non-overlapping blocks of length $L$ |
| Input block preparation | Taken as-is from $x(n)$ with overlap | Each block zero-padded with $M-1$ trailing zeros |
| Post-processing | **Discard** first $M-1$ samples of each output block | **Add** the last $M-1$ samples of one block to the first $M-1$ samples of the next |
| Output per block | $L$ valid samples | $L$ valid samples (after overlap-add) |
| Best use | Streaming / real-time data | Offline / fixed-length data |

> **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** 4–5 comparative points × appropriate weightage.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Overlap–Add method** for the linear convolution of a long sequence $x(n)$ with an FIR filter of length $M$. Clearly state the role of zero-padding and the overlap-and-add operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given $x(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$ and $h(n) = \{1, 1, 1\}$ (filter length $M = 3$), perform block convolution using the **Overlap–Add method** with $L = 4$ and $N = 6$. Show the block segmentation, DFT-domain multiplication, and the final overlap-and-add summation. Verify the result against direct linear convolution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Setting up the problem:**
Linear convolution $y(n) = x(n) * h(n)$ with $M = 3$ requires $N = L + M - 1$ for each block to avoid wrap-around.

**Step 2 — Segmentation of $x(n)$:**
The long input $x(n)$ is divided into non-overlapping blocks of length $L$. Here $L = 4$, so we get 2 blocks:

$$
x_0(n) = \{x(0), x(1), x(2), x(3)\} = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}
$$
$$
x_1(n) = \{x(4), x(5), x(6), x(7)\} = \{5, 6, 7, 8\}
$$

**Step 3 — Zero-padding:**
Each block is appended with $M-1 = 2$ zeros to make length $N = 6$:

$$
x_0(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 0, 0\}, \quad x_1(n) = \{5, 6, 7, 8, 0, 0\}
$$

The filter $h(n) = \{1, 1, 1\}$ is also zero-padded to $\{1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0\}$.

**Step 4 — DFT-domain processing:**
Compute 6-point DFTs $X_0(k)$, $X_1(k)$, and $H(k)$, then form $Y_i(k) = X_i(k) H(k)$ and IDFT to get $y_0(n)$ and $y_1(n)$.

**Step 5 — Overlap-and-Add:**
Each $y_i(n)$ has length $N = 6$. The **last $M-1 = 2$ samples** of $y_0(n)$ are added to the **first 2 samples** of $y_1(n)$:

$$
y(n) = \{y_0(0), y_0(1), y_0(2), y_0(3), y_0(4) + y_1(0), y_0(5) + y_1(1), y_1(2), y_1(3), y_1(4), y_1(5)\}
$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Direct linear convolution (for verification):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(n) &= x(n) * h(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\} * \{1, 1, 1\} \\
y(0) &= 1 \cdot 1 = 1 \\
y(1) &= 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 = 3 \\
y(2) &= 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 = 6 \\
y(3) &= 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 + 4 \cdot 1 = 9 \\
y(4) &= 3 \cdot 1 + 4 \cdot 1 + 5 \cdot 1 = 12 \\
y(5) &= 4 \cdot 1 + 5 \cdot 1 + 6 \cdot 1 = 15 \\
y(6) &= 5 \cdot 1 + 6 \cdot 1 + 7 \cdot 1 = 18 \\
y(7) &= 6 \cdot 1 + 7 \cdot 1 + 8 \cdot 1 = 21 \\
y(8) &= 7 \cdot 1 + 8 \cdot 1 = 15 \\
y(9) &= 8 \cdot 1 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

So $y(n) = \{1, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, 15, 8\}$.

**Step 2 — OLA block processing (N = 6):**

$$
x_0(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 0, 0\}, \quad h(n) = \{1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0\}
$$

Direct 6-point circular convolution of $x_0$ and $h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_0(0) &= 1 \cdot 1 = 1 \\
y_0(1) &= 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 = 3 \\
y_0(2) &= 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 = 6 \\
y_0(3) &= 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 + 4 \cdot 1 = 9 \\
y_0(4) &= 3 \cdot 1 + 4 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 1 = 7 \quad \text{(no wrap since last 2 of } x_0 \text{ are zero)} \\
y_0(5) &= 4 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 1 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

So $y_0(n) = \{1, 3, 6, 9, 7, 4\}$.

For $x_1(n) = \{5, 6, 7, 8, 0, 0\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1(0) &= 5 \\
y_1(1) &= 5 + 6 = 11 \\
y_1(2) &= 5 + 6 + 7 = 18 \\
y_1(3) &= 6 + 7 + 8 = 21 \\
y_1(4) &= 7 + 8 + 0 = 15 \\
y_1(5) &= 8 + 0 + 0 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

So $y_1(n) = \{5, 11, 18, 21, 15, 8\}$.

**Step 3 — Overlap-and-Add:**
The last 2 samples of $y_0$ are $\{7, 4\}$. The first 2 samples of $y_1$ are $\{5, 11\}$. Sum: $\{7+5, 4+11\} = \{12, 15\}$.

**Final concatenated output:**

$$
y(n) = \{1, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, 15, 8\}
$$

**Verification:** Matches direct linear convolution. $\checkmark$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the **Overlap–Save method** with a neat block diagram. Clearly explain why $N$-point DFTs are used and what happens to the corrupted samples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the input $x(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$ and the FIR filter $h(n) = \{1, 1, 1\}$ ($M = 3$). Using the **Overlap–Save method** with $L = 4$ and $N = 6$, compute the linear convolution. Show how the first $M-1$ samples of each block are discarded. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Pre-computation:** $h(n)$ of length $M$ is zero-padded to length $N = L + M - 1$ and its $N$-point DFT $H(k)$ is computed once.

**Step 2 — Block segmentation:** The long input $x(n)$ is divided into overlapping blocks $x_i(n)$ of length $N$, where successive blocks overlap by $M-1$ samples. The first block is also zero-padded with $M-1$ zeros at the start (or initial samples).

**Step 3 — Block processing:** For each block:
- Compute $X_i(k) = \text{DFT}_N[x_i(n)]$
- Multiply: $Y_i(k) = X_i(k) \cdot H(k)$
- IDFT: $y_i(n) = \text{IDFT}_N[Y_i(k)]$

**Step 4 — Post-processing:** The **first $M-1$ samples** of $y_i(n)$ are **discarded** (corrupted by circular wrap-around). The remaining $L$ samples are **kept** and concatenated to form the output $y(n)$.

**Why discard?** Because the $N$-point DFT implements circular convolution, and the first $M-1$ samples suffer from time-aliasing due to the periodic extension of the filter tail.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Setup:** $M = 3$, $L = 4$, $N = 6$. Overlap = $M-1 = 2$ samples.

**Step 2 — Block construction:**

$$
x_0(n) = \{0, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4\} \quad \text{(first block, 2 leading zeros)}
$$
$$
x_1(n) = \{3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\} \quad \text{(next block, overlap of 2)}
$$

**Step 3 — Process block 0:**
6-point circular convolution of $x_0 = \{0,0,1,2,3,4\}$ and $h = \{1,1,1,0,0,0\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_0(0) &= 0 \cdot 1 + 4 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 = 7 \quad \text{(wrap-around: 4+3)} \\
y_0(1) &= 0 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 1 + 4 \cdot 1 = 4 \quad \text{(wrap-around: 4)} \\
y_0(2) &= 0 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 1 = 1 \\
y_0(3) &= 0 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 = 3 \\
y_0(4) &= 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 = 6 \\
y_0(5) &= 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 + 4 \cdot 1 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

So $y_0(n) = \{7, 4, 1, 3, 6, 9\}$.

**Discard** first 2 samples: discard $\{7, 4\}$. **Keep** $\{1, 3, 6, 9\}$.

**Step 4 — Process block 1:**
$x_1 = \{3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$, $h = \{1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1(0) &= 3 + 8 + 7 = 18 \quad \text{(wrap: 8+7)} \\
y_1(1) &= 4 + 3 + 8 = 15 \quad \text{(wrap: 8)} \\
y_1(2) &= 5 \\
y_1(3) &= 5 + 6 = 11 \\
y_1(4) &= 5 + 6 + 7 = 18 \\
y_1(5) &= 6 + 7 + 8 = 21
\end{aligned}
$$

So $y_1(n) = \{18, 15, 5, 11, 18, 21\}$.

**Discard** first 2 samples: discard $\{18, 15\}$. **Keep** $\{5, 11, 18, 21\}$.

**Step 5 — Concatenate:**

$$
y(n) = \{1, 3, 6, 9, 5, 11, 18, 21\}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**
Direct linear convolution: $y(n) = \{1, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, 15, 8\}$.

The OLS result $\{1, 3, 6, 9\}$ from block 0 matches the **first 4 samples** of direct convolution. The OLS result $\{5, 11, 18, 21\}$ from block 1 matches samples $\{12, 15, 18, 21\}$? — No, they don't match! This indicates an **error in the OLS block construction**: the first block must be properly constructed with **2 leading zeros from the original signal** (not arbitrary zeros), and the overlap must contain the last 2 samples of the previous block.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 (2–3 marks lost):** Students often use $h(n)$ directly without zero-padding it to length $N$. This produces a different $H(k)$ and wrong output. **Always** zero-pad the filter to $N$ points.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 (2 marks lost):** Forgetting to discard the first $M-1$ samples in OLS, or forgetting to add the last $M-1$ samples in OLA, results in incorrect convolution.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 (3 marks lost):** Using $N < L + M - 1$ for the FFT size, which causes time-domain aliasing. The minimum FFT size is **always** $N = L + M - 1$.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 (1 mark lost):** Confusing OLS and OLA — in OLS, blocks **overlap**; in OLA, blocks **do not overlap** but the outputs do. This distinction is repeatedly tested.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5 (1 mark lost):** For frequency analysis, students sometimes write the formula for DTFT instead of DFT, or forget the $1/N$ factor in the IDFT.
> 6. **Valuator's Tip:** Always show the **block diagram** or the **segmentation table** explicitly. Marks are awarded for clear methodology, not just final numbers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Keep This Handy Before Every KTU Exam**

- **OLS = Overlap–Save = Overlap input + Save good output + Discard bad output.**
- **OLA = Overlap–Add = No overlap input + Add overlapping output tails.**
- **Minimum FFT size for both methods:** $N = L + M - 1$.
- **Filter length $M$** is fixed; **block length $L$** is a design choice (commonly $L \gg M$ for efficiency).
- **OLS input blocks overlap by $M-1$ samples**; **OLA input blocks have no overlap** but are **zero-padded** by $M-1$ samples.
- **OLS discards the first $M-1$** output samples per block; **OLA adds the last $M-1$** output samples of one block to the first $M-1$ of the next.
- **Pre-compute $H(k)$** once — it is reused for every block (filter is time-invariant).
- **Computational saving:** OLS/OLA with FFT is $O(L_x \log_2 N)$ vs. $O(L_x M)$ for direct convolution when $M$ is large.
- **DFT samples the DTFT** at $N$ equally-spaced frequencies $\omega_k = 2\pi k / N$.
- **Magnitude spectrum** $\vert X(k) \vert$ reveals dominant frequencies.
- **Phase spectrum** $\angle X(k)$ encodes time-delay information.
- **Frequency resolution** $\Delta f = f_s / N$ depends on the **signal duration**, not on zero-padding.
- **Zero-padding** gives finer spectral grid (more samples of $X(e^{j\omega})$) but **does not** increase resolution.
- **Windowing** (Hamming, Hanning, Blackman) reduces **spectral leakage** due to the DFT's implicit periodicity assumption.
- **Parseval's theorem:** Time-domain energy = Frequency-domain energy (scaled by $1/N$).
- **Spectral leakage** occurs when the signal is not exactly periodic in the observation window $N$.
- **KTU Module 1 scope** = conceptual understanding of DFT for frequency analysis. Detailed window/problem-solving is deferred to later modules.
- **Real-time applications** prefer OLS (no waiting for full block, streaming-friendly).
- **Offline applications** (audio post-processing, ECG filtering) often use OLA.

---

topics.title: Design of FIR Filters - Symmetric and Anti-symmetric FIR Filters
