# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the impulse invariance method of designing IIR filters? Mention its major limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

The impulse invariance method designs a digital IIR filter $H(z)$ such that its impulse response $h[n]$ is a uniformly sampled (and scaled) version of the analog filter's impulse response $h_a(t)$:

$$
h[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT)
$$

In the $z$-domain, given $H_a(s) = \sum_k \dfrac{R_k}{s-p_k}$:

$$
H(z) = T \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{R_k}{1 - e^{p_k T} z^{-1}}
$$

**Major Limitation:** *Aliasing.* Because of the many-to-one mapping $z = e^{sT}$, frequency components of $H_a(j\Omega)$ above $\Omega = \pi/T$ are folded back into the digital pass-band, distorting the response. Hence the method is suitable **only for strictly band-limited analog filters** (typically low-pass).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the bilinear transformation. Why is pre-warping necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

The bilinear transformation is a one-to-one conformal map from the analog $s$-plane to the digital $z$-plane given by:

$$
s = \frac{2}{T}\cdot\frac{z-1}{z+1} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad z = \frac{1 + sT/2}{1 - sT/2}
$$

The corresponding analog-to-digital frequency relation is non-linear:

$$
\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)
$$

**Why pre-warping is necessary:** Because the warping is non-linear, only one frequency (or pair of frequencies) can be made to match exactly. Pre-warping *forces* a chosen critical digital frequency $\omega_k$ (typically $\omega_p$ or $\omega_s$) to map precisely to the desired analog critical frequency $\Omega_k$:

$$
\Omega_k = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_k}{2}\right)
$$

Without pre-warping, the digital filter's pass-band edge would land at the wrong location and the specifications would be violated.

---

## PART B — LONG Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to design an IIR digital Butterworth low-pass filter using the **bilinear transformation** method. Include the pre-warping step and order-determination formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a digital Butterworth low-pass filter satisfying:
- Pass-band edge: $\omega_p = 0.25\pi$ rad/sample
- Stop-band edge: $\omega_s = 0.55\pi$ rad/sample
- Pass-band ripple: $A_p = 1$ dB
- Stop-band attenuation: $A_s = 15$ dB
- Sampling period: $T = 1$ s

Determine the filter order $N$ and the pre-warped analog frequencies. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Convert digital specs to analog specs via pre-warping:**

$$
\Omega_p = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_p}{2}\right) \qquad \Omega_s = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_s}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Determine filter order $N$ for Butterworth:**

$$
N \geq \frac{\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{10^{0.1A_s}-1}{10^{0.1A_p}-1}\right]}{2\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right]}
$$

Take the **ceiling** to get an integer.

**Step 3 — Find the 3-dB cutoff frequency $\Omega_c$:**

Use either pass-band or stop-band relation (and average / re-check):

$$
\Omega_c = \frac{\Omega_p}{\left(10^{0.1A_p}-1\right)^{1/(2N)}} = \frac{\Omega_s}{\left(10^{0.1A_s}-1\right)^{1/(2N)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Construct the normalized analog prototype** $H_{a,\text{norm}}(s)$ (Butterworth, order $N$):

$$
H_{a,\text{norm}}(s) = \frac{1}{\prod_{k=1}^{N}(s - p_k)}, \quad p_k = \Omega_c e^{j\pi(2k+N-1)/(2N)}
$$

**Step 5 — Frequency-scale the prototype to $\Omega_c$:**

Replace $s$ by $s/\Omega_c$ in the normalized prototype to get $H_a(s)$.

**Step 6 — Apply BLT:** Substitute $s = \dfrac{2}{T}\dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$ into $H_a(s)$ and simplify to obtain $H(z)$.

**Step 7 — Realize** $H(z)$ using Direct Form-I or cascade form.

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Pre-warp the critical frequencies:**

$$
\Omega_p = \frac{2}{1}\tan\!\left(\frac{0.25\pi}{2}\right) = 2\tan(0.125\pi) = 2 \times 0.4142 = 0.8284 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\Omega_s = \frac{2}{1}\tan\!\left(\frac{0.55\pi}{2}\right) = 2\tan(0.275\pi) = 2 \times 1.1708 = 2.3416 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator and denominator of order formula:**

$$
10^{0.1 \times 1} - 1 = 10^{0.1} - 1 = 1.2589 - 1 = 0.2589
$$

$$
10^{0.1 \times 15} - 1 = 10^{1.5} - 1 = 31.6228 - 1 = 30.6228
$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**

$$
\frac{10^{0.1A_s}-1}{10^{0.1A_p}-1} = \frac{30.6228}{0.2589} = 118.27
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right) = 2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.3416}{0.8284}\right) = 2 \log_{10}(2.8264) = 2 \times 0.4512 = 0.9024
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $N$:**

$$
N \geq \frac{\log_{10}(118.27)}{0.9024} = \frac{2.0729}{0.9024} = 2.297
$$

**Step 6 — Take the ceiling:**

$$
\boxed{\,N = 3\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute 3-dB cutoff $\Omega_c$ using the pass-band relation:**

$$
\Omega_c = \frac{0.8284}{(0.2589)^{1/6}} = \frac{0.8284}{0.7943} = 1.0429 \text{ rad/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The final digital filter will be of order **$N = 3$**, with the pre-warped analog prototype cutoff at $\Omega_c \approx 1.043$ rad/s. Subsequent design requires constructing the third-order Butterworth pole locations on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$ and applying BLT.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the bilinear transformation formula $s = \dfrac{2}{T}\dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$ starting from the trapezoidal rule for numerical integration. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the analog filter $H_a(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,s + 1}$, obtain the digital filter $H(z)$ using the bilinear transformation with $T = 1$ s. Verify stability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Continuous-time integrator in the $s$-domain:**

For a continuous integrator:

$$
y_a(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x_a(\tau) \, d\tau \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad H_a(s) = \frac{Y_a(s)}{X_a(s)} = \frac{1}{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Trapezoidal approximation of the integral:**

$$
y(n) = y(n-1) + \frac{T}{2}\bigl[x(n) + x(n-1)\bigr]
$$

This approximates the area under $x(\tau)$ between $(n-1)T$ and $nT$ as a trapezoid.

**Step 3 — Take the Z-transform of both sides:**

Using $y(n-1) \leftrightarrow z^{-1}Y(z)$ and $x(n-1) \leftrightarrow z^{-1}X(z)$:

$$
Y(z) = z^{-1}Y(z) + \frac{T}{2}\bigl[X(z) + z^{-1}X(z)\bigr]
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$:**

$$
Y(z)\bigl[1 - z^{-1}\bigr] = \frac{T}{2}\,X(z)\bigl[1 + z^{-1}\bigr]
$$

$$
H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{T}{2}\cdot\frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $z$:

$$
H(z) = \frac{T}{2}\cdot\frac{z + 1}{z - 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Equate to the analog integrator:**

Since both represent an integrator, $H_a(s) = 1/s$ corresponds to $H(z) = \dfrac{T}{2}\dfrac{z+1}{z-1}$:

$$
\frac{1}{s} = \frac{T}{2}\cdot\frac{z+1}{z-1}
$$

**Step 6 — Invert to obtain $s$ in terms of $z$:**

$$
s = \frac{2}{T}\cdot\frac{z-1}{z+1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,s = \frac{2}{T}\cdot\frac{z-1}{z+1}\,}
$$

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Write the analog transfer function:**

$$
H_a(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,s + 1}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the BLT** with $T = 1$ s:

$$
s = \frac{2(z-1)}{z+1}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the denominator:**

$$
s^2 = \left[\frac{2(z-1)}{z+1}\right]^2 = \frac{4(z-1)^2}{(z+1)^2}
$$

$$
\sqrt{2}\,s = \frac{2\sqrt{2}(z-1)}{z+1}
$$

The denominator becomes:

$$
D = \frac{4(z-1)^2}{(z+1)^2} + \frac{2\sqrt{2}(z-1)}{z+1} + 1
$$

**Step 4 — Common denominator $(z+1)^2$:**

$$
D = \frac{4(z-1)^2 + 2\sqrt{2}(z-1)(z+1) + (z+1)^2}{(z+1)^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Expand each term:**

- $4(z-1)^2 = 4(z^2 - 2z + 1) = 4z^2 - 8z + 4$
- $2\sqrt{2}(z-1)(z+1) = 2\sqrt{2}(z^2 - 1) = 2\sqrt{2}\,z^2 - 2\sqrt{2}$
- $(z+1)^2 = z^2 + 2z + 1$

**Step 6 — Sum them:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = (4 + 2\sqrt{2} + 1)z^2 + (-8 + 2)z + (4 - 2\sqrt{2} + 1)
$$

$$
= (5 + 2\sqrt{2})z^2 - 6z + (5 - 2\sqrt{2})
$$

Using $2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828$:

$$
\approx 7.828\,z^2 - 6z + 2.172
$$

**Step 7 — Therefore $H(z)$:**

$$
H(z) = \frac{(z+1)^2}{7.828\,z^2 - 6z + 2.172}
$$

**Step 8 — Express in $z^{-1}$ form:**

Divide numerator and denominator by $z^2$:

$$
\boxed{\,H(z) = \frac{1 + 2z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{7.828 - 6z^{-1} + 2.172\,z^{-2}}\,}
$$

**Step 9 — Verify stability — find digital poles:**

Solve $7.828\,z^2 - 6z + 2.172 = 0$:

$$
z = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{36 - 4(7.828)(2.172)}}{2(7.828)} = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{36 - 68.0}}{15.656} = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{-32.0}}{15.656}
$$

$$
z = \frac{6 \pm j\,5.657}{15.656} = 0.3833 \pm j\,0.3613
$$

Magnitude: $\vert z \vert = \sqrt{0.3833^2 + 0.3613^2} = \sqrt{0.1469 + 0.1305} = \sqrt{0.2774} = 0.5267$

**Both poles have magnitude $0.5267 < 1$ ⇒ stable.** ✔

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the pre-warping step** in the bilinear method — costs 2–3 marks immediately. Always pre-warp *both* $\Omega_p$ and $\Omega_s$ before computing the order $N$.
> 2. **Using $\omega = \Omega T$ in bilinear problems** — this is the *impulse invariance* relation, not the BLT. The correct BLT relation is $\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan(\omega/2)$.
> 3. **Choosing the wrong method** — Impulse invariance is band-limited; using it for a high-pass or wide-band filter leads to severe aliasing. KTU examiners specifically test whether you recognize this.
> 4. **Forgetting the $T$ scaling factor** in the IIM formula $H(z) = T \sum R_k / (1 - e^{p_k T} z^{-1})$. Many students drop the $T$.
> 5. **Stability verification not done** — KTU awards 1 mark specifically for checking that all digital poles lie inside the unit circle.
> 6. **Order $N$ rounding error** — always take the **ceiling** of the order formula, never the floor. A 2nd-order filter that meets pass-band spec may not meet stop-band spec.
> 7. **Not mentioning aliasing** as a limitation of IIM — even in a 3-mark definition question, this is mandatory.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density, last-minute revision summary:**

- [ ] **IIR filters** have feedback (denominator polynomial in $z^{-1}$); impulse response is infinitely long.
- [ ] **Design strategy:** Digital specs → Analog prototype $H_a(s)$ → Transformation → $H(z)$ → Realize.
- [ ] **Impulse Invariance (IIM):** $h[n] = T\,h_a(nT)$. Mapping: $z_k = e^{p_k T}$. Major drawback: **aliasing**. Use only for **band-limited** low-pass/band-pass filters.
- [ ] **Bilinear Transformation (BLT):** $s = \dfrac{2}{T}\dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$. One-to-one, no aliasing. Use for **all** filter types. **Pre-warp** critical frequencies: $\Omega_k = \dfrac{2}{T}\tan(\omega_k/2)$.
- [ ] **Frequency warping** is non-linear in BLT: $\Omega = \dfrac{2}{T}\tan(\omega/2)$.
- [ ] **Stability:** LHP in $s$ ↔ inside unit circle in $z$ for **both** methods.
- [ ] **Butterworth magnitude:** $\vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1+(\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}}$ — maximally flat pass-band.
- [ ] **Chebyshev Type-I:** equiripple in pass-band, monotonic in stop-band. Type of $T_N(\cdot)$ polynomial.
- [ ] **Order formula (Butterworth):** $N \geq \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\left[(10^{0.1A_s}-1)/(10^{0.1A_p}-1)\right]}{2\log_{10}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)}$ — take the ceiling.
- [ ] **3-dB cutoff from specs:** $\Omega_c = \dfrac{\Omega_p}{(10^{0.1A_p}-1)^{1/(2N)}}$.
- [ ] **Pole locations of Butterworth:** $p_k = \Omega_c e^{j\pi(2k+N-1)/(2N)}$ for $k=1,2,\dots,N$ — on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$ in the LHP.
- [ ] **Digital filter realization options:** Direct Form-I, Direct Form-II, Cascade (1st/2nd-order sections), Parallel.
- [ ] **Engineering applications:** digital audio crossovers, biomedical filters (ECG denoising), SDR channel filters, control systems, IoT edge DSP.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Transformations in the Analog Domain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT504 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Frequency Transformations in the Analog Domain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Frequency Transformation in the Analog Domain** is a systematic mathematical procedure used in analog filter design whereby a **normalized lowpass prototype filter** $H_a(s)$ — designed with a reference cutoff frequency of $\Omega_c = 1$ rad/s — is algebraically mapped into a different filter type (lowpass, highpass, bandpass, or bandstop) and/or shifted to a new set of cutoff frequencies by substituting the complex frequency variable $s$ with a rational function of $s$.

The general transformation is denoted as:
$$s \longrightarrow g(s)$$
where $g(s)$ is a **rational function of $s$** chosen according to the desired filter type.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Definition:** A frequency transformation preserves the **magnitude response shape** of the prototype filter while altering its **passband location** and **bandwidth**. The transformation is applied directly to the transfer function $H_a(s)$, not to individual components.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you have a **master recipe for plain white bread** (the normalized lowpass filter). Instead of writing four completely different recipes for whole wheat, rye, focaccia, and bagels, you simply apply **transformation rules** to your master recipe — "if rye, add caraway seeds and molasses"; "if bagel, boil before baking".

In filter design:
- The **master recipe** = normalized lowpass prototype $H_a(s)$ with $\Omega_c = 1$
- The **transformation rules** = the substitution $s \rightarrow g(s)$
- The **different breads** = highpass, bandpass, bandstop filters with any desired cutoff

> [!NOTE]
> Just as the chef **doesn't start from scratch**, the engineer **doesn't design every filter from scratch** — they use a single prototype and apply frequency transformations to obtain all four filter types economically.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Frequency Transformations?

| Reason | Engineering Justification |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Design Reusability** | One prototype (Butterworth, Chebyshev, etc.) yields four filter families. |
| **Time Efficiency** | Avoids repeated approximation mathematics. |
| **Tabulated Values** | Standardized tables exist for normalized prototypes. |
| **Scalability** | Easy to retune filters to new specifications. |
| **Physical Realizability** | Transformations are applied mathematically; hardware/circuit design follows from $H_a(s)$. |

> [!TIP]
> The transformations are **mathematically exact** and **do not introduce any approximation error** — they only re-map the $s$-plane. The **shape** (ripple, roll-off rate) is preserved; only the **frequency axis is stretched, inverted, or warped**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Transformations

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1: Start with a prototype.**
Design (or obtain from tables) a normalized lowpass filter $H_a(s)$ with passband edge $\Omega_p = 1$ rad/s.

**Step 2: Choose the target filter type.**
Select one of the four canonical filter types based on the application requirement.

**Step 3: Substitute $s$ using the appropriate transformation rule.**
The variable $s$ in the prototype is replaced by $g(s)$ as listed in the formula sheet below.

**Step 4: Simplify and obtain the new transfer function $H_{new}(s)$.**
Algebraic manipulation yields the final filter. The order of the filter is preserved for LP↔LP and LP↔HP, but is **doubled** for LP↔BP and LP↔BS transformations.

**Step 5: Verify passband/stopband edges.**
Confirm the new transfer function satisfies the desired specifications.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All values $\Omega$ are in rad/s (analog angular frequency)**. The bandwidth $B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l$, and $\Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_l \cdot \Omega_u}$ is the **geometric center frequency**.

| Transformation | Substitution $s \rightarrow$ | Order Change | Final Transfer Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Lowpass → Lowpass** | $s / \Omega_0$ | Same order $N$ | $H_{LP}(s) = H_a(s / \Omega_0)$ |
| **Lowpass → Highpass** | $\Omega_0 / s$ | Same order $N$ | $H_{HP}(s) = H_a(\Omega_0 / s)$ |
| **Lowpass → Bandpass** | $(s^2 + \Omega_0^2)/(s \cdot B)$ | Doubled: $2N$ | $H_{BP}(s) = H_a\!\left(\dfrac{s^2 + \Omega_0^2}{sB}\right)$ |
| **Lowpass → Bandstop** | $(s \cdot B)/(s^2 + \Omega_0^2)$ | Doubled: $2N$ | $H_{BS}(s) = H_a\!\left(\dfrac{sB}{s^2 + \Omega_0^2}\right)$ |

### Notation Convention

- $B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l$ — **Bandwidth** (rad/s)
- $\Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_l \cdot \Omega_u}$ — **Geometric center frequency** (rad/s)
- $\Omega_l$ — Lower cutoff (rad/s)
- $\Omega_u$ — Upper cutoff (rad/s)
- $\Omega_0$ (LP/HP) — Desired cutoff frequency (rad/s)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Application Domains for Frequency Transformations:**
> - **Telecommunications:** Designing channel-selection filters in RF front-ends.
> - **Audio Engineering:** Graphic equalizers (bandpass filters) and rumble filters (highpass).
> - **Biomedical:** ECG/EEG signal conditioning (bandpass to remove baseline drift and 50/60 Hz noise).
> - **Control Systems:** Compensator design (lead/lag compensators derived from prototypes).
> - **Power Systems:** Harmonic notch filters (bandstop) to eliminate specific interference frequencies.

## 2.4 The Underlying Mathematics

For the transformation to be valid, $g(s)$ must satisfy **two key properties**:

1. **Rational Function:** $g(s)$ must be a ratio of polynomials in $s$ (so that the new $H(s)$ remains a rational transfer function).
2. **Imaginary-Axis Mapping:** $g(j\Omega)$ must map the $j\Omega$-axis of the prototype to the $j\Omega$-axis of the target. This guarantees that a stable, minimum-phase prototype yields a stable, minimum-phase target filter.

> [!TIP]
> The most elegant mathematical justification: $g(s)$ is a **conformal mapping** that takes the $j\Omega$-axis onto itself, preserving filter stability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lowpass to Lowpass (LP → LP)

### Objective
Shift the cutoff of a normalized LPF from $\Omega_c = 1$ rad/s to a new cutoff $\Omega_0$ rad/s.

### Derivation

We require the new filter to satisfy:
$$\vert H_{LP}(j\Omega_0) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\Omega_0 / \Omega_0')^2}}$$
where $\Omega_0'$ is the new cutoff. Setting $\Omega_0' = \Omega_0$ in the prototype $H_a(s) = H_a(j\omega)$ with $\omega$ as the prototype frequency:

$$\omega = \dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_0}$$

Therefore the substitution is:
$$s \longrightarrow \dfrac{s}{\Omega_0}$$

And the new transfer function is:
$$H_{LP}(s) = H_a\!\left(\dfrac{s}{\Omega_0}\right)$$

### Worked Example

**Problem:** A normalized LPF has transfer function $H_a(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$. Find the new LPF with cutoff $\Omega_0 = 100$ rad/s.

**Solution:**

Apply the substitution $s \to s / 100$:

$$H_{LP}(s) = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{s}{100} + 1}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by 100:

$$H_{LP}(s) = \dfrac{100}{s + 100}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check:** At $\Omega = 100$ rad/s, $\vert H_{LP}(j100) \vert = \dfrac{100}{\sqrt{100^2 + 100^2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, confirming the $-3$ dB cutoff. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lowpass to Highpass (LP → HP)

### Objective
Convert a normalized LPF (passing low frequencies, blocking high) into a HPF (passing high frequencies, blocking low) with cutoff $\Omega_0$ rad/s.

### Derivation

The lowpass prototype has response:
$$\vert H_a(j\omega) \vert = f(\omega) \quad \text{(decreasing with } \omega\text{)}$$

For a highpass response, we require the new frequency $\omega_{new}$ to satisfy:
$$\omega_{new} = \dfrac{\Omega_0}{\Omega}$$

This way, when $\Omega \to 0$ (low freq), $\omega_{new} \to \infty$ (blocked by LPF prototype); and when $\Omega \to \infty$ (high freq), $\omega_{new} \to 0$ (passed by LPF prototype). The substitution is:
$$s \longrightarrow \dfrac{\Omega_0}{s}$$

And the new transfer function is:
$$H_{HP}(s) = H_a\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega_0}{s}\right)$$

### Worked Example

**Problem:** Normalized LPF $H_a(s) = \dfrac{1}{(s+1)^2}$. Find the HPF with cutoff $\Omega_0 = 200$ rad/s.

**Solution:**

Apply $s \to 200/s$:

$$H_{HP}(s) = \dfrac{1}{\left(\dfrac{200}{s} + 1\right)^2}$$

Simplify the inner expression:

$$\dfrac{200}{s} + 1 = \dfrac{200 + s}{s}$$

Square it:

$$\left(\dfrac{200 + s}{s}\right)^2 = \dfrac{(s + 200)^2}{s^2}$$

Substitute back:

$$H_{HP}(s) = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{(s + 200)^2}{s^2}} = \dfrac{s^2}{(s + 200)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check:** As $s \to 0$ (low freq), $H_{HP}(s) \to 0$ (blocked). As $s \to \infty$ (high freq), $H_{HP}(s) \to 1$ (passed). ✓ **Order preserved at 2.**

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lowpass to Bandpass (LP → BP)

### Objective
Convert a normalized LPF into a BPF with passband $[\Omega_l, \Omega_u]$.

### Derivation

Define:
- $B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l$ (bandwidth)
- $\Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_l \cdot \Omega_u}$ (geometric center)

We require the prototype frequency $\omega$ to behave as:
$$\omega = \dfrac{\Omega^2 - \Omega_0^2}{\Omega \cdot B}$$

The reasoning: when $\Omega = \Omega_l$ or $\Omega = \Omega_u$, we get $\omega = \pm 1$ (edges of the LPF passband). When $\Omega = \Omega_0$, we get $\omega = 0$ (peak of LPF, i.e., passband center of BPF).

The substitution is:
$$s \longrightarrow \dfrac{s^2 + \Omega_0^2}{s \cdot B}$$

And the new transfer function is:
$$H_{BP}(s) = H_a\!\left(\dfrac{s^2 + \Omega_0^2}{sB}\right)$$

### Worked Example

**Problem:** Normalized 2nd-order Butterworth LPF $H_a(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,s + 1}$. Convert to BPF with $\Omega_l = 900$ rad/s and $\Omega_u = 1100$ rad/s.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute parameters.

$$B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l = 1100 - 900 = 200 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_l \cdot \Omega_u} = \sqrt{900 \times 1100} = \sqrt{990{,}000} \approx 994.99 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the substitution. Let:

$$s \to \dfrac{s^2 + \Omega_0^2}{sB} = \dfrac{s^2 + 990000}{200s}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into $s^2$ term:

$$s^2 \to \left(\dfrac{s^2 + 990000}{200s}\right)^2 = \dfrac{(s^2 + 990000)^2}{40000 \, s^2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into $s$ term:

$$s \to \dfrac{s^2 + 990000}{200s}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into constant $1$ (unchanged).

**Step 6:** Write the new denominator:

$$D(s) = \dfrac{(s^2 + 990000)^2}{40000 \, s^2} + \sqrt{2} \cdot \dfrac{s^2 + 990000}{200s} + 1$$

**Step 7:** Multiply through by $40000 s^2$ to clear denominators:

$$D(s) \cdot 40000 s^2 = (s^2 + 990000)^2 + 200\sqrt{2} \, s (s^2 + 990000) + 40000 s^2$$

**Step 8:** Final transfer function:

$$H_{BP}(s) = \dfrac{40000 \, s^2}{(s^2 + 990000)^2 + 200\sqrt{2} \, s(s^2 + 990000) + 40000 s^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Order is doubled:** A 2nd-order LPF yields a 4th-order BPF. ✓ The DC gain is 0 (since $s = 0$ gives $H = 0$), and the high-frequency gain is 0 — characteristic of a BPF.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Lowpass to Bandstop (LP → BS)

### Objective
Convert a normalized LPF into a BSF (notch filter) with stopband $[\Omega_l, \Omega_u]$.

### Derivation

By analogous reasoning, the prototype frequency is:
$$\omega = \dfrac{\Omega \cdot B}{\Omega_0^2 - \Omega^2} = \dfrac{\Omega \cdot B}{\vert \Omega_0^2 - \Omega^2 \vert}$$

The substitution is:
$$s \longrightarrow \dfrac{sB}{s^2 + \Omega_0^2}$$

And the new transfer function is:
$$H_{BS}(s) = H_a\!\left(\dfrac{sB}{s^2 + \Omega_0^2}\right)$$

### Worked Example

**Problem:** Normalized LPF $H_a(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$. Convert to BSF with $\Omega_l = 90$ rad/s, $\Omega_u = 110$ rad/s.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute parameters.

$$B = 110 - 90 = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\Omega_0 = \sqrt{90 \times 110} = \sqrt{9900} \approx 99.50 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the substitution $s \to sB/(s^2 + \Omega_0^2) = 20s/(s^2 + 9900)$:

$$H_{BS}(s) = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{20s}{s^2 + 9900} + 1}$$

**Step 3:** Combine the denominator:

$$\dfrac{20s}{s^2 + 9900} + 1 = \dfrac{20s + s^2 + 9900}{s^2 + 9900} = \dfrac{s^2 + 20s + 9900}{s^2 + 9900}$$

**Step 4:** Final transfer function:

$$H_{BS}(s) = \dfrac{s^2 + 9900}{s^2 + 20s + 9900}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check:** At $s = 0$ (DC): $H_{BS}(0) = 9900/9900 = 1$ (passes low freq ✓). At $s = j\Omega_0$ (center): $H_{BS}(j\Omega_0) = 0$ (notch ✓). At $s \to \infty$: $H_{BS} \to 1$ (passes high freq ✓). **Order doubled from 1 to 2.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below illustrates the systematic procedure of analog filter design using frequency transformations. It also maps each transformation to the corresponding $s$-plane behavior — the $j\Omega$-axis is preserved (conformal mapping property) while the impedance locus is shifted.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Normalized LPF Prototype H of s with cutoff 1 rad per sec] --> decisionB{Choose Target Filter Type}
    decisionB --> routeC[Lowpass to Lowpass LP to LP]
    decisionB --> routeD[Lowpass to Highpass LP to HP]
    decisionB --> routeE[Lowpass to Bandpass LP to BP]
    decisionB --> routeF[Lowpass to Bandstop LP to BS]
    routeC --> subC1[Substitute s with s divided by omega0]
    routeD --> subD1[Substitute s with omega0 divided by s]
    routeE --> subE1[Substitute s with s squared plus omega0 squared over s times B]
    routeF --> subF1[Substitute s with s times B over s squared plus omega0 squared]
    subC1 --> outC[Final H LP of s with new cutoff omega0]
    subD1 --> outD[Final H HP of s with cutoff omega0]
    subE1 --> outE[Final H BP of s with bandwidth B and center omega0]
    subF1 --> outF[Final H BS of s with notch band omegaL to omegaU]
    outC --> verify[Verify Passband and Stopband Edges]
    outD --> verify
    outE --> verify
    outF --> verify
    verify --> endA[Filter Realization using Active RC or Opamp Circuit]
```

## 4.2 Transformation Mapping Summary Diagram

The diagram below maps each canonical transformation to its **operational effect on the $j\Omega$-axis**, providing a visual understanding of how frequencies are re-mapped.

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Source LPF Axis omega equals 0 to infinity] --> lp2lpT[LP to LP Transformation]
    srcA --> lp2hpT[LP to HP Transformation]
    srcA --> lp2bpT[LP to BP Transformation]
    srcA --> lp2bsT[LP to BS Transformation]
    lp2lpT --> dstA[Destination LP axis scaled by 1 over omega0]
    lp2hpT --> dstB[Destination HP axis inverted and scaled by omega0]
    lp2bpT --> dstC[Destination BP axis with center omega0 and bandwidth B]
    lp2bsT --> dstD[Destination BS axis with notch between omegaL and omegaU]
    dstA --> note1[Passband shifts to omega0]
    dstB --> note2[Passband at high frequencies above omega0]
    dstC --> note3[Passband at omegaL to omegaU]
    dstD --> note4[Stopband at omegaL to omegaU]
```

## 4.3 Filter Order Transformation Matrix

```mermaid
graph TD
    baseN[Order N Prototype] --> t1[LP to LP gives Order N]
    baseN --> t2[LP to HP gives Order N]
    baseN --> t3[LP to BP gives Order 2N]
    baseN --> t4[LP to BS gives Order 2N]
    t1 --> rule1[No order change reason single substitution is linear in s]
    t2 --> rule2[No order change reason reciprocal substitution s inverse preserves order]
    t3 --> rule3[Order doubled reason s replaced by quadratic rational function in s]
    t4 --> rule4[Order doubled reason s replaced by quadratic rational function in s]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Takeaway:** In BP and BS transformations, the substitution contains $s^2$, causing the denominator of the original $H_a(s)$ to expand quadratically — this mathematically explains why the order doubles. For a 5th-order prototype, you obtain a 10th-order BPF or BSF.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**State the need for frequency transformations in analog filter design. List the four canonical transformations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **need for frequency transformations** arises because designing filters of different types (lowpass, highpass, bandpass, bandstop) and arbitrary cutoff frequencies from scratch is **mathematically tedious and time-consuming**. By designing a single **normalized lowpass prototype** with $\Omega_c = 1$ rad/s, and then applying algebraic substitutions, we can efficiently obtain all four filter types with desired specifications. This reduces design complexity, leverages tabulated prototype values, and ensures reusability of design procedures.

The **four canonical transformations** are:
1. Lowpass to Lowpass (LP → LP)
2. Lowpass to Highpass (LP → HP)
3. Lowpass to Bandpass (LP → BP)
4. Lowpass to Bandstop (LP → BS)

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Obtain the frequency transformation relation to convert a normalized lowpass filter with cutoff 1 rad/s to a highpass filter with cutoff $\Omega_0$ rad/s.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a lowpass prototype $H_a(s)$ with cutoff $\omega_c = 1$ rad/s, the response at prototype frequency $\omega$ is governed by the substitution. For a highpass filter, low frequencies must be blocked and high frequencies passed. We require the prototype frequency to behave as:

$$\omega = \dfrac{\Omega_0}{\Omega}$$

so that $\Omega \to 0$ maps to $\omega \to \infty$ (blocked) and $\Omega \to \infty$ maps to $\omega \to 0$ (passed). The transformation is:

$$s \longrightarrow \dfrac{\Omega_0}{s}$$

Therefore:
$$H_{HP}(s) = H_a\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega_0}{s}\right)$$

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks. Each sub-question (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**. Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** is mandatory as per the 2024 scheme.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the frequency transformation to convert a normalized lowpass filter into a **bandpass filter** with lower cutoff $\Omega_l$ and upper cutoff $\Omega_u$. State the effect on filter order. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A normalized second-order Butterworth lowpass filter has the transfer function:
$$H_a(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,s + 1}$$
Transform it into a bandpass filter with $\Omega_l = 1000$ rad/s and $\Omega_u = 2000$ rad/s. Find the resulting transfer function. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

We need a transformation that maps the prototype $j\omega$-axis to the BPF $j\Omega$-axis. For a BPF, the passband extends from $\Omega_l$ to $\Omega_u$, with the geometric center:
$$\Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_l \cdot \Omega_u}$$
and bandwidth:
$$B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l$$

**Step 1:** The prototype frequency $\omega$ must equal $\pm 1$ at the band edges $\Omega = \Omega_l$ and $\Omega = \Omega_u$, and must equal 0 at the center $\Omega = \Omega_0$. This is satisfied by:
$$\omega = \dfrac{\Omega^2 - \Omega_0^2}{\Omega \cdot B}$$

**Step 2:** Therefore the substitution is:
$$s \longrightarrow \dfrac{s^2 + \Omega_0^2}{s \cdot B}$$

**Step 3:** The resulting transfer function is:
$$H_{BP}(s) = H_a\!\left(\dfrac{s^2 + \Omega_0^2}{sB}\right)$$

**Step 4:** **Order Doubling Effect:** Since the substitution contains $s^2$, the degree of the denominator doubles. An $N$-th order prototype becomes a $2N$-th order BPF. **(1 mark)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute parameters.
$$B = 2000 - 1000 = 1000 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\Omega_0 = \sqrt{1000 \times 2000} = \sqrt{2{,}000{,}000} = 1000\sqrt{2} \approx 1414.21 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the substitution $s \to (s^2 + \Omega_0^2)/(sB) = (s^2 + 2 \times 10^6)/(1000s)$.

**Step 3:** Compute $s^2$ term in the prototype:
$$s^2 \to \dfrac{(s^2 + 2 \times 10^6)^2}{10^6 s^2}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $s$ term in the prototype:
$$s \to \dfrac{s^2 + 2 \times 10^6}{1000 s}$$

**Step 5:** The new transfer function denominator becomes:
$$D_{BP}(s) = \dfrac{(s^2 + 2 \times 10^6)^2}{10^6 s^2} + \dfrac{\sqrt{2}(s^2 + 2 \times 10^6)}{1000 s} + 1$$

**Step 6:** Multiply throughout by $10^6 s^2$:
$$D_{BP}(s) \cdot 10^6 s^2 = (s^2 + 2 \times 10^6)^2 + 1000\sqrt{2} \, s(s^2 + 2 \times 10^6) + 10^6 s^2$$

**Step 7:** Final transfer function:
$$\boxed{H_{BP}(s) = \dfrac{10^6 \, s^2}{(s^2 + 2 \times 10^6)^2 + 1000\sqrt{2} \, s(s^2 + 2 \times 10^6) + 10^6 s^2}}$$

**Order:** 2nd-order prototype → **4th-order** BPF. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the four frequency transformations in the analog domain. Mention how the filter order is affected in each case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A normalized lowpass filter has the transfer function:
$$H_a(s) = \dfrac{1}{(s+1)^2}$$
Design a **highpass filter** with cutoff frequency $\Omega_0 = 500$ rad/s. Determine the transfer function. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Explanation (7 marks):**

| Transformation | Substitution | Order Effect | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| LP → LP | $s/\Omega_0$ | Same order $N$ | Shift cutoff |
| LP → HP | $\Omega_0/s$ | Same order $N$ | Block DC, pass AC |
| LP → BP | $(s^2 + \Omega_0^2)/(sB)$ | Doubled: $2N$ | Select frequency band |
| LP → BS | $sB/(s^2 + \Omega_0^2)$ | Doubled: $2N$ | Notch out interference |

For LP→LP and LP→HP, the substitution is **linear** in $s$ (or its reciprocal), so the order of the resulting transfer function is unchanged. For LP→BP and LP→BS, the substitution involves $s^2$, causing the polynomial degrees in the denominator to double, hence the order doubles from $N$ to $2N$. **(3 marks for explanation, 4 marks for table and order effect)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify the prototype.
$$H_a(s) = \dfrac{1}{(s+1)^2}$$

**Step 2:** Apply LP → HP substitution with $\Omega_0 = 500$ rad/s: $s \to 500/s$.

**Step 3:** Substitute into the prototype:
$$H_{HP}(s) = \dfrac{1}{\left(\dfrac{500}{s} + 1\right)^2}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the inner expression:
$$\dfrac{500}{s} + 1 = \dfrac{500 + s}{s}$$

**Step 5:** Square the inner expression:
$$\left(\dfrac{s + 500}{s}\right)^2 = \dfrac{(s + 500)^2}{s^2}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back:
$$H_{HP}(s) = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{(s+500)^2}{s^2}} = \dfrac{s^2}{(s+500)^2}$$

**Step 7:** Expand the denominator if required:
$$(s+500)^2 = s^2 + 1000s + 250000$$

So the final transfer function is:
$$\boxed{H_{HP}(s) = \dfrac{s^2}{s^2 + 1000s + 250000}}$$

**Verification:**
- At $s = 0$ (DC): $H_{HP}(0) = 0/250000 = 0$ ✓ (DC blocked)
- At $s = j500$: $\vert H_{HP}(j500) \vert = \vert 250000 \vert / \vert 250000 - 250000 + j1000 \cdot 500 \vert$ ... cutoff confirmed.
- At $s \to \infty$: $H_{HP} \to 1$ ✓ (high frequencies passed)
- **Order preserved at 2.** ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $B$ and $\Omega_0$ explicitly** in BP/BS problems — this is where **2 marks** are typically lost. Always write: $B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l$ and $\Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u}$ at the **start** of the solution.
> 2. **Algebraic sign errors** when simplifying the substitution. Double-check the simplification $\Omega_0/s + 1 = (s + \Omega_0)/s$ — a common mistake is writing $(s - \Omega_0)/s$.
> 3. **Not stating the order change** in derivations — examiners **explicitly allocate marks** for mentioning that BP/BS transformations **double the filter order**.
> 4. **Failing to verify** the resulting transfer function at $\Omega = 0$ and $\Omega \to \infty$ — a quick check can catch sign/order mistakes.
> 5. **Mixing up $B$ and $\Omega_0$** — recall: $B$ is the **arithmetic** difference, $\Omega_0$ is the **geometric mean** of the cutoffs.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2):**

- ⭐ **Master Transformation:** $s \to g(s)$, where $g(s)$ is a rational function in $s$.
- ⭐ **LP → LP:** Substitute $s \to s/\Omega_0$. **Order unchanged.**
- ⭐ **LP → HP:** Substitute $s \to \Omega_0/s$. **Order unchanged.**
- ⭐ **LP → BP:** Substitute $s \to (s^2 + \Omega_0^2)/(sB)$. **Order doubles to $2N$.**
- ⭐ **LP → BS:** Substitute $s \to sB/(s^2 + \Omega_0^2)$. **Order doubles to $2N$.**
- ⭐ **Bandwidth Formula:** $B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l$ (arithmetic difference).
- ⭐ **Center Frequency Formula:** $\Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_l \cdot \Omega_u}$ (geometric mean, not arithmetic mean).
- ⭐ **Prototype Requirement:** All transformations start from a **normalized LPF with $\Omega_c = 1$ rad/s**.
- ⭐ **Conformal Mapping:** $g(j\Omega)$ maps the $j\Omega$-axis to itself, preserving stability.
- ⭐ **Order Doubling Reason:** The $s^2$ term in BP/BS substitutions increases polynomial degree.
- ⭐ **Verification Trick:** At $s = 0$, LP→HP gives 0; LP→LP gives $H_a(0)$; LP→BP gives 0; LP→BS gives 1.
- ⭐ **High-Frequency Behavior:** LP→HP and LP→BS tend to **constant gain** as $s \to \infty$; LP→LP and LP→BP tend to **0** as $s \to \infty$.
- ⭐ **Units Consistency:** All frequencies in rad/s for analog; Hz is used only in digital domain.
- ⭐ **Practical Tip:** When answering derivations, always begin with "Let $\omega$ = prototype frequency, $\Omega$ = target frequency" to clearly set up the mapping.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structures for the Realization of Discrete-Time Systems - Block Diagram and Signal Flow Graph Representations of Filters; FIR Filter Structures - Linear Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT504 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Structures for the Realization of Discrete-Time Systems - Block Diagram and Signal Flow Graph Representations of Filters; FIR Filter Structures - Linear Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Realization" of a Discrete-Time System?

In Digital Signal Processing (DSP), a discrete-time system is mathematically described by its **difference equation** or equivalently by its **transfer function** $H(z)$. However, a mathematical formula is only a *specification* — it does not tell us *how* to actually build the system using physical hardware (or simulate it in software).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** A **realization (or structure)** of a discrete-time system is a block diagram (or signal flow graph) that shows exactly how the input samples $x(n)$, delayed samples $x(n-k)$, multipliers, adders, and delay elements are interconnected to compute the output $y(n)$ from the input $x(n)$.

A single transfer function $H(z)$ can be realized in **multiple, mathematically equivalent ways** (Direct Form, Cascade Form, Lattice Form, etc.). Each structure differs in:
- The **number of internal memory elements (delays)** required
- **Sensitivity to coefficient quantization** (finite word-length effects)
- **Computational complexity** (number of multiplications and additions per output sample)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe vs. Kitchen" View

Think of the difference equation as a **recipe** that says *what* ingredients (input samples) go in and *what* dish (output) should come out. A **realization structure** is the actual **kitchen layout** — which chef grabs which ingredient, in what order, and where the mixing bowls are placed.

| Analogy Element | DSP Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Recipe | Difference equation / Transfer function $H(z)$ |
| Ingredient list | Coefficients $\{h(k)\}$ or $\{a_k, b_k\}$ |
| Cold storage (delays) | Unit delay elements $z^{-1}$ |
| Chef who multiplies weights | Multiplier (coefficient) |
| Mixing bowl | Adder (summing junction) |
| Kitchen blueprint | Block diagram / Signal flow graph |

Different kitchen layouts (structures) can produce the *same dish* but differ in time, energy, and number of helpers (multipliers/adders) used. This is why the study of structures is vital for efficient VLSI / embedded DSP implementation.

## 1.3 Classification of Filter Structures (FIR Focus)

> [!NOTE]
> **FIR Filter Definition:** A Finite Impulse Response (FIR) filter has a transfer function of the form 
> $$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) z^{-k}$$
> where $N$ is the filter length (number of taps). Because there is **no feedback** (no poles except at the origin), the output depends *only* on present and past input samples:
> $$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) \, x(n-k)$$

The **linear (non-recursive) FIR structures** covered in this module are:
1. **Direct Form** (convolution / transversal structure)
2. **Linear-Phase Direct Form** (exploiting coefficient symmetry)
3. **Cascade Form** (product of 2nd-order sections)
4. **Frequency-Sampling Structure**
5. **Lattice Structure**

All of these are called *linear structures* because they involve **no feedback loops** — the signal flows strictly in one direction (input → output) without returning to earlier nodes.

## 1.4 Basic Building Blocks

Every realization is built from only **three elementary components**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Canonical Building Blocks of DSP Structures:**
> 1. **Adder (Summing Junction):** $y = x_1 + x_2$ — combines two or more signal branches.
> 2. **Multiplier (Gain Block):** $y = a \cdot x$ — scales a signal by a constant coefficient $a$.
> 3. **Unit Delay Element ($z^{-1}$):** $y(n) = x(n-1)$ — stores the previous input sample for one clock cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> A **Signal Flow Graph (SFG)** is simply the mathematical version of a block diagram. It uses **nodes** (representing signals/summation points) connected by **directed branches** (representing multipliers whose value is the *branch transmittance*). It is preferred in theoretical analysis because it follows strict linear-graph rules (Mason's Gain Formula can be applied).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The FIR Difference Equation and Transfer Function

Starting from the standard convolution sum of an $N$-tap FIR filter:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) \, x(n-k) = h(0)x(n) + h(1)x(n-1) + \dots + h(N-1)x(n-N+1)$$

Taking the $z$-transform of both sides (using the time-shift property $\mathcal{Z}\{x(n-k)\} = z^{-k}X(z)$) gives the transfer function:

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) \, z^{-k} = h(0) + h(1)z^{-1} + h(2)z^{-2} + \dots + h(N-1)z^{-(N-1)}$$

Because $H(z)$ is a polynomial in $z^{-1}$ with **no denominator** (all poles are at the origin $z=0$), the FIR system is **always Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable**.

## 2.2 Structure 1 — Direct Form FIR (Transversal / Tapped-Delay-Line)

This is the **most straightforward realization** — it implements the convolution sum literally.

**Operational Logic (How it works):**
- The input $x(n)$ enters a chain of $N-1$ unit delays.
- Each tap of the delay line is multiplied by the corresponding coefficient $h(k)$.
- All $N$ products are summed in a single adder to produce $y(n)$.

**Why this works:** Each delayed signal $x(n-k)$ at the $k$-th tap is multiplied by $h(k)$ and added, directly realizing the sum $\sum h(k) x(n-k)$.

**Number of components for an $N$-tap Direct Form FIR:**
- Delays: $N-1$
- Multipliers: $N$ (one per coefficient)
- Adders: $N-1$ (an $N$-input summer can be built from $N-1$ two-input adders)

## 2.3 Structure 2 — Linear-Phase Direct Form FIR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetry Property:** For most practical FIR filters (Type I and Type II), the impulse response satisfies the symmetry $h(k) = \pm h(N-1-k)$. This symmetry can be **exploited** to nearly halve the number of multipliers.

For a symmetric $N$-tap linear-phase FIR, pair the terms:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)x(n-k) = \sum_{k=0}^{N/2-1} h(k)\bigl[x(n-k) \pm x(n-N+1+k)\bigr]$$

**Why it saves hardware:** Each pair of multiplications $h(k) \cdot x(n-k)$ and $h(N-1-k) \cdot x(n-N+1+k)$ becomes **one** multiplication (after pre-adding the two input samples). This reduces multipliers from $N$ to roughly $N/2$.

## 2.4 Structure 3 — Cascade Form FIR

Factor the polynomial $H(z)$ into a product of 2nd-order (and possibly one 1st-order) sections:

$$H(z) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} H_k(z), \qquad H_k(z) = b_{0k} + b_{1k}z^{-1} + b_{2k}z^{-2}$$

where $K = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor$. Each 2nd-order section is implemented in Direct Form, and the sections are connected in series (cascade).

**Why cascade?** It provides better numerical stability, allows easier coefficient sensitivity control, and enables modular design (sections can be designed/quantized independently).

## 2.5 Structure 4 — Lattice FIR Filter

The lattice structure is built from a **cascade of identical "lattice stages"**, each parameterized by a **reflection coefficient** $k_m$ (with $|k_m| < 1$ for stability).

For an $M$-stage lattice FIR, the recursive relations are:

$$f_{M}(n) = x(n), \qquad f_{m-1}(n) = f_m(n) + k_m \, g_{m-1}(n-1)$$

$$g_m(n) = k_m \, f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)$$

$$y(n) = f_0(n) = g_0(n)$$

**Why lattice?** It is **inherently modular** (one stage per coefficient), has excellent numerical properties, and is the standard structure for **speech processing** (Linear Predictive Coding), **adaptive filters**, and **wavelet decomposition**.

## 2.6 Block Diagram ↔ Signal Flow Graph Equivalence

> [!NOTE]
> A block diagram and its signal flow graph carry **exactly the same information**. The conversion rules are:
> - A **sum block (adder)** → a node with **multiple incoming branches**.
> - A **multiplier block (gain $a$)** → a directed branch with **transmittance $a$**.
> - A **delay block ($z^{-1}$)** → a directed branch with **transmittance $z^{-1}$** (or, in the time-domain, $z^{-1}$ is treated as an edge labeled with the shift operator).

The **Signal Flow Graph (SFG)** is preferred by theorists because:
- It allows application of **Mason's Gain Formula** for finding transfer functions.
- It reveals **delay-free loops** (which would make the system unrealizable).
- It is compact and easily written down for large systems.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Quantity** | **Expression** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| FIR difference equation | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,x(n-k)$ | Output is weighted sum of past inputs |
| FIR transfer function | $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,z^{-k}$ | Polynomial in $z^{-1}$, no poles (except $z=0$) |
| Number of delays (Direct Form) | $N-1$ | One per memory slot |
| Number of multipliers (Direct Form) | $N$ | One per non-zero coefficient |
| Number of multipliers (Linear-Phase) | $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor$ | Symmetry exploited |
| 2nd-order FIR section | $H_k(z) = b_{0k} + b_{1k}z^{-1} + b_{2k}z^{-2}$ | Cascade building block |
| Number of cascade sections | $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor$ | For real coefficients |
| Lattice order-recursion | $f_m(n) = f_{m+1}(n) + k_{m+1}\,g_m(n-1)$ | Forward error |
| Lattice order-recursion | $g_{m+1}(n) = k_{m+1}\,f_m(n) + g_m(n-1)$ | Backward error |
| Unit delay transmittance | $z^{-1}$ | In SFG, this is a branch label |
| Mason's Gain Formula | $T = \frac{1}{\Delta}\sum_k P_k \Delta_k$ | Used to find $H(z)$ from SFG |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application** | **Preferred FIR Structure** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| Audio equalizers, music DSP | Linear-Phase Direct Form | Exact linear phase; no phase distortion |
| Multi-rate filtering (decimators/interpolators) | Polyphase / Cascade | Computational efficiency |
| Speech coding (LPC analysis) | Lattice Form | Stable reflection coefficients; modular |
| Narrowband filtering (sharp roll-off) | Cascade of 2nd-order sections | Low coefficient sensitivity |
| Adaptive echo cancellers | Lattice / Transversal | LMS algorithm works on $h(k)$ taps directly |
| FPGA hardware implementation | Direct Form (transposed) | Pipelineable, no long critical path |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Direct Form FIR from the Difference Equation

**Starting point:** The $N$-tap FIR convolution sum.

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,x(n-k)$$

**Step 1 — Expand the sum explicitly for $N=4$ (illustrative case).**

$$y(n) = h(0)x(n) + h(1)x(n-1) + h(2)x(n-2) + h(3)x(n-3)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the delay line.** The signal $x(n)$ entering a cascade of unit delays produces:
- After 1 delay: $x(n-1)$
- After 2 delays: $x(n-2)$
- After 3 delays: $x(n-3)$

**Step 3 — Multiply each tap by its coefficient.** Each of the four signals is scaled by its corresponding $h(k)$:
- $h(0) \cdot x(n)$, $h(1) \cdot x(n-1)$, $h(2) \cdot x(n-2)$, $h(3) \cdot x(n-3)$

**Step 4 — Sum all products in a single adder.** The adder output is exactly $y(n)$.

$$\boxed{y(n) = h(0)x(n) + h(1)x(n-1) + h(2)x(n-2) + h(3)x(n-3)}$$

This completes the realization. The number of delays required is $N-1 = 3$, the number of multipliers is $N = 4$, and the number of additions is $N-1 = 3$.

## 3.2 Derivation B — Transfer Function from the Difference Equation

**Starting point:** $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) x(n-k)$.

**Step 1 — Apply the $z$-transform to both sides.** Using linearity:

$$Y(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,\mathcal{Z}\{x(n-k)\}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the time-shift property** $\mathcal{Z}\{x(n-k)\} = z^{-k} X(z)$:

$$Y(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,z^{-k} X(z)$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $X(z)$:**

$$Y(z) = \left[\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) z^{-k}\right] X(z)$$

**Step 4 — Form the transfer function $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$:**

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) z^{-k} = h(0) + h(1)z^{-1} + h(2)z^{-2} + \dots + h(N-1)z^{-(N-1)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation C — Linear-Phase Reduction of Multipliers

**Starting point:** Symmetric FIR with $N$ taps, satisfying $h(k) = h(N-1-k)$.

**Step 1 — Pair the symmetric terms.** For $N$ even and pairing $k$ with $N-1-k$:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) x(n-k) = \sum_{k=0}^{N/2-1} h(k)\bigl[x(n-k) + x(n-N+1+k)\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Define the pre-summed signal** $s_k(n) = x(n-k) + x(n-N+1+k)$.

**Step 3 — Each pair requires only ONE multiplication** $h(k) \cdot s_k(n)$, instead of two.

**Step 4 — Count the savings:**

$$\text{Multipliers} = N/2 \quad (\text{instead of } N)$$

$$\text{Additions} = N/2 + (N-1) \quad (\text{N/2 pre-adds + final accumulation})$$

This is why linear-phase FIR filters are nearly **50% cheaper** to implement than the generic Direct Form.

## 3.4 Derivation D — Cascade Form Factorization

**Starting point:** $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) z^{-k}$, an $(N-1)$-degree polynomial in $z^{-1}$.

**Step 1 — Find the roots.** Let the roots of $H(z)$ be $z_1, z_2, \dots, z_{N-1}$ (complex, occurring in conjugate pairs for real $h(k)$).

$$H(z) = h(0)\left[1 - z_1 z^{-1}\right]\left[1 - z_2 z^{-1}\right] \cdots \left[1 - z_{N-1} z^{-1}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Group conjugate pairs into 2nd-order sections** (using $N$ even for simplicity):

$$H(z) = h(0) \prod_{k=1}^{N/2} \left[b_{0k} + b_{1k} z^{-1} + b_{2k} z^{-2}\right]$$

where

$$b_{0k} = 1, \qquad b_{1k} = -(z_k + z_k^*), \qquad b_{2k} = \vert z_k \vert^2$$

**Step 3 — Cascade the sections:** The output of section $k$ feeds section $k+1$:

$$Y(z) = H_1(z)\,H_2(z)\,\dots\,H_{N/2}(z)\,X(z)$$

**Step 4 — Practical note:** Pairing roots (pole-zero) such that each section has a well-conditioned polynomial (avoiding clustered roots in the same section) **minimizes coefficient sensitivity**.

## 3.5 Derivation E — Lattice FIR Recursion

**Starting point:** Define the **forward** and **backward** prediction-error sequences $f_m(n)$ and $g_m(n)$ at stage $m$.

**Stage $M$ (input stage):**
$$f_M(n) = g_M(n) = x(n)$$

**Stage-order recursion (going from stage $m+1$ down to stage $m$):**

$$f_m(n) = f_{m+1}(n) + k_{m+1} \, g_m(n-1)$$

$$g_{m+1}(n) = k_{m+1} \, f_m(n) + g_m(n-1)$$

**Output stage (stage 0):**
$$y(n) = f_0(n)$$

**Derivation justification:** These two equations are the *Levinson-Durbin* recursions translated into time-domain filtering. The reflection coefficient $k_{m+1}$ is the same coefficient that appears in the equivalent FIR polynomial.

**Conversion of lattice → Direct Form coefficients:**
The Levinson-Durbin recursion gives:

$$h_m(k) = h_{m+1}(k) + k_{m+1}\,h_{m+1}(m-k), \qquad 0 \le k \le m$$

which uniquely maps the lattice coefficients $\{k_1, k_2, \dots, k_M\}$ to the Direct Form coefficients $\{h(0), h(1), \dots, h(M)\}$.

## 3.6 Derivation F — Block Diagram to Signal Flow Graph Conversion

A **multiplier-by-$a$ block** becomes a directed branch with label $a$.

A **delay block** becomes a directed branch with label $z^{-1}$.

A **two-input adder** becomes a **node** (junction) with two incoming branches.

**Mason's Gain Formula** for a single-input/single-output SFG:

$$T = \frac{1}{\Delta}\sum_{k} P_k \Delta_k$$

where
- $P_k$ = product of branch transmittances along the $k$-th forward path
- $\Delta$ = $1 - \sum(\text{individual loop gains}) + \sum(\text{products of 2 non-touching loops}) - \dots$
- $\Delta_k$ = the determinant $\Delta$ evaluated with the loops touching path $k$ removed

For FIR structures, there are **no feedback loops** (no $z^{+1}$ branches), so $\Delta = 1$ and Mason's formula reduces to $T = \sum P_k$, which is just a sum of forward path gains — consistent with the all-zero polynomial $H(z)$.

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — Direct Form Realization of a 5-Tap FIR

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** $h = \{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\}$ (a symmetric 5-tap FIR). **Find:** Realize using Direct Form, then verify the transfer function.

**Step 1 — Write the difference equation.**

$$y(n) = 1\cdot x(n) + 2\cdot x(n-1) + 3\cdot x(n-2) + 2\cdot x(n-3) + 1\cdot x(n-4)$$

**Step 2 — Identify hardware counts.**

- Delays: $N-1 = 4$
- Multipliers: $N = 5$
- Adders: $N-1 = 4$

**Step 3 — Write the transfer function.**

$$H(z) = 1 + 2z^{-1} + 3z^{-2} + 2z^{-3} + z^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the linear-phase trick.** Since $h(k) = h(N-1-k)$, we can reduce multipliers to $\lceil N/2 \rceil = 3$:

$$y(n) = 1\cdot[x(n)+x(n-4)] + 2\cdot[x(n-1)+x(n-3)] + 3\cdot x(n-2)$$

Hardware savings: **5 multipliers → 3 multipliers** (40% reduction).

**Step 5 — Verify symmetry in the frequency response.** $H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j2\omega} \cdot [3 + 4\cos(\omega) + 2\cos(2\omega)]$ — the term in brackets is purely real, confirming **linear phase** with group delay $= 2$ samples.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Direct Form FIR (Block Diagram) — 4-Tap Example

```mermaid
graph LR
    inNode["x(n) INPUT"] --> dly1["z^-1"]
    dly1 --> dly2["z^-1"]
    dly2 --> dly3["z^-1"]
    dly3 --> dly4["z^-1"]

    inNode --> m0["h(0)"]
    dly1 --> m1["h(1)"]
    dly2 --> m2["h(2)"]
    dly3 --> m3["h(3)"]

    m0 --> adder["SUMMER +"]
    m1 --> adder
    m2 --> adder
    m3 --> adder

    adder --> outNode["y(n) OUTPUT"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The top row is the **tapped delay line** (4 delays → 5 taps available).
- The middle row contains the **5 multipliers** (only 4 needed if we use the input as the 0th tap).
- The bottom row contains the **adder** that sums all multiplier outputs.

## 4.2 Signal Flow Graph of a 4-Tap Direct Form FIR

```mermaid
graph LR
    n0["n0 x n"] --> n1["n1 x n-1"]
    n0 --> a0["br h(0)"]
    n1 --> a0
    n1 --> n2["n2 x n-2"]
    n0 --> a1["br h(1)"]
    n2 --> a1
    n2 --> n3["n3 x n-3"]
    n0 --> a2["br h(2)"]
    n3 --> a2
    n3 --> a4["n4 x n-4"]
    n0 --> a3["br h(3)"]
    n4 --> a3
    a0 --> outN["nOut y n"]
    a1 --> outN
    a2 --> outN
    a3 --> outN
    n0 --> n1
    n1 --> n2
    n2 --> n3
    n3 --> n4
```

**Reading the SFG:**
- Each node represents a signal value.
- Each directed edge carries a transmittance (the branch coefficient or $z^{-1}$).
- The five forward paths from input to output correspond to the five terms in $H(z)$.

## 4.3 Cascade Form FIR — Two 2nd-Order Sections

```mermaid
graph LR
    inC["x(n)"] --> s1["SECTION 1 b01 plus b11 z^-1 plus b21 z^-2"]
    s1 --> d11["z^-1"]
    d11 --> d12["z^-1"]
    s1 --> s2["SECTION 2 b02 plus b12 z^-1 plus b22 z^-2"]
    s2 --> d21["z^-1"]
    d21 --> d22["z^-1"]
    s2 --> outC["y(n)"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The output of Section 1 is fed as input to Section 2. Each section is itself a small Direct Form structure with 2 multipliers and 1 delay (or 2 multipliers and 2 delays depending on whether $b_{0k}$ is implemented as a gain or absorbed).

## 4.4 Lattice FIR Filter (3-Stage Lattice)

```mermaid
graph LR
    xIn["x(n)"] --> fm3["f3 n"]
    xIn --> gm3["g3 n"]
    fm3 --> s3a["SUM +"]
    gm3 --> dlyG2["z^-1 g2 n-1"]
    dlyG2 --> s3a
    s3a --> fm2["f2 n"]
    fm2 --> mulF["k3 times f2 n"]
    mulF --> s3b["SUM +"]
    dlyG2 --> s3b
    s3b --> gm2out["g2 n OUTPUT STAGE"]
    fm2 --> dlyF2["z^-1 f1 n-1"]
    dlyF2 --> s2a["SUM +"]
    gm2out --> s2a
    s2a --> fm1["f1 n"]
    fm1 --> mulF1["k2 times f1 n"]
    mulF1 --> s2b["SUM +"]
    dlyF2 --> s2b
    s2b --> gm1out["g1 n"]
    fm1 --> dlyF1["z^-1 f0 n-1"]
    dlyF1 --> s1a["SUM +"]
    gm1out --> s1a
    s1a --> fm0["f0 n equals y n OUTPUT"]
    fm0 --> mulF0["k1 times f0 n"]
    mulF0 --> s1b["SUM +"]
    dlyF1 --> s1b
    s1b --> gm0out["g0 n"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The structure is built from **3 identical lattice stages**, each containing:
  - One multiplier by the reflection coefficient $k_m$
  - Two adders (forward and backward paths)
  - One shared delay element
- The input $x(n)$ enters at the top; the output $y(n) = f_0(n)$ is taken from the bottom of the forward chain.

## 4.5 Linear-Phase Direct Form (Symmetry Exploited)

```mermaid
graph LR
    lpIn["x(n)"] --> lpd1["z^-1"]
    lpd1 --> lpd2["z^-1"]
    lpd2 --> lpd3["z^-1"]
    lpd3 --> lpd4["z^-1"]

    lpIn --> lpAddA["SUM x(n)+x(n-4)"]
    lpd4 --> lpAddA
    lpd1 --> lpAddB["SUM x(n-1)+x(n-3)"]
    lpd3 --> lpAddB

    lpAddA --> lpM0["h(0)"]
    lpAddB --> lpM1["h(1)"]
    lpd2 --> lpM2["h(2)"]

    lpM0 --> lpFinal["SUMMER +"]
    lpM1 --> lpFinal
    lpM2 --> lpFinal

    lpFinal --> lpOut["y(n)"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The pre-adders (`SUM x(n)+x(n-4)` and `SUM x(n-1)+x(n-3)`) exploit the symmetry $h(0)=h(4)$ and $h(1)=h(3)$, reducing **5 multipliers → 3 multipliers** with only 2 extra adders. The savings grow with $N$ and are critical in VLSI/FPGA implementations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 marks each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define a realization structure for a discrete-time system. Why are multiple realization structures possible for the same transfer function?**

**Model Answer:**
A realization structure of a discrete-time system is a block diagram (or signal flow graph) that specifies the interconnections of unit-delay elements, multipliers, and adders required to compute the output $y(n)$ from the input $x(n)$, in accordance with the system's difference equation.

A single transfer function $H(z)$ can be realized in multiple ways because $H(z)$ is only an *input-output relation*, not a unique blueprint. Different internal arrangements (Direct Form, Cascade, Lattice, etc.) can produce the same $H(z)$ while differing in:
- Number of delays and arithmetic operations
- Sensitivity to coefficient quantization
- Suitability for hardware / software / pipelined implementation

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List the basic building blocks used in the block diagram representation of a discrete-time system. Draw the symbol of a unit delay element and write its input-output relation.**

**Model Answer:**
The three basic building blocks are:
1. **Adder (Summing Junction):** $y = x_1 + x_2$
2. **Multiplier (Gain Block):** $y = a \cdot x$
3. **Unit Delay Element ($z^{-1}$):** $y(n) = x(n-1)$

The unit delay element is drawn as a rectangular block with the symbol $z^{-1}$ inside (or simply a box labeled $D$ in time-domain notation).

**Input-output relation:** $y(n) = x(n-1)$, or equivalently in the $z$-domain: $Y(z) = z^{-1} X(z)$.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice)

### Question A. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** (14 Marks)

**For an FIR filter with impulse response $h(n) = \{2, 3, 1, 4, 3, 2\}$ (6-tap):**

**(a)** Realize the filter using the **Direct Form** structure. Label all delay elements, multipliers, and adders. Determine the number of delays, multipliers, and adders required. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Draw the **Signal Flow Graph (SFG)** equivalent of the Direct Form structure and verify the transfer function using Mason's Gain Formula. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Direct Form Realization

**Step 1 — Write the difference equation:**

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{5} h(k) x(n-k) = 2x(n) + 3x(n-1) + 1x(n-2) + 4x(n-3) + 3x(n-4) + 2x(n-5)$$

**Step 2 — Identify hardware count:**

- **Number of delays:** $N - 1 = 6 - 1 = 5$
- **Number of multipliers:** $N = 6$ (one per coefficient)
- **Number of two-input adders:** $N - 1 = 5$ (to combine 6 products)

**Step 3 — Draw the block diagram:**

The block diagram consists of a tapped delay line of 5 unit delays at the top, 6 multipliers (each labeled with the corresponding $h(k)$), and a 6-input summing adder at the bottom feeding the output $y(n)$.

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| 5 cascaded $z^{-1}$ blocks | Generate $x(n-1), x(n-2), \dots, x(n-5)$ |
| 6 multipliers | Scale each tap by $h(0), h(1), \dots, h(5)$ |
| Summer | Sum the 6 scaled products to give $y(n)$ |

**Hardware summary table:**

| Component | Quantity |
|---|---|
| Unit Delays | **5** |
| Multipliers | **6** |
| Two-input Adders | **5** |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Signal Flow Graph and Mason's Verification

**Step 1 — Draw the SFG.** The SFG contains:
- 6 nodes $n_0, n_1, \dots, n_5$ for the signals $x(n), x(n-1), \dots, x(n-5)$
- 5 directed edges (each labeled $z^{-1}$) connecting consecutive nodes
- 6 branches from the nodes to the output node, each carrying transmittance $h(k)$

**Step 2 — Identify all forward paths** from input node $n_0$ to output node $n_{\text{out}}$:

| Path $k$ | Nodes traversed | Path gain $P_k$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n_0 \to n_{\text{out}}$ | $h(0) = 2$ |
| 2 | $n_0 \to n_1 \to n_{\text{out}}$ | $z^{-1} \cdot h(1) = 3z^{-1}$ |
| 3 | $n_0 \to n_1 \to n_2 \to n_{\text{out}}$ | $z^{-2} \cdot h(2) = z^{-2}$ |
| 4 | $n_0 \to n_1 \to n_2 \to n_3 \to n_{\text{out}}$ | $z^{-3} \cdot h(3) = 4z^{-3}$ |
| 5 | $n_0 \to n_1 \to n_2 \to n_3 \to n_4 \to n_{\text{out}}$ | $z^{-4} \cdot h(4) = 3z^{-4}$ |
| 6 | $n_0 \to n_1 \to n_2 \to n_3 \to n_4 \to n_5 \to n_{\text{out}}$ | $z^{-5} \cdot h(5) = 2z^{-5}$ |

**Step 3 — Apply Mason's Gain Formula.** Since the FIR SFG has **no feedback loops**, the graph determinant is

$$\Delta = 1 - 0 = 1$$

Each path $P_k$ touches **all** other paths (they all share the $z^{-1}$ delay chain), so $\Delta_k = 1$ for all $k$.

**Step 4 — Sum the path gains:**

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{5} P_k = 2 + 3z^{-1} + z^{-2} + 4z^{-3} + 3z^{-4} + 2z^{-5}$$

This matches the direct substitution into $H(z) = \sum h(k) z^{-k}$. **Verification successful.**

---

### Question B. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** (14 Marks)

**Consider the FIR filter described by the transfer function:**
$$H(z) = (1 + 0.5z^{-1} + z^{-2})(1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.12z^{-2})$$

**(a)** Realize this filter using the **Cascade Form** with two 2nd-order Direct Form sections. Draw the block diagram and identify the section coefficients. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages of the cascade realization** over the direct form, with particular reference to coefficient sensitivity and modularity. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Cascade Form Realization

**Step 1 — Identify the two sections.**

Section 1:
$$H_1(z) = 1 + 0.5z^{-1} + z^{-2}$$
$$b_{01} = 1, \quad b_{11} = 0.5, \quad b_{21} = 1$$

Section 2:
$$H_2(z) = 1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.12z^{-2}$$
$$b_{02} = 1, \quad b_{12} = -0.7, \quad b_{22} = 0.12$$

**Step 2 — Intermediate signal.** Let $w(n)$ be the output of Section 1 (input to Section 2):

$$w(n) = x(n) + 0.5x(n-1) + x(n-2)$$

**Step 3 — Final output.**

$$y(n) = w(n) - 0.7w(n-1) + 0.12w(n-2)$$

**Step 4 — Total hardware count.**

| Component | Per Section | Two Sections | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| Delays | 2 | 2 × 2 | **4** |
| Multipliers | 3 | 2 × 3 | **6** |
| Adders | 2 | 2 × 2 | **4** |

**Step 5 — Block diagram structure:**
- **Section 1** (top): tapped delay line with 2 delays; multipliers by $\{1, 0.5, 1\}$; summer giving $w(n)$.
- **Section 2** (bottom): tapped delay line on $w(n)$ with 2 delays; multipliers by $\{1, -0.7, 0.12\}$; summer giving $y(n)$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Advantages of Cascade Form

**1. Reduced Coefficient Sensitivity (Numerical Robustness):**
In a high-order Direct Form filter ($N \ge 8$), all coefficients appear inside a single high-degree polynomial. A small change (quantization) in one coefficient causes large changes in the roots (especially clustered roots). In the cascade form, each 2nd-order section is a low-degree polynomial whose roots are far less sensitive — quantization of one section's coefficients affects only that section's poles/zeros.

**2. Modularity and Independent Design:**
Each 2nd-order section can be designed, optimized, and quantized independently. Sections can be reordered to minimize overflow, and individual sections can be reused (e.g., in adaptive filters) without redesigning the whole system.

**3. Easier Pole-Zero Pairing Control:**
Roots of $H(z)$ that are close together (which cause sharp peaks or notches in the frequency response) can be kept in the **same** 2nd-order section, avoiding catastrophic cancellation. Roots that are far apart can be placed in **different** sections.

**4. Scalability and Hardware Reuse:**
A library of optimized 2nd-order section modules can be built once and cascaded as needed. This is the basis of modern IIR/FIR filter chips and IP cores.

**5. Lower Probability of Overflow:**
Intermediate signals in each section have bounded growth, so overflow in fixed-point arithmetic is less catastrophic than in Direct Form.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Cascade Form:**
> Many students **incorrectly add** the total number of delays as $2 + 2 = 4$ but **forget that the second section's delay line operates on $w(n)$, not on $x(n)$**. The blocks are separate but the **intermediate signal is $w(n)$**, not the original $x(n)$. Always draw and label the intermediate signal explicitly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's General Warning — Common Marks-Loss Points in This Module:**
> 1. **Confusing Direct Form with Transposed Form.** The transposed form has the same $H(z)$ but a different block diagram. Marks are lost if a student draws one and labels it the other.
> 2. **Forgetting that an $N$-tap FIR needs $N-1$ delays, not $N$.** The input $x(n)$ is already "available"; only the *previous* $N-1$ samples need to be stored.
> 3. **Drawing feedback loops in an FIR structure.** FIR filters have **no feedback**; if your diagram contains a loop going backward, you have drawn an IIR structure by mistake.
> 4. **Mixing up Linear-Phase symmetry and Anti-symmetry.** Type I/II are symmetric; Type III/IV are antisymmetric. The pre-add signs differ.
> 5. **Omitting the intermediate signal $w(n)$ in cascade form.** The marker expects to see $w(n)$ clearly labeled between sections.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Realization = Block Diagram or Signal Flow Graph** that implements a difference equation using delays, multipliers, and adders.
- **FIR filter:** $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) x(n-k)$, transfer function $H(z) = \sum h(k) z^{-k}$ — a polynomial in $z^{-1}$ with **no denominator** (no finite poles).
- **No feedback** in any FIR structure — all paths flow strictly from input to output.
- **Direct Form (Transversal):** $N-1$ delays, $N$ multipliers, $N-1$ adders. Most direct realization of the convolution sum.
- **Linear-Phase Direct Form:** Exploits $h(k) = \pm h(N-1-k)$; reduces multipliers from $N$ to $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor$.
- **Cascade Form:** $H(z) = \prod H_k(z)$ with each $H_k$ a 2nd-order section. Better coefficient sensitivity, modular, scalable.
- **Lattice Form:** Built from reflection coefficients $\{k_m\}$; modular, numerically robust, used in speech (LPC) and adaptive filtering.
- **Signal Flow Graph (SFG):** Mathematical version of block diagram — uses nodes and directed branches with transmittances. Use **Mason's Gain Formula** to extract $H(z)$.
- **Mason's Gain Formula for FIR:** Since there are no loops, $\Delta = 1$ and $H(z) = \sum P_k$ (sum of forward-path gains).
- **Three basic building blocks:** Adder $\Sigma$, Multiplier $\times a$, Delay $z^{-1}$.
- **Hardware-count formulas:** Delays $= N-1$, Multipliers $= N$ (Direct), $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor$ (Linear-Phase), $3\lfloor N/2 \rfloor$ (Cascade).
- **Symmetry property saves ~50% multipliers** in linear-phase FIR — *always exploit it* in KTU numerical problems.
- **FIR filters are always stable** because all poles are at $z=0$ (inside the unit circle).
- **Cascade section ordering matters:** Pair "close" roots together to minimize numerical sensitivity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Form; IIR Filter Structures - Direct Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT504 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Direct Form; IIR Filter Structures - Direct Form

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) filter** is a discrete-time linear shift-invariant system whose impulse response extends over an **infinite** number of samples because it contains feedback (recursive) paths. The general **N-th order IIR difference equation** is:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y(n-k) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x(n-k), \quad a_0 = 1$$

The corresponding **system function (transfer function)** in the z-domain is:

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}} = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}$$

A **Direct Form realization** is a block-diagram implementation that **directly maps** the I/O difference equation (or its z-domain transfer function) into a flow graph, *without* performing any factorization, partial-fraction expansion, or cascade/parallel decomposition. The two canonical direct realizations are:

| Form | Name | Key Property |
|------|------|--------------|
| **DF-I** | Direct Form I | Direct implementation of difference equation — feedforward block followed by feedback block |
| **DF-II** | Direct Form II (Canonical) | Minimum number of delay elements (N for N-th order) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The term "Direct Form" in the syllabus refers specifically to **Direct Form I and Direct Form II**. Students must derive both from the difference equation and identify the canonical (minimum-delay) structure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Two-Tank Water System

Imagine two connected water tanks used to filter sediment from inflow water:

- **Tank A (Feedforward / Non-Recursive part)** — Pure sieve action. Water flows in, passes through a fine mesh (computed from past inputs only), and exits. The output depends *only* on current and past **inputs** — it has finite memory, no feedback.

- **Tank B (Feedback / Recursive part)** — Recycles water. Its output flows back to influence itself (controlled by a recirculation pipe representing the $a_k$ coefficients). The output here depends on past **outputs** — infinite memory in principle.

- **Direct Form I** simply connects Tank A's output as Tank B's input — two separate, sequential filter blocks, requiring a **lot of storage** (delays).

- **Direct Form II** is the smart plumber: he notices that the delay between Tank A's intermediate storage and Tank B's input is *redundant* — he merges the two delay lines into **one shared storage line**, halving the memory requirement. The math is identical, but the hardware is half the size.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Summary:** Direct Form structures translate the algebraic polynomial equation of the filter into a hardware flow graph. Direct Form II is the *canonical* (most economical) realization because it shares delay elements between the pole-producing and zero-producing sections.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Order N of an IIR filter** — the maximum of {M, N} where M, N are the orders of the numerator and denominator polynomials of $H(z)$. It determines stability, frequency response sharpness, and computational cost.
- **Multiplications per output sample (MPS)** — Direct Form I: $(M+N+1)$; Direct Form II: $(M+N+1)$ (same computational cost, but fewer delays in DF-II).
- **Storage elements (Delays)** — Direct Form I: $(M+N)$; Direct Form II: $\max(M,N) = N$ (for $M \le N$). This is why DF-II is called *canonical*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Building the Direct Form

### 2.1.1 Starting Point: The N-th Order I/O Equation

Every direct-form realization begins from the same algebraic starting point — the linear constant-coefficient difference equation (LCCDE):

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x(n-k) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y(n-k)$$

The RHS has two distinct groups:
1. **Non-recursive group (Zeros)** — $\sum b_k x(n-k)$: depends on inputs only.
2. **Recursive group (Poles)** — $-\sum a_k y(n-k)$: depends on past outputs.

**Why split them?** Each group corresponds to a *single* rational polynomial in $z^{-1}$, namely the numerator $B(z)$ and denominator $A(z)$. Drawing them as **separate** building blocks makes the diagram easier to construct.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Construction Logic

| Step | Action | Reason |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Take z-transform of the LCCDE | Convert difference equation to algebraic rational function $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$ |
| 2 | Recognize $H(z) = B(z) \cdot \frac{1}{A(z)}$ (cascade of zeros then poles) | Allows sequential block-by-block drawing |
| 3 | Implement $\frac{1}{A(z)}$ as a feedback network with $a_k$ coefficients | Feedback produces poles |
| 4 | Implement $B(z)$ as a feedforward network with $b_k$ coefficients | Feedforward produces zeros |
| 5 | Choose order of cascade | DF-I: poles first then zeros; DF-II: zeros first then poles (but delays are merged) |

## 2.2 Direct Form I (DF-I) — Non-Canonical Realization

Direct Form I realizes $H(z) = \frac{1}{A(z)} \cdot B(z)$ literally: **poles block first, then zeros block**. Let the intermediate signal be $w(n)$ such that $W(z) = \frac{X(z)}{A(z)}$.

The two sub-equations are:

$$w(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, w(n-k) \quad \text{(feedback / poles block)}$$

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, w(n-k) \quad \text{(feedforward / zeros block)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structural property of DF-I:** Total delay elements = $M + N$. The two delay lines are *physically separate*, so memory is **not** conserved.

## 2.3 Direct Form II (DF-II) — Canonical Realization

Direct Form II reverses the cascade order and exploits a **key algebraic identity**: because $H(z) = B(z) \cdot \frac{1}{A(z)} = \frac{1}{A(z)} \cdot B(z)$ (multiplication of scalar transfer functions is commutative), we may compute the **zeros first, then poles** without changing the I/O behaviour.

Define the intermediate signal $w(n)$ via the zeros-block output first:

$$w(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, w(n-k)$$

Wait — this equation actually comes from the **poles block applied to $w$**. To make the zeros block run *first*, we invert the algebra. Let $V(z) = \frac{X(z) \cdot B(z)}{1}$ and $W(z) = \frac{V(z)}{A(z)}$. Equivalently, by linearity of LTI systems:

$$w(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, w(n-k) \quad ; \quad y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, w(n-k)$$

**The crucial observation:** the same intermediate $w(n)$ appears in *both* equations. Therefore the delay elements storing $w(n), w(n-1), \dots, w(n-N)$ are **shared** between the two sub-systems.

### 2.3.1 Why is DF-II "Canonical"?

- A canonical structure uses the **minimum possible** number of delay elements for a given transfer function.
- An N-th order IIR filter with $M \le N$ has a state vector of dimension $N$. A canonical realization needs exactly $N$ delays — no more, no less.
- DF-II achieves this minimum; DF-I uses $M + N$ delays (more than necessary).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula / Definition | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|----------|----------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | IIR Difference Equation | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x(n-k) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y(n-k)$ | Foundation of all direct-form derivations |
| 2 | System Function | $H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | Starting point for block-diagram construction |
| 3 | Split into two sub-systems | $H(z) = B(z) \cdot \frac{1}{A(z)}$ with $a_0 = 1$ | Justifies two-block direct-form drawing |
| 4 | DF-I delays required | $M + N$ | Asked in 3-mark questions |
| 5 | DF-II delays required | $\max(M, N) = N$ (for $M \le N$) | "Canonical" property — frequent theory question |
| 6 | DF-I intermediate equation | $w(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k w(n-k)$ | Required for drawing and analysis |
| 7 | DF-II intermediate equation | $w(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k w(n-k); \; y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k w(n-k)$ | Used in transposed form derivations too |
| 8 | Multiplications per sample | $(M + N + 1)$ for both DF-I and DF-II | Computational cost comparison |
| 9 | Order of filter | $N = \max(M, N)$ | Defines canonical delay count |
| 10 | Stability condition | All poles of $H(z)$ inside unit circle $\vert z \vert < 1$ | Independent of realization; same for DF-I and DF-II |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid-rendering note**: The vertical bar in $\vert z \vert < 1$ uses `\vert` macro so it does **not** break the markdown table column separator.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Direct-form structures are the workhorse implementation in:

- **Audio equalizers and noise-cancelling headphones** — IIR biquads in Direct Form II run on low-power DSP chips.
- **Biomedical signal processing** — ECG/EEG filters with sharp roll-off use IIR Direct Form II to minimize silicon area in implantable devices.
- **Telecom channel equalizers** — Adaptive IIR filters in DF-II topology converge faster and require less memory.
- **Control systems (digital controllers)** — PID controllers discretized as IIR filters are realized in DF-II for microcontroller implementation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical sensitivity caveat:** Direct forms (especially high-order) are sensitive to coefficient quantization. In practice, **engineers prefer cascade or parallel biquad sections** (built from 2nd-order direct forms) for production hardware — but the KTU 2024 syllabus still tests the basic direct forms thoroughly.
