## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cascade Form Block Diagram (Biquad Sections)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode["x(n)"] --> s1Mul["b01"]
    s1Mul --> s1Add["+"]
    s1Add --> s1D["z-1"]
    s1D --> s1D2["z-1"]
    s1D --> s1FB1["a11"]
    s1D2 --> s1FB2["a21"]
    s1FB1 --> s1Add
    s1FB2 --> s1Add
    s1Add --> s1Out["w1(n)"]
    s1Out --> s2Mul["b02"]
    s2Mul --> s2Add["+"]
    s2Add --> s2D["z-1"]
    s2D --> s2D2["z-1"]
    s2D --> s2FB1["a12"]
    s2D2 --> s2FB2["a22"]
    s2FB1 --> s2Add
    s2FB2 --> s2Add
    s2Add --> s2Out["w2(n)"]
    s2Out --> s3Mul["b03"]
    s3Mul --> s3Add["+"]
    s3Add --> s3D["z-1"]
    s3D --> s3D2["z-1"]
    s3D --> s3FB1["a13"]
    s3D2 --> s3FB2["a23"]
    s3FB1 --> s3Add
    s3FB2 --> s3Add
    s3Add --> outNode["y(n)"]
```

**Description:** Input $x(n)$ enters Section 1, whose output $w_1(n)$ feeds Section 2, whose output $w_2(n)$ feeds Section 3, producing $y(n)$. Each section is a Direct Form II biquad with two delays, two feed-forward paths ($b_{0k}, b_{1k}, b_{2k}$), and two feedback paths ($a_{1k}, a_{2k}$).

---

### 4.2 Parallel Form Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode["x(n)"] --> branchSplit["Common Input Bus"]
    branchSplit --> cb1["C x(n) Constant Path"]
    branchSplit --> sec1["Section 1: R1 z-1 / 1-p1 z-1"]
    branchSplit --> sec2["Section 2: R2 z-1 / 1-p2 z-1"]
    branchSplit --> sec3["Section 3: R3 z-1 / 1-p3 z-1"]
    cb1 --> sumAdd["+"]
    sec1 --> sumAdd
    sec2 --> sumAdd
    sec3 --> sumAdd
    sumAdd --> outNode["y(n)"]
```

**Description:** The same input $x(n)$ is fed to all branches. Each section $k$ computes a first-order IIR response $w_k(n)$, and all responses plus the constant path $C \cdot x(n)$ are summed at the output.

---

### 4.3 Decimation (Downsampling by $M$) System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode["x(n) at Fs"] --> lpfBlock["H_AA(z) : Anti-Aliasing Lowpass Filter, Cutoff pi/M, Gain 1"]
    lpfBlock --> filtered["v(n)"]
    filtered --> downArrow["Downsampler ↓ M, Keep 1 sample every M"]
    downArrow --> outNode["y_D(n) at Fs/M"]
```

**Description:** $x(n)$ is first low-pass filtered to remove frequency components above $\pi/M$ (preventing aliasing). The downsampler $\downarrow M$ then retains every $M$-th sample, reducing the sampling rate by a factor of $M$.

---

### 4.4 Interpolation (Upsampling by $L$) System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode["x(n) at Fs"] --> upArrow["Upsampler ↑ L, Insert L-1 Zeros between samples"]
    upArrow --> zeroIns["w(n) at L*Fs with images"]
    zeroIns --> lpfBlock["H_AI(z) : Anti-Imaging Lowpass Filter, Cutoff pi/L, Gain L"]
    lpfBlock --> outNode["y_I(n) at L*Fs, smooth"]
```

**Description:** $x(n)$ is upsampled by inserting $L-1$ zeros between samples (creating $L$ spectral images). The anti-imaging low-pass filter (with gain $L$) removes images and produces a smooth, interpolated signal at the new, higher sampling rate $L F_s$.

---

### 4.5 Combined Decimation-Interpolation Rate Changer Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode["x(n) at Fs"] --> lpfIn["H_LP(z) : Combined Lowpass, Cutoff min(pi/L, pi/M), Gain L"]
    lpfIn --> filtOut["v(n) at Fs"]
    filtOut --> upArrow["Upsampler ↑ L, Insert L-1 Zeros"]
    upArrow --> upsampled["w(n) at L*Fs"]
    upsampled --> downArrow["Downsampler ↓ M, Keep 1 of M samples"]
    downArrow --> outNode["y(n) at L*Fs/M"]
```

**Description:** A *single* low-pass filter combines both anti-aliasing and anti-imaging functions. Upsampling is done first (zero-insertion is cheap — no arithmetic), then the high-rate signal is filtered, then downsampled (discarding most samples — also cheap). This structure is computationally much more efficient than performing the two operations in reverse order.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Noble Identities** (used in efficient multirate filter design):
> - **First Noble Identity:** $H(z) \downarrow M \equiv \downarrow M \, H(z^M)$ — moving a filter to after a downsampler requires the filter to operate at the *lower* rate, with the $z$-transform argument replaced by $z^M$.
> - **Second Noble Identity:** $\uparrow L \, H(z) \equiv H(z^L) \, \uparrow L$ — moving a filter to before an upsampler allows it to operate at the *lower* rate.
>
> These identities enable the **polyphase decomposition** of filters, which is the foundation of efficient implementation of sampling-rate converters and filter banks.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Cascade Form realization. Why is it preferred over Direct Form for high-order IIR filters?**

**Model Answer:**

In Cascade Form realization, the overall transfer function $H(z)$ of an LTI system is factored into a product of second-order (biquad) or first-order sections, each implemented separately and connected in series:

$$ H(z) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} H_k(z) $$

where each $H_k(z) = \dfrac{b_{0k} + b_{1k}z^{-1} + b_{2k}z^{-2}}{1 + a_{1k}z^{-1} + a_{2k}z^{-2}}$.

It is preferred over Direct Form for high-order IIR filters because: (1) it provides **better numerical stability** by reducing the sensitivity of the overall response to coefficient quantization; (2) it allows **independent control** of each biquad's poles and zeros, enabling the designer to pair nearby poles and zeros to minimize intermediate signal overflow; (3) it **prevents large intermediate signal growth** in systems with poles near the unit circle; and (4) it is modular, simplifying design and testing.

---

**Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the time-domain and frequency-domain expressions for the interpolation of a signal $x(n)$ by a factor $L$. Mention the role of the anti-imaging filter.**

**Model Answer:**

**Time-Domain:** The upsampler $\uparrow L$ inserts $L-1$ zero-valued samples between consecutive samples of $x(n)$:

$$ y_I(n) = \begin{cases} x(n/L), & n = kL, \, k \in \mathbb{Z} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

**Frequency-Domain:** The spectrum of the upsampled signal is a frequency-compressed (by factor $L$) version of the input spectrum, repeating $L$ times over the baseband $0 \leq \omega < 2\pi$:

$$ Y_I(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{jL\omega}) $$

**Role of the Anti-Imaging Filter:** The zero-insertion creates $L-1$ unwanted spectral images (replicas of the baseband) in the $0$ to $2\pi$ range. The anti-imaging filter $H_{AI}(e^{j\omega})$ is a low-pass filter with **cutoff frequency $\omega_c = \pi/L$** and **gain $L$**. It removes these images and provides amplitude scaling, producing a smooth, properly band-limited interpolated signal at the higher sampling rate $L F_s$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Realize the following transfer function in **Cascade Form** using biquad sections. Identify each biquad's coefficients.

$$ H(z) = \frac{(1 - 0.5z^{-1})(1 + 1.5z^{-1} + 0.56z^{-2})}{(1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.12z^{-2})(1 + 0.4z^{-1})} $$

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An analog signal $x_a(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m = 4 \text{ kHz}$ is sampled at $F_s = 32 \text{ kHz}$. The signal is to be downsampled by a factor of $M = 4$. Determine:

(i) The new sampling rate.
(ii) The required anti-aliasing filter cutoff frequency in **Hz** and **radians/sample**.
(iii) The number of samples generated in 1 second after decimation.

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A (a):**

The transfer function is already in factored form. We can identify two biquad-compatible factor groups:

**Section 1 (Biquad — second order):**
$$ H_1(z) = \frac{1 + 1.5z^{-1} + 0.56z^{-2}}{1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.12z^{-2}} $$

**Coefficients:** $b_{01} = 1$, $b_{11} = 1.5$, $b_{21} = 0.56$; $a_{11} = -0.7$, $a_{21} = 0.12$.

**Section 2 (First-order section, treated as a degenerate biquad with $a_{22} = b_{22} = 0$):**
$$ H_2(z) = \frac{1 - 0.5z^{-1}}{1 + 0.4z^{-1}} $$

**Coefficients:** $b_{02} = 1$, $b_{12} = -0.5$, $b_{22} = 0$; $a_{12} = 0.4$, $a_{22} = 0$.

**Verification:**
$$ H(z) = H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z) = \frac{1 + 1.5z^{-1} + 0.56z^{-2}}{1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.12z^{-2}} \cdot \frac{1 - 0.5z^{-1}}{1 + 0.4z^{-1}} $$

Numerator: $(1 + 1.5z^{-1} + 0.56z^{-2})(1 - 0.5z^{-1}) = 1 - 0.5z^{-1} + 1.5z^{-1} - 0.75z^{-2} + 0.56z^{-2} - 0.28z^{-3} = 1 + z^{-1} - 0.19z^{-2} - 0.28z^{-3}$.

Denominator: $(1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.12z^{-2})(1 + 0.4z^{-1}) = 1 + 0.4z^{-1} - 0.7z^{-1} - 0.28z^{-2} + 0.12z^{-2} + 0.048z^{-3} = 1 - 0.3z^{-1} - 0.16z^{-2} + 0.048z^{-3}$.

Each biquad is implemented using Direct Form II structure, and the two sections are connected in series. The output of Section 1 is the input of Section 2.

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A (b):**

**Given:** $f_m = 4$ kHz, $F_s = 32$ kHz, $M = 4$.

**(i) New sampling rate:**
$$ F_s' = \frac{F_s}{M} = \frac{32 \text{ kHz}}{4} = 8 \text{ kHz} $$

**(ii) Anti-aliasing filter cutoff frequency:**

In **Hz**, the Nyquist frequency of the new (lower) sampling rate is:
$$ f_c = \frac{F_s'}{2} = \frac{8 \text{ kHz}}{2} = 4 \text{ kHz} $$

This matches the signal's maximum frequency $f_m$, so the cutoff is set to $f_c = 4$ kHz.

In **radians/sample** (normalized digital frequency):
$$ \omega_c = \frac{\pi}{M} = \frac{\pi}{4} \text{ rad/sample} $$

Verification: $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c / F_s' = 2\pi(4000)/8000 = \pi$ rad/sample? No — $\omega_c = 2\pi \cdot (f_c/F_s') = 2\pi \cdot (4000/8000) = \pi$ rad/sample. 

Wait, this contradicts $\pi/M = \pi/4$. The discrepancy arises because the signal's bandwidth in this problem *exactly* matches the new Nyquist frequency.

The correct interpretation: The **anti-aliasing filter cutoff in radians/sample is computed with respect to the original sampling rate**, but is normally expressed as $\pi/M = \pi/4$ rad/sample in the **normalized frequency scale of the original $F_s$**. Equivalently, in the new sampling rate, the cutoff is at $\pi$ rad/sample (the new Nyquist), which is the same as $4 \times \pi/4 = \pi$ in the new scale.

Practical answer: The cutoff is $f_c = 4$ kHz in Hz, and $\pi/4$ rad/sample relative to the original $F_s = 32$ kHz (or $\pi$ rad/sample relative to the new $F_s' = 8$ kHz).

**(iii) Number of samples in 1 second after decimation:**

$$ N' = \frac{F_s'}{1 \text{ second}} = 8 \text{ k samples/second} = 8000 \text{ samples} $$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** The transfer function of an IIR filter is given by:
$$ H(z) = \frac{1 + 0.5z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.6z^{-1})(1 - 0.3z^{-1})} $$

Realize this system in **Parallel Form**. Write the difference equation for each branch.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A discrete-time signal $x(n)$ has spectral support in the range $0 \leq |\omega| \leq 0.4\pi$ radians/sample. It is to be downsampled by a factor $M = 3$ and then upsampled by a factor $L = 2$. Sketch and explain the spectrum at every stage of the multirate system.

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B (a):**

**Step 1 — Partial-Fraction Expansion of $H(z)$ in the $z$-domain (positive powers):**

$$ H(z) = \frac{z^2 + 0.5z}{(z - 0.6)(z - 0.3)} $$

Since numerator and denominator are both degree 2, perform long division:

$$ H(z) = \frac{z^2 + 0.5z}{z^2 - 0.9z + 0.18} = 1 + \frac{0.5z + 0.18 - 1 + 0.9z - 0.18}{z^2 - 0.9z + 0.18} $$

Wait, let me redo: $1 \cdot (z^2 - 0.9z + 0.18) = z^2 - 0.9z + 0.18$.
Remainder: $(z^2 + 0.5z) - (z^2 - 0.9z + 0.18) = 1.4z - 0.18$.

So:
$$ H(z) = 1 + \frac{1.4z - 0.18}{(z - 0.6)(z - 0.3)} $$

**Step 2 — PFE on the proper part:**
$$ \frac{1.4z - 0.18}{(z - 0.6)(z - 0.3)} = \frac{A}{z - 0.6} + \frac{B}{z - 0.3} $$

$A = (1.4 \cdot 0.6 - 0.18)/(0.6 - 0.3) = (0.84 - 0.18)/0.3 = 0.66/0.3 = 2.2$
$B = (1.4 \cdot 0.3 - 0.18)/(0.3 - 0.6) = (0.42 - 0.18)/(-0.3) = 0.24/(-0.3) = -0.8$

Verify: $A + B = 2.2 - 0.8 = 1.4$ ✓ (coefficient of $z$); $-0.6(2.2) - 0.3(-0.8) = -1.32 + 0.24 = -1.08$, but we need $-0.18$. **Discrepancy!**

Let me recompute: $A(z-0.3) + B(z-0.6) = (A+B)z - (0.3A + 0.6B)$. So:
- $A + B = 1.4$
- $0.3A + 0.6B = 0.18$

From first: $B = 1.4 - A$. Substituting: $0.3A + 0.6(1.4 - A) = 0.18 \Rightarrow 0.3A + 0.84 - 0.6A = 0.18 \Rightarrow -0.3A = -0.66 \Rightarrow A = 2.2$. Then $B = 1.4 - 2.2 = -0.8$.

Check: $0.3(2.2) + 0.6(-0.8) = 0.66 - 0.48 = 0.18$ ✓.

**Step 3 — Express in $z^{-1}$ form:**

$$ H(z) = 1 + \frac{2.2 \, z^{-1}}{1 - 0.6 z^{-1}} + \frac{-0.8 \, z^{-1}}{1 - 0.3 z^{-1}} $$

**Step 4 — Difference equations for each branch:**

**Branch 0 (constant path):** $w_0(n) = x(n)$.

**Branch 1:** Let $W_1(z) = \frac{2.2 \, z^{-1}}{1 - 0.6 z^{-1}} X(z)$.

Difference equation: $w_1(n) = 0.6 \, w_1(n-1) + 2.2 \, x(n-1)$.

**Branch 2:** Let $W_2(z) = \frac{-0.8 \, z^{-1}}{1 - 0.3 z^{-1}} X(z)$.

Difference equation: $w_2(n) = 0.3 \, w_2(n-1) - 0.8 \, x(n-1)$.

**Output:** $y(n) = w_0(n) + w_1(n) + w_2(n) = x(n) + w_1(n) + w_2(n)$.

**Verification (for large $n$ in steady state):** The parallel structure sums the constant path and the two first-order IIR responses, which is equivalent to the original transfer function.

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B (b):**

**Given:** Signal $x(n)$ has spectrum in $|\omega| \leq 0.4\pi$ rad/sample. $M = 3$ (decimation), $L = 2$ (interpolation).

**Stage 0 — Original spectrum $X(e^{j\omega})$:**

A band-limited spectrum in $|\omega| \leq 0.4\pi$, periodic with period $2\pi$.

```
Magnitude
   1.0 |        ┌────┐        ┌────┐
       |        │    │        │    │
   0.5 |        │    │        │    │
       |________│    │________│    │____
      -2π   -0.4π  0   0.4π        2π    ω
```

**Stage 1 — Decimation by $M = 3$:**

First, **anti-aliasing LPF** with cutoff $\omega_c = \pi/3$ rad/sample.

If the input bandwidth ($0.4\pi$) is greater than $\pi/3 \approx 0.333\pi$, then the anti-aliasing filter must truncate the spectrum to $|\omega| \leq \pi/3$ to prevent aliasing. (Otherwise, the spectral images will overlap after stretching.)

Output of LPF: $V(e^{j\omega})$ — band-limited to $|\omega| \leq \pi/3$.

After downsampling: $Y_D(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{3} \sum_{k=0}^{2} V(e^{j(\omega - 2\pi k)/3})$.

**Spectrum is stretched by factor 3** (in normalized frequency scale). The original band $0.4\pi$ becomes $1.2\pi$ after stretching — but since the input was filtered to $\pi/3 \approx 0.333\pi$, after stretching the band becomes $\pi$ (filling the entire new Nyquist range). So the downsampled signal occupies the **full** new baseband.

```
Magnitude
   1.0 |   ┌────────────────────────┐
       |   │                        │
   0.5 |   │                        │
       |___│                        │___
      -π                          0    π    ω (new scale)
```

**Stage 2 — Interpolation by $L = 2$:**

Upsampler $\uparrow 2$ inserts one zero between samples. New sampling rate is $2 F_s' = 2 F_s / 3$.

Spectrum of upsampled signal: $W(e^{j\omega}) = Y_D(e^{j2\omega})$. The baseband spectrum is **compressed by 2**, and **2 replicas** appear in $0 \leq \omega < 2\pi$.

```
Magnitude
   1.0 |   ┌────┐         ┌────┐
       |   │    │         │    │
   0.5 |   │    │         │    │
       |___│    │_________│    │___
       0    π/2  π       3π/2  2π   ω
```

The two replicas are at $|\omega| \leq \pi/2$ (desired baseband) and $\pi/2 \leq |\omega| \leq \pi$ (image).

**Anti-imaging LPF** with cutoff $\omega_c = \pi/2$ rad/sample and gain $L = 2$:

Removes the image replica and scales the desired baseband by 2.

**Final output spectrum $Y(e^{j\omega})$:**

```
Magnitude
   2.0 |        ┌────┐
       |        │    │
   1.0 |        │    │
       |________│    │________
      -2π   -π/2  0   π/2     2π    ω
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls / Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the anti-aliasing filter** in decimation — aliasing destroys information irreversibly; the LPF cutoff MUST be $\pi/M$ (not $\pi$).
> 2. **Forgetting the gain factor $L$** in the anti-imaging filter during interpolation — without it, the output amplitude is scaled down by $1/L$.
> 3. **Confusing the spectral stretching direction** in decimation: the input spectrum is *stretched* (not compressed) in frequency. After decimation by $M$, each unit of $\omega$ in the output corresponds to $1/M$ units in the input.
> 4. **Pole-zero pairing incorrectly** in cascade form — always pair each pole with its *nearest* zero, not arbitrarily.
> 5. **Mixing up Cascade and Parallel forms** — remember: Cascade = product of $H_k(z)$'s, Parallel = sum of $H_k(z)$'s plus a constant.
> 6. **In Partial-Fraction Expansion**, students often forget the constant $C$ when numerator and denominator have the same degree.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3 (Cascade/Parallel/Multirate DSP):**

**Cascade Form**
- $H(z) = \prod_k H_k(z)$, where each $H_k(z)$ is a biquad (or first-order) section.
- **Pole-zero pairing:** pair each pole with its *nearest* zero.
- **Advantages:** numerical stability, modularity, lower coefficient sensitivity.
- **Implementation:** Direct Form II for each biquad; sections in series.

**Parallel Form**
- $H(z) = C + \sum_k H_k(z)$ via partial-fraction expansion.
- All sections share the same input; outputs are summed.
- **When to use:** broad-band filters, sum of resonances.
- **First-order branch difference equation:** $w_k(n) = p_k w_k(n-1) + R_k x(n-1)$.

**Decimation by $M$**
- Time: $y_D(n) = x(nM)$.
- Frequency: $Y_D(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} X(e^{j(\omega - 2\pi k)/M})$.
- **Must precede with anti-aliasing LPF** of cutoff $\pi/M$ rad/sample.
- New sampling rate: $F_s/M$.

**Interpolation by $L$**
- Time: insert $L-1$ zeros between samples.
- Frequency: $Y_I(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{jL\omega})$ (spectrum compressed by $L$, $L$ replicas in baseband).
- **Must follow with anti-imaging LPF** of cutoff $\pi/L$ rad/sample and gain $L$.
- New sampling rate: $L \cdot F_s$.

**Noble Identities** (key for efficient implementation):
- $H(z) \downarrow M \equiv \downarrow M \, H(z^M)$
- $\uparrow L \, H(z) \equiv H(z^L) \, \uparrow L$

**Sampling-Rate Conversion** (combined decimation-interpolation)
- $L/M$ ratio determines overall rate change.
- Filter cutoff: $\min(\pi/L, \pi/M)$.
- **Optimal structure:** filter → upsample → downsample (cheap operations at both ends).

**Polyphase Decomposition** (foundation of efficient filter banks):
- $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} E_k(z^M)$, where $E_k$ are polyphase components.

**Numerical Stability Tips**
- Cascade form: biquad sections with poles far from unit circle are stable even with coarse coefficient quantization.
- Parallel form: each section is independent; failure of one does not propagate.
- Direct form: avoid for orders $> 3$ due to coefficient sensitivity and overflow.

**Common Exam Favourite Topics**
- Deriving cascade realization from a given $H(z)$ (factoring polynomials).
- PFE for parallel form with complex-conjugate pole pairs.
- Sketching spectra before/after decimation or interpolation.
- Computing filter cutoffs and gain in multirate systems.
- Stating the time and frequency-domain equations correctly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anti-aliasing and Anti-imaging Filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT504 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Anti-aliasing and Anti-imaging Filter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Digital Signal Processing (DSP), the continuous-time (analog) to discrete-time (digital) conversion and vice-versa cannot be performed ideally without proper filtering. Two critical analog filters govern this process:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF):** A continuous-time **low-pass filter (LPF)** placed *before* the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) / sampler. Its purpose is to **band-limit the input analog signal** to a frequency less than half the sampling frequency, thereby preventing the phenomenon of **aliasing** — where high-frequency components masquerade as lower frequencies in the digitized signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Imaging Filter (AIF):** A continuous-time **low-pass filter (LPF)** placed *after* the Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) / reconstruction stage. Its purpose is to **smooth the stair-step output of the zero-order hold (ZOH)** and eliminate the periodic **spectral images** (replicas) centered at multiples of the sampling frequency, leaving only the baseband replica of the original signal.

Both filters are essentially **analog low-pass filters**, but they are placed at opposite ends of the digital system pipeline. They are mandatory components in any practical DSP system, including **audio codecs, CD players, digital communication receivers, biomedical instruments (ECG/EEG machines), and SDR (Software Defined Radio) systems**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Wagon Wheel Effect

Imagine you watch an old movie of a **spinning wagon wheel**. At low speeds, you clearly see the spokes rotating forward. As the wheel speeds up, the spokes appear to slow down, then **rotate backward** — even though the wheel is physically moving forward!

**Why does this happen?** The movie camera samples reality at a fixed frame rate (say, **24 frames per second**). When the spoke's rotation rate exceeds half the frame rate, the discrete snapshots can no longer faithfully represent the true motion. The high-frequency motion *folds back* into a low-frequency illusion.

| System Component | Movie Analogy | DSP Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous motion of wheel | Real-world analog signal $x_a(t)$ | Continuous-time signal |
| Camera frame rate (24 fps) | Sampling frequency $F_s$ | Sampling frequency $F_s$ |
| Spoke appearing to reverse | Alias (false low-frequency content) | Aliased frequency $F_s - f$ |
| Blurring the film | Low-pass filter on lens | Anti-aliasing filter |
| Projector with smooth shutter | Reconstruction + smoothing | Anti-imaging filter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem demands that the input signal be **strictly band-limited** to $F \leq F_s/2$ *before* sampling. The anti-aliasing filter is the engineering mechanism that enforces this mathematical requirement on real, naturally broadband signals.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Nyquist Rate:** $F_{Nyq} = 2 F_{max}$ (the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing for a signal with maximum frequency $F_{max}$).
- **Nyquist Frequency:** $F_s/2$ (the highest unambiguously representable frequency).
- **Transition Ratio ($k$):** $k = \dfrac{\text{Transition Bandwidth}}{F_s/2}$ — a measure of filter sharpness; smaller $k$ means sharper (higher-order) filter.
- **Filter Order ($N$):** For a Butterworth LPF, the order needed is $N = \dfrac{-20 \log_{10}\sqrt{\delta_p \delta_s}}{10 \log_{10}(1/\varepsilon)}$ where $\varepsilon = \sqrt{(10^{0.1 A_p} - 1)}$ and $A_p$ is passband ripple in dB.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, the typical design problem gives you a **passband edge** ($\Omega_p$), **stopband edge** ($\Omega_s$), **passband ripple** ($A_p$), and **stopband attenuation** ($A_s$). You must determine the *minimum filter order* and the *cutoff frequency* for either the AAF or the AIF.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept: Why Are These Filters Necessary?

The entire justification comes from the **Sampling Theorem** and the mathematics of impulse-train sampling.

**Step 1 — Continuous-time signal $x_a(t)$ exists in nature with arbitrary bandwidth.**
A real audio signal, for example, contains energy from 20 Hz up to 20 kHz, plus noise and out-of-band interference well above 20 kHz.

**Step 2 — Impulse-train sampling creates periodic spectral replicas.**
When $x_a(t)$ is sampled at $F_s = 1/T$, the spectrum becomes:

$$X_s(j\Omega) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_a(j(\Omega - k\Omega_s))$$

where $\Omega_s = 2\pi F_s$ is the sampling angular frequency.

**Step 3 — Overlap (aliasing) occurs if the replicas touch.**
If the bandwidth of $X_a(j\Omega)$ exceeds $\Omega_s/2$, the shifted copies **overlap**, and frequency components from the $k=1$ replica fold into the baseband $[-\Omega_s/2, \Omega_s/2]$. The result is **irreversible corruption** — once aliased, the original signal *cannot* be recovered by any filter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"You cannot unscramble an egg."** The anti-aliasing filter's job is to *prevent* the egg from getting scrambled in the first place.

**Step 4 — Reconstruction generates stair-step artifacts.**
On the DAC side, a Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) holds each sample value constant for one sampling period $T$, producing a **piecewise-constant** stair-step waveform. The spectrum of this waveform is the baseband spectrum multiplied by the sinc-like ZOH transfer function, **plus** attenuated copies of all spectral images at $k\Omega_s$. The anti-imaging filter removes these images and partially compensates for the $\sin(x)/x$ droop.

**Step 5 — Together, AAF and AIF form a matched pair.**
The AAF band-limits the input; the AIF smooths the output. Both must have **cutoff frequency = $F_s/2$** in an ideal system, and both must be of the **same family** (typically Butterworth) to maintain matched magnitude response.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you need to answer KTU 2024 Scheme exam questions on this topic. Pay special attention to the angular vs. linear frequency conversions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Rate | $F_{Nyq} = 2 F_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing (Hz) |
| 2 | Nyquist Frequency | $F_{Nyq} = F_s / 2$ | Half the sampling frequency (Hz) |
| 3 | Angular Sampling Freq. | $\Omega_s = 2\pi F_s$ | Radians per second (rad/s) |
| 4 | Aliased Frequency | $f_a = \vert F_s - f \vert$ | True frequency $f$ appears as $f_a$ (Hz) |
| 5 | Sampling Theorem (no alias) | $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$ | Strict inequality preferred in practice |
| 6 | ZOH Transfer Function | $H_{ZOH}(j\Omega) = T \dfrac{\sin(\Omega T / 2)}{\Omega T / 2} \, e^{-j\Omega T / 2}$ | Sinc roll-off with linear-phase delay |
| 7 | Reconstruction (Ideal) | $x_a(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{t - nT}{T}\right)$ | Whittaker-Shannon interpolation |
| 8 | Butterworth Order (AAF/AIF) | $N = \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\left[\left(10^{A_s/10} - 1\right) / \left(10^{A_p/10} - 1\right)\right]}{2 \log_{10}(\Omega_s / \Omega_p)}$ | Min. order for given specs |
| 9 | Cutoff Frequency (AAF) | $\Omega_c = \dfrac{\Omega_p}{(10^{A_p/10} - 1)^{1/(2N)}}$ | Butterworth -3 dB cutoff |
| 10 | Transition Bandwidth | $\Delta F = F_s / 2 - F_{max}$ | Gap between $F_{max}$ and $F_s/2$ |
| 11 | Spectral Replica Positions | $f_k = \pm F_s \pm 2F_{max}$ | Locations of baseband and image spectra |
| 12 | Image Attenuation Required | $A_{img} \geq 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\pi (F_s - F_{max})}{F_s}\right)$ | dB; attenuation needed to suppress $1^{st}$ image |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Using $F_s$ (Hz) where the formula requires $\Omega_s$ (rad/s), or vice versa. The conversion factor is always $\Omega = 2\pi F$. Examiners specifically test for this.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of AAF | Role of AIF |
|---|---|---|
| **Digital Audio (CD 44.1 kHz)** | LPF at 20 kHz before ADC; prevents ultrasonic aliasing that would corrupt audible band | LPF at 20 kHz after DAC; removes images at $\pm$24.1 kHz, $\pm$68.2 kHz, etc. |
| **Biomedical (ECG 360 Hz)** | Removes 50/60 Hz mains hum and EMG interference above 150 Hz | Smooths stair-stepped QRS complex output |
| **Telecommunications (Voice 8 kHz)** | Anti-aliasing at 3.4 kHz to prevent cross-talk with adjacent channels | Removes spectral images at 4.6 kHz, 12.6 kHz, ... |
| **Radar / SDR** | Wideband AAF before high-speed ADC; critical to avoid false targets | Post-DAC filtering for clean analog output |
| **Music Synthesis** | Limits input bandwidth from analog instruments | Removes zipper noise and images from DAC output |

> [!NOTE]
> In **Over-Sampling** ADCs/DACs, the sampling rate is many times higher than $2F_{max}$, which allows the AAF/AIF specifications to be **relaxed** (lower order, gentler roll-off). This is why modern audio uses 192 kHz or 384 kHz internal rates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Mathematics of Aliasing

Let the input analog signal be $x_a(t)$ with Fourier Transform $X_a(j\Omega)$. Define the **ideal impulse train** sampler as:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT)$$

where $T = 1/F_s$ is the sampling period. The sampled signal is:

$$x_s(t) = x_a(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_a(nT) \, \delta(t - nT)$$

**Step 1 — Fourier Transform of the impulse train.**

Using the standard result that the Fourier series of an impulse train is another impulse train:

$$P(j\Omega) = \frac{2\pi}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\Omega - k\Omega_s)$$

where $\Omega_s = 2\pi / T = 2\pi F_s$.

**Step 2 — Apply the multiplication-in-convolution property.**

Multiplication in time domain corresponds to **convolution in frequency domain** scaled by $1/(2\pi)$:

$$X_s(j\Omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \, X_a(j\Omega) * P(j\Omega)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $P(j\Omega)$ and distribute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_s(j\Omega) &= \frac{1}{2\pi} \, X_a(j\Omega) * \left[\frac{2\pi}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\Omega - k\Omega_s)\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \left[X_a(j\Omega) * \delta(\Omega - k\Omega_s)\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_a\!\left(j(\Omega - k\Omega_s)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

The spectrum of the sampled signal consists of the original spectrum $X_a(j\Omega)$ replicated at every integer multiple of $\Omega_s$. If $X_a(j\Omega)$ is **strictly band-limited** to $[-\Omega_m, \Omega_m]$ with $\Omega_m \leq \Omega_s/2$, the replicas do **not overlap**, and the original can be recovered by an **ideal low-pass filter** of gain $T$ and cutoff $\Omega_s/2$.

**Step 5 — Condition for aliasing-free sampling.**

For zero overlap, we require:

$$\Omega_m \leq \frac{\Omega_s}{2} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad F_s \geq 2 F_m$$

This is the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**. Violation of this condition is what creates aliasing.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Reconstruction via Ideal Low-Pass Filter (Anti-Imaging Action)

Given the discrete samples $x(nT)$ obtained from $x_a(t)$, we wish to reconstruct $x_a(t)$ exactly.

**Step 1 — Place an ideal LPF after the sampler.**

Let $H_r(j\Omega)$ be the transfer function of an ideal reconstruction filter:

$$H_r(j\Omega) = \begin{cases} T, & \vert \Omega \vert \leq \Omega_c \\ 0, & \vert \Omega \vert > \Omega_c \end{cases}$$

with cutoff $\Omega_c = \Omega_s / 2$.

**Step 2 — Filter the sampled spectrum.**

$$X_r(j\Omega) = X_s(j\Omega) \cdot H_r(j\Omega) = \frac{1}{T} X_a(j\Omega) \cdot T = X_a(j\Omega)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the inverse Fourier Transform.**

The impulse response of the ideal LPF is the **normalized sinc function**:

$$h_r(t) = \text{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{T}\right) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi t / T)}{\pi t / T}$$

**Step 4 — Convolve with the impulse train to get the reconstructed signal.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_a(t) &= x_s(t) * h_r(t) \\
&= \left[\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_a(nT) \, \delta(t - nT)\right] * \text{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{T}\right) \\
&= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_a(nT) \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{t - nT}{T}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the celebrated **Whittaker-Shannon Interpolation Formula**. It shows that the band-limited signal can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples using a sum of shifted, scaled sinc functions.

> [!NOTE]
> **In practice**, the sinc function is non-causal and infinite in extent, so the *ideal* reconstruction is unrealizable. The **anti-imaging filter** is a *practical approximation* to this ideal LPF, combined with a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** that produces the stair-step output.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Zero-Order Hold Transfer Function and the Need for Compensation

The ZOH holds each sample constant for $T$ seconds. It can be modeled as a **rectangular pulse convolved with the impulse train**:

$$x_{ZOH}(t) = x_s(t) * h_{ZOH}(t)$$

where

$$h_{ZOH}(t) = u(t) - u(t - T) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t < T \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 1 — Fourier Transform of the ZOH pulse.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{ZOH}(j\Omega) &= \int_0^T e^{-j\Omega t} \, dt \\
&= \left[\frac{e^{-j\Omega t}}{-j\Omega}\right]_0^T \\
&= \frac{1 - e^{-j\Omega T}}{j\Omega} \\
&= \frac{e^{-j\Omega T/2}\!\left(e^{j\Omega T/2} - e^{-j\Omega T/2}\right)}{j\Omega} \\
&= T \cdot \frac{\sin(\Omega T / 2)}{\Omega T / 2} \cdot e^{-j\Omega T / 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude response (the "sinc droop").**

$$\vert H_{ZOH}(j\Omega) \vert = T \left\vert \dfrac{\sin(\Omega T / 2)}{\Omega T / 2} \right\vert$$

This is the **sinc envelope**. At $\Omega = 0$, $\vert H_{ZOH} \vert = T$ (peak). The first null occurs at $\Omega = 2\pi / T = \Omega_s$.

**Step 3 — Why compensation is needed.**

At the edge of the audio band, say $F = 20$ kHz with $F_s = 44.1$ kHz:

$$\Omega T / 2 = \pi F / F_s = \pi \cdot 20 / 44.1 \approx 1.425 \text{ rad}$$

$$\vert H_{ZOH} \vert = T \cdot \dfrac{\sin(1.425)}{1.425} \approx T \cdot 0.988$$

This is roughly a **$-0.1$ dB** drop — small but audible in high-fidelity systems. A digital **$\sin(x)/x$ corrector** filter ($1/\text{sinc}$) is often inserted before the DAC to flatten this response.

**Step 4 — The anti-imaging filter must remove the images.**

The ZOH output has spectral images centered at $\pm k \Omega_s$ (for $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$) with magnitudes decreasing as $\sin(k\pi)/(k\pi) = 0$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots$ (the first image is actually zero at $\Omega = \Omega_s$, but the spectrum still contains significant energy near $\Omega_s$ from the broadened main lobe). A low-pass filter with cutoff $\Omega_s/2$ and high stopband attenuation is required.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of AAF vs. AIF

| Parameter | Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF) | Anti-Imaging Filter (AIF) |
|---|---|---|
| **Position in system** | Before ADC (analog input side) | After DAC (analog output side) |
| **Purpose** | Prevent aliasing during sampling | Remove spectral images + smooth stair-step output |
| **Input signal nature** | Continuous-time, possibly broadband | Stair-stepped piecewise-constant |
| **Cutoff frequency** | $\leq F_s/2$ (often slightly less, e.g., $0.45 F_s$) | $\leq F_s/2$ (matched to AAF) |
| **Critical concern** | Stopband attenuation $A_s$ at $F_s - F_{max}$ | Stopband attenuation $A_s$ at first image $F_s - F_{max}$ |
| **Type** | Analog LPF (Butterworth, Chebyshev, Elliptic) | Analog LPF (often same family as AAF) |
| **Phase response** | Linear phase desired (audio) | Linear phase desired (audio) |
| **Failure mode if removed** | Irreversible aliasing corruption | Audible/visible spectral images, harshness |
| **Filter order rule of thumb** | Higher order (stricter specs) | Lower order (relaxed by oversampling) |
| **In practice** | Often integrated inside modern $\Delta\Sigma$ ADC chip | Often integrated inside modern $\Delta\Sigma$ DAC chip |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem:** An audio signal has maximum frequency $F_{max} = 4$ kHz. It is sampled at $F_s = 10$ kHz. Determine:
- (i) The Nyquist rate.
- (ii) The location of the first spectral image.
- (iii) The minimum stopband attenuation required if the AAF is to be replaced by a practical filter with passband edge $F_p = 3.8$ kHz.
- (iv) A signal at 11 kHz is present in the input. What aliased frequency will it appear as?

**Solution:**

**(i) Nyquist Rate:**

$$F_{Nyq} = 2 \cdot F_{max} = 2 \cdot 4 \text{ kHz} = 8 \text{ kHz}$$

Since $F_s = 10$ kHz $> 8$ kHz, the sampling theorem is satisfied — but only barely (small guard band).

**(ii) First spectral image location:**

$$F_{image,1} = F_s - F_{max} = 10 - 4 = 6 \text{ kHz}$$

(The first image sits in the stopband of the reconstruction filter, between $F_{max} = 4$ kHz and $F_s/2 = 5$ kHz.)

**(iii) Stopband attenuation required:**

The image of a component at $F = F_p = 3.8$ kHz appears at:

$$F_{alias} = \vert F_s - F_p \vert = \vert 10 - 3.8 \vert = 6.2 \text{ kHz}$$

This is in the stopband, which begins at $F_s - F_p = 6.2$ kHz. The attenuation needed is determined by the acceptable aliasing noise level. For a typical 16-bit audio system, $A_s \geq 96$ dB is required.

**(iv) Alias of 11 kHz signal:**

$$F_{aliased} = \vert F_s - F \vert = \vert 10 - 11 \vert = 1 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A 11 kHz signal — completely inaudible to humans (above 20 Hz – 20 kHz? No, 11 kHz is within hearing!) — would alias to 1 kHz, a very audible tone!** This is exactly the kind of interference the anti-aliasing filter MUST remove.

### 4.3 Aliasing Visualization (How Frequencies Fold)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POS["POSITIVE FREQUENCY AXIS"]
        P0[0 HZ DC]
        P1[FMAX HZ TRUE BAND EDGE]
        P2[FS OVER 2 NYQUIST]
        P3[FS HZ FIRST IMAGE CENTER]
        P4[3FS OVER 2]
    end

    subgraph NEG["NEGATIVE FREQUENCY AXIS"]
        N0[0 HZ DC]
        N1[MINUS FMAX HZ]
        N2[MINUS FS OVER 2]
        N3[MINUS FS HZ]
    end

    P0 --- N0
    P1 --- N1
    P2 --- N2
    P3 --- N3

    ALIAS_ARROW1[TRUE SIGNAL AT 11 KHZ] -.->|FOLDED TO 1 KHZ| APPEARS_AS[APPEARS AS 1 KHZ AFTER SAMPLING]
    ALIAS_ARROW2[TRUE SIGNAL AT 13 KHZ] -.->|FOLDED TO 3 KHZ| APPEARS_AS2[APPEARS AS 3 KHZ AFTER SAMPLING]

    style ALIAS_ARROW1 fill:#ffcccc
    style ALIAS_ARROW2 fill:#ffcccc
    style APPEARS_AS fill:#ccffcc
    style APPEARS_AS2 fill:#ccffcc
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The arrows above demonstrate the **folding** property. Any frequency $f > F_s/2$ maps to a *unique* apparent frequency in $[0, F_s/2]$. Specifically, a true frequency $f$ appears as:
>
> $$f_{apparent} = \begin{cases} f, & f \leq F_s/2 \\ F_s - f, & F_s/2 < f \leq F_s \\ f - F_s, & F_s < f \leq 3F_s/2 \\ \cdots \end{cases}$$
>
> This is why the **anti-aliasing filter must remove ALL energy above $F_s/2$** before sampling.
