# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pipelining a First-Order IIR Section (Exhaustive)

**Given.** First-order IIR filter:

$$y(n) = -a_1 y(n-1) + b_0 x(n)$$

Transfer function:

$$H(z) = \frac{b_0}{1 + a_1 z^{-1}}$$

**Goal.** Produce a 2-stage pipelined version $(L=2)$ with the *same* magnitude response.

---

**Step 1 — Identify feedback critical path.**

The recursion expresses $y(n)$ as a function of $y(n-1)$, which itself depends on $y(n-2)$, etc. In hardware, this loop forces the multiplier and adder to be in series within a single clock period.

---

**Step 2 — Apply direct 2-stage pipelining (naive).**

$$y(n) = -a_1 y(n-1-2) + b_0 x(n)$$

The $z$-domain equivalent is

$$H_{naive}(z) = \frac{b_0}{1 + a_1 z^{-3}}$$

This has a pole at $z = -a_1^{1/3}$, which is **NOT** the same system. ❌

---

**Step 3 — Look-ahead transformation.**

We demand that the original pole at $z = -a_1$ be preserved. Compute the new recursion that reproduces the *same* $H(z)$:

$$y(n) = -a_1 y(n-3) + b_0 x(n) - a_1 b_0 x(n-3) + a_1^{2} b_0 x(n-3) \cdot \text{(correction)}$$

Let us derive systematically. Multiply the original recursion by $(-a_1)$ and delay by 2:

$$-a_1 y(n-2) = a_1^{2} y(n-3) - a_1 b_0 x(n-2)$$

Add the original at $(n-2)$:

$$y(n-2) = -a_1 y(n-3) + b_0 x(n-2)$$

Combine:

$$y(n) = -a_1 [-a_1 y(n-3) + b_0 x(n-2)] - a_1 y(n-3) + b_0 x(n)$$

$$y(n) = a_1^{2} y(n-3) - a_1 b_0 x(n-2) - a_1 y(n-3) + b_0 x(n)$$

$$y(n) = -(a_1 + a_1^{2}) y(n-3) + b_0 [x(n) - a_1 x(n-2)]$$

Wait — this is incorrect. The correct look-ahead recursion is:

$$y(n) = -a_1 y(n-1) + b_0 x(n) \quad \Rightarrow \quad y(n+1) = -a_1 y(n) + b_0 x(n+1)$$

By induction, $y(n) = (-a_1)^{L} y(n-L) + \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} (-a_1)^{i} b_0 x(n-i)$.

For $L = 2$:

$$y(n) = a_1^{2} y(n-2) + b_0 [x(n) - a_1 x(n-1)]$$

**Step 4 — Verify $H(z)$.**

Take the $z$-transform:

$$Y(z) = a_1^{2} z^{-2} Y(z) + b_0 X(z) - a_1 b_0 z^{-1} X(z)$$

$$Y(z) [1 - a_1^{2} z^{-2}] = b_0 [1 - a_1 z^{-1}] X(z)$$

$$H_{L=2}(z) = \frac{b_0 (1 - a_1 z^{-1})}{(1 - a_1 z^{-1})(1 + a_1 z^{-1})} = \frac{b_0}{1 + a_1 z^{-1}} = H(z) \quad ✅$$

The $(1 - a_1 z^{-1})$ factor cancels — this is the **look-ahead cancellation polynomial**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — General $L$-Stage Look-Ahead Formula

Starting from $y(n) = -a_1 y(n-1) + b_0 x(n)$:

Iterate $L$ times:

$$y(n) = (-a_1)^{L} y(n-L) + \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} (-a_1)^{i} b_0 x(n-i)$$

The denominator polynomial becomes

$$D_{L}(z) = 1 - (-a_1)^{L} z^{-L} = (1 + a_1 z^{-1}) \cdot \left[ 1 - a_1 z^{-1} + a_1^{2} z^{-2} - \cdots + (-a_1)^{L-1} z^{-(L-1)} \right]$$

The bracketed term is the **cancellation polynomial** $C_{L}(z) = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} (-a_1)^{i} z^{-i}$.

The numerator is the same cancellation polynomial times $b_0$. The $(1 + a_1 z^{-1})$ factor cancels between numerator and denominator, leaving the original $H(z)$ with the $C_L(z)$ factor absorbed into the **feedforward path** of the hardware.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Trick Question:** "Does look-ahead pipelining *change* the poles of the system?" 
> **Answer:** No — the original pole $(1 + a_1 z^{-1})$ is preserved. The cancellation polynomial $C_L(z)$ introduces *new* poles at the roots of $1 - (-a_1)^L z^{-L} = 0$, which lie on the circle $\vert z \vert = \vert a_1 \vert$. If $\vert a_1 \vert < 1$, all these new poles are **inside the unit circle** → stable.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Clustered Look-Ahead Decomposition

We desire $z^{-L}$. Trivially:

$$z^{-L} = \frac{1 - z^{-L}}{1 - z^{-1}} \cdot (1 - z^{-1}) \cdot z^{-(L-1)}$$

Equivalently, using a *feedforward* expression:

$$z^{-L} = (1 - z^{-1}) \cdot \left[ z^{-L} + z^{-(L+1)} + \cdots \right]$$

The block diagram of clustered look-ahead adds an accumulator that sums $L$ consecutive samples. Hardware cost: $L-1$ extra adders and $L-1$ latches.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Scattered Look-Ahead for Stability

For an $L$-stage scattered decomposition, factor the cancellation polynomial:

$$1 - (-a_1)^{L} z^{-L} = \prod_{i=1}^{M} \left( 1 + p_i z^{-s} \right), \qquad s = L/M$$

The poles introduced are at the $L$-th roots of $(-a_1)^L$. Each factor corresponds to a *sparser* sub-filter, and crucially **none of the new poles lie exactly at the origin** in the standard form, so the system remains well-conditioned.

For 1st-order IIR with $\vert a_1 \vert < 1$, scattered look-ahead is **automatically stable** — no magnitude-truncation is required.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Pipelined FIR vs. Pipelined IIR

| Property | FIR (Non-Recursive) | IIR (Recursive) |
|---|---|---|
| Stability after pipelining | Always stable | Stability **may be lost** |
| Look-ahead needed? | No | **Yes** (essential) |
| Hardware overhead | $L$ extra latches | $L$ latches + cancellation polynomial hardware |
| Frequency response | Identical (with $z^{-L}$ delay) | Identical (with $z^{-L}$ delay) |
| Group delay change | $L$ samples added | $L$ samples added |
| KTU typical example | FIR equalizer, matched filter | Biquad, lattice, all-pass |
| Coefficient sensitivity | Low | High (in scattered form) |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A first-order IIR filter has $a_1 = 0.5$, $b_0 = 1$. Design a 3-stage pipelined version using look-ahead.

**Solution:**

Original: $H(z) = 1 / (1 + 0.5 z^{-1})$, pole at $z = -0.5$ (stable).

For $L = 3$, cancellation polynomial:

$$C_3(z) = 1 - 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2}$$

Pipelined recursion:

$$y(n) = -(-0.5)^{3} y(n-3) + \sum_{i=0}^{2} (-0.5)^{i} x(n-i)$$

$$y(n) = 0.125 y(n-3) + x(n) - 0.5 x(n-1) + 0.25 x(n-2)$$

Verify: 

$$\frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1 - 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2}}{1 - 0.125 z^{-3}}$$

$$= \frac{1 - 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2}}{(1 + 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2})} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}} \quad ✅$$

**Stability check:** New denominator roots are 3rd roots of $0.125 = (-0.5)^3$, all on $\vert z \vert = 0.5 < 1$. Stable. ✅

```mermaid
        FFW --> S2["ADDER SUM"]
```

```mermaid
    B --> C["STEP 2 APPLY NAIVE PIPELINING Z INVERSE L"]
    C --> D["POLE MISMATCH CHECK"]
    D --> E["STEP 3 ADD LOOK AHEAD CANCELLATION TERMS"]
    E --> F["STEP 4 DERIVE CANCELLATION POLYNOMIAL C L Z"]
    F --> G["STEP 5 NEW RECURSION WITH L DELAY IN FEEDBACK"]
    G --> H["STEP 6 STABILITY CHECK ALL POLES INSIDE UNIT CIRCLE"]
    H --> I["FINAL PIPELINED H PIPE Z EQUALS Z POWER L H Z"]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Pipelining for IIR: Stability Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["PIPELINED IIR SYSTEM"] --> Q["COMPUTE DENOMINATOR ROOTS"]
    Q --> R{"ALL ROOTS HAVE MAGNITUDE LESS THAN 1"}
    R -->|"YES"| STABLE["SYSTEM IS BIBO STABLE"]
    R -->|"NO"| UNSTABLE["SYSTEM IS UNSTABLE"]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]

> **Q:** Define *pipelining* in digital filters. State **two** advantages and **one** limitation of pipelining an IIR filter.

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** *Pipelining* is a technique of inserting delay (latch) elements between the combinational sub-operations of a digital filter so as to reduce the critical path delay, thereby increasing the maximum operable sample rate, while preserving the original input-output transfer function up to a constant latency.

**Advantages (1 mark each, any two):**
1. The critical path between successive latches is reduced, allowing a higher clock frequency and hence higher throughput.
2. The lower supply voltage possible at higher speed leads to reduced dynamic power dissipation.
3. It enables modular, regular VLSI / FPGA implementation.

**Limitation (1 mark):**
1. In recursive (IIR) filters, naive insertion of delays alters the feedback path and can render the system unstable; *look-ahead* transformation is mandatory, which increases hardware complexity.

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

> **Q:** What is *look-ahead* pipelining? Why is it required for IIR filters and not for FIR filters?

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

*Look-ahead pipelining* is a transformation in which the recursion is expanded by expressing the current output $y(n)$ in terms of the output delayed by $L$ samples, $y(n-L)$, together with a sum of weighted input terms that "fill in" the omitted intermediate history. **(1 mark)**

It is required for IIR filters because the feedback path of an IIR filter is recursive: a direct insertion of a delay in the feedback loop would change the transfer function $H(z)$ by replacing the pole at $z = -a_k$ with a different pole, and could also make the system unstable. **(1 mark)**

For FIR filters, the difference equation has no feedback term — all $a_k = 0$ — so inserting latches simply adds a constant delay $z^{-L}$ to the output without altering $H(z)$. Hence FIR filters are *trivially* pipelinable, and no look-ahead is needed. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

> **Q (a) [7 marks]:** Consider the first-order IIR filter described by the difference equation
> 
> $y(n) = -0.6 \, y(n-1) + x(n)$
> 
> Design a **2-stage pipelined** version of this filter using look-ahead pipelining. Derive the new difference equation, verify the transfer function, and check the stability of the pipelined system.
> 
> **Q (b) [7 marks]:** With the help of a block diagram, explain *clustered* and *scattered* look-ahead pipelining techniques. Compare their hardware complexity and stability properties.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Original transfer function (1 mark):**

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 + 0.6 z^{-1}}$$

Pole at $z = -0.6$, which lies inside the unit circle $\Rightarrow$ stable.

**Step 2 — Apply 2-stage look-ahead (2 marks):**

The general look-ahead formula for a first-order IIR gives:

$$y(n) = (-a_1)^{L} y(n-L) + \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} (-a_1)^{i} b_0 x(n-i)$$

With $a_1 = 0.6$, $b_0 = 1$, $L = 2$:

$$y(n) = (-0.6)^{2} y(n-2) + \sum_{i=0}^{1} (-0.6)^{i} x(n-i)$$

$$y(n) = 0.36 \, y(n-2) + x(n) - 0.6 x(n-1)$$

**Step 3 — Derive the pipelined transfer function (2 marks):**

Take the $z$-transform:

$$Y(z) = 0.36 z^{-2} Y(z) + X(z) - 0.6 z^{-1} X(z)$$

$$Y(z) [1 - 0.36 z^{-2}] = X(z) [1 - 0.6 z^{-1}]$$

$$H_{pipe}(z) = \frac{1 - 0.6 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.36 z^{-2}} = \frac{1 - 0.6 z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.6 z^{-1})(1 + 0.6 z^{-1})} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.6 z^{-1}} = H(z) \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Stability check (1 mark):**

Denominator $1 - 0.36 z^{-2} = 0 \Rightarrow z^{2} = 0.36 \Rightarrow z = \pm 0.6$. Both poles have magnitude $0.6 < 1$ $\Rightarrow$ **stable**. The original pole at $z = -0.6$ is preserved; a new pole at $z = +0.6$ is introduced (cancellation polynomial root). Both stable.

**Step 5 — Hardware impact (1 mark):**

The pipelined recursion requires 2 latches (one in feedback, one in feedforward), 2 multiplications, and 2 additions per output sample. Critical path is reduced to one multiplier delay + one adder delay (vs. 1 multiplier + 1 adder + 1 latch in original).

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Clustered Look-Ahead (3 marks):**

The delay $z^{-L}$ is implemented as a *single* block of $L$ consecutive registers. Equivalently,

$$z^{-L} = (1 - z^{-L}) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

The term $(1 - z^{-L})$ is a feedforward FIR block, and $\frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$ is an accumulator (recursive). The combination realizes the same $z^{-L}$ delay with $L-1$ extra latches and $L-1$ extra adders but introduces $L-1$ poles at the origin (benign) and $L-1$ zeros at the roots of $1 - z^{-L} = 0$.

**Scattered Look-Ahead (2 marks):**

The delay $z^{-L}$ is *factored* into a product of $M$ smaller delays:

$$z^{-L} = \prod_{i=1}^{M} (1 + p_i z^{-s}), \qquad s = L/M$$

The cancellation polynomial $1 - (-a_1)^{L} z^{-L}$ is split into $M$ sub-filters of order $s$. The system is restructured as $M$ cascaded sparse sub-filters, each pipelined by $s$ stages. The critical path is divided across $M$ sub-filters, giving a per-stage delay of $s$ instead of $L$.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Clustered | Scattered |
|---|---|---|
| Poles added at origin | Yes ($L-1$) | None |
| Stability | Needs magnitude truncation | Naturally stable if $\vert a_k \vert < 1$ |
| Hardware cost | Lower for small $L$ | Higher (M sub-filters) |
| Numerical sensitivity | High (cancellation polynomial close to zero) | Lower |
| KTU typical application | FIR & simple IIR | High-order IIR, lattice filters |

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4] — *Internal Choice Alternative*

> **Q (a) [7 marks]:** For a digital filter with difference equation
> 
> $y(n) = 0.8 y(n-1) - 0.15 y(n-2) + x(n) + 0.5 x(n-1)$
> 
> obtain a **2-stage pipelined realization** using look-ahead. Verify that the magnitude response is preserved and determine whether the pipelined system is stable.
> 
> **Q (b) [7 marks]:** Explain how pipelining can be combined with parallel processing to increase the throughput of an FIR filter by a factor of $LM$. Draw the block diagram and discuss the trade-offs involved.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Transfer function (1 mark):**

$$H(z) = \frac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 2 — Original poles (1 mark):** $z^{2} - 0.8 z + 0.15 = 0 \Rightarrow z = 0.5, \, 0.3$. Both inside unit circle → stable.

**Step 3 — Look-ahead for $L=2$ (2 marks):** Multiply original recursion by $z^{-1}$ and substitute iteratively. The pipelined recursion takes the form:

$$y(n) = 0.8 y(n-2) - 0.15 y(n-3) - 0.8 [0.8 y(n-3) - 0.15 y(n-4)] + 0.15 [\cdots] + \text{input terms}$$

The simplified result is:

$$y(n) = c_1 y(n-2) + c_2 y(n-3) + d_0 x(n) + d_1 x(n-1) + d_2 x(n-2) + d_3 x(n-3)$$

where the coefficients $c_1, c_2, d_i$ are computed by expanding the look-ahead cancellation polynomial $(1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2})$ at the $L=2$ look-ahead step.

**Step 4 — Magnitude preservation (1.5 marks):** The new $H_{pipe}(z) = z^{-2} H(z)$ by the look-ahead theorem. Hence $\vert H_{pipe}(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$. ✅

**Step 5 — Stability check (1.5 marks):** The new denominator is $1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2}$ *multiplied by the cancellation polynomial*. The cancellation polynomial for a 2nd-order IIR with $L=2$ has roots on $\vert z \vert = $ magnitude of original poles, all of which are < 1. The original poles $0.5$ and $0.3$ are preserved, and the new poles introduced also satisfy $\vert z \vert < 1$ (since the magnitudes are 0.5 and 0.3). Hence **stable**. ✅

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Combined Pipelining + Parallel Processing Architecture (3 marks):**

For an FIR filter $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) x(n-k)$, we can:

1. **Decompose into $M$ polyphase sub-filters** (parallel processing):

$$y(n) = \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \sum_{k} h(kM+m) x(n-kM-m)$$

2. Each sub-filter is then **pipelined by $L$ stages** internally.

The total throughput is increased by a factor of $LM$: the polyphase decomposition gives a factor of $M$ (each sub-filter runs at $f_s/M$ but the system emits one output per input sample), and the internal pipelining of each sub-filter gives a further $L$× speedup. **Combined speedup = $LM$.**

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

```
INPUT x(n) → [DEMUX M-PHASE] → M PARALLEL PIPELINED SUB-FILTERS → [MUX] → OUTPUT y(n)
```

Each of the $M$ sub-filters is itself an FIR with $N/M$ taps, internally pipelined with $L$ latches.

**Trade-offs (2 marks):**

- **Area:** $M$ sub-filters require $M$ × hardware (multipliers, adders) plus $L$ latches per sub-filter.
- **Power:** Increased dynamic power due to parallel hardware, though clock frequency can be reduced.
- **Latency:** Output of sub-filter $m$ is delayed by $m$ samples + $L \cdot T_{clk}$.
- **Design complexity:** Higher; needs careful clock distribution and polyphase coefficient storage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 
> 1. **Failing to verify stability explicitly** after look-ahead transformation. Marks are awarded for *naming* the pole locations and showing $\vert z \vert < 1$.
> 2. **Confusing clustered vs. scattered look-ahead.** Students often write scattered decomposition without stating the factorization step.
> 3. **Omitting the cancellation polynomial** in look-ahead derivations. The $C_L(z) = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} (-a_1)^i z^{-i}$ expression must appear explicitly for full marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the $z^{-L}$ delay** in the final $H_{pipe}(z)$ — the pipelined system is *not* equal to $H(z)$, only to $z^{-L} H(z)$.
> 5. **Assuming pipelining always works for IIR.** Students must mention the *conditional* nature (need for $\vert a_k \vert < 1$ for first-order stability).
> 6. **Not drawing the block diagram** in 7-mark sub-questions that ask for one — typically 1–2 marks are reserved for the schematic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pipelining (Module 4, PBECT504)**

- **Definition:** Inserting latches inside DSP data-paths to shorten the critical path and raise sample rate.
- **Throughput vs. Latency:** Pipelining increases throughput ($f_s$) at the cost of latency ($L$ samples).
- **FIR pipelining:** Trivial — no look-ahead needed, simply insert latches anywhere.
- **IIR pipelining:** Requires *look-ahead* to preserve $H(z)$ and stability.
- **Look-ahead formula (1st order, depth $L$):** $y(n) = (-a_1)^L y(n-L) + \sum_{i=0}^{L-1}(-a_1)^i b_0 x(n-i)$.
- **Cancellation polynomial:** $C_L(z) = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} (-a_1)^i z^{-i}$ — explicitly appears in feedforward path.
- **Pipelined transfer function:** $H_{pipe}(z) = z^{-L} H(z)$ — magnitude is preserved; only a constant group delay is added.
- **Clustered look-ahead:** Realizes $z^{-L}$ as a single block; adds $L-1$ poles at origin.
- **Scattered look-ahead:** Factors $z^{-L}$ into $M$ sub-filters; preserves stability for $\vert a_1 \vert < 1$.
- **Stability condition (1st order):** $\vert a_1 \vert < 1$ ensures all new poles are inside the unit circle.
- **General $N$-th order stability:** All roots of $1 - (-a_1)^{L} z^{-L}$ must satisfy $\vert z \vert < 1$, equivalent to $\vert a_k \vert < 1$ for low-order cases.
- **Combined speedup:** Parallel processing ($M$×) + Pipelining ($L$×) = $LM$× throughput.
- **Power:** Pipelining allows lower $V_{DD}$ → quadratic power savings.
- **Hardware cost:** $L$ latches + cancellation polynomial adders/multipliers.
- **Use cases:** High-speed FIR equalizers, lattice filters, multi-rate filters, 5G/Wi-Fi baseband, audio codecs, radar.
- **Key equation to memorize:** $H_{pipe}(z) = z^{-L} H(z)$ — for any pipelined LTI system.
- **KTU exam signal words:** "pipelining," "look-ahead," "scattered look-ahead," "clustered look-ahead," "cancellation polynomial," "stability preservation."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to TMS320C67xx Digital Signal Processor
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Introduction to TMS320C67xx Digital Signal Processor

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **TMS320C67xx** is a family of high-performance **32-bit floating-point Digital Signal Processors (DSPs)** developed by **Texas Instruments (TI)**, built upon the advanced **VelociTI™ architecture** — a **Very Long Instruction Word (VLIW)** based architecture.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the C67xx processor is described as a *single-chip DSP solution* designed for applications requiring:
- High-precision floating-point arithmetic
- High throughput for numerically intensive algorithms
- Real-time signal processing (audio, video, communications, control systems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 4):**
> Students must be able to describe the **architectural features**, **CPU structure**, **register files**, **functional units**, **pipelining**, and **memory organization** of the TMS320C67xx processor family.

## 1.2 Key Members of the TMS320C67xx Family

| Processor | Clock Speed | Key Application Focus |
|-----------|-------------|------------------------|
| TMS320C6701 | Up to 167 MHz | General-purpose floating-point DSP |
| TMS320C6711 | Up to 200 MHz | Low-power applications |
| TMS320C6713 | Up to 225 MHz | Audio, medical imaging |
| TMS320C6727 | Up to 350 MHz | High-end audio processing |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Understanding the C67xx in Simple Terms

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Master Chef's Kitchen"**
>
> Imagine a kitchen where **one master chef (CPU)** has **8 specialized assistants (functional units)** working in parallel. The chef can issue instructions to all 8 assistants **simultaneously** through a **single very long instruction word (VLIW)**.
>
> - The chef does not wait for one assistant to finish before assigning work to another.
> - Each assistant has their own **specialty** (multiplication, addition, logical operations, data movement).
> - Two large **pantry shelves (register files A and B)** hold the ingredients.
>
> This parallel organization is precisely what makes the C67xx a powerful DSP for real-time processing tasks like filtering, FFT, and convolution.

## 1.4 Why Floating-Point? — The Engineering Motivation

In many real-world DSP applications (e.g., audio codecs, radar signal processing, biomedical signal analysis), the dynamic range of values can vary enormously. The C67xx solves this by supporting:

- **32-bit single-precision IEEE-754 floating point**
- **64-bit double-precision IEEE-754 floating point**
- **32-bit fixed-point** (for legacy code)

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Use Case:** Adaptive filters, LMS algorithms, and high-resolution spectral analysis benefit enormously from floating-point because intermediate computations do not overflow/underflow as easily as in fixed-point processors like the C54xx or C64xx.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Overview — VelociTI™ VLIW Architecture

The VelociTI architecture is the heart of the C67xx. It uses **VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word)** where multiple instructions are issued in parallel every clock cycle.

### Core Architectural Features

- **Instruction Word Width:** 256 bits (fetch packet contains up to 8 instructions × 32 bits each)
- **Functional Units:** 8 (organized into 2 data paths, A and B)
- **General-Purpose Registers:** 32 registers × 32 bits per data path = **64 registers total**
- **Pipeline Depth:** 8 stages (for C67x family)
- **Multiplier:** Two 32-bit × 32-bit fixed-point multipliers, OR **two IEEE-754 floating-point multipliers**

## 2.2 CPU Structure — Block Level Functional Architecture

The CPU of the TMS320C67xx consists of the following major sub-blocks:

1. **Program Fetch Unit** — fetches 256-bit instruction packets
2. **Instruction Dispatch Unit** — decodes and dispatches instructions to functional units
3. **Two Data Paths (A and B)** — each containing:
   - 1 **`.L` unit** (Logical/Arithmetic operations)
   - 1 **`.S` unit** (Shift, Branch, Bit manipulation)
   - 1 **`.M` unit** (Multiplication)
   - 1 **`.D` unit** (Data load/store)
4. **Register Files A and B** (32 × 32-bit each)
5. **Control Registers** (CSR, AMR, etc.)
6. **Memory Load/Store Architecture** (no direct memory-to-memory operations)

## 2.3 Functional Units — Detailed Role Description

| Unit | Type | Operations Performed |
|------|------|----------------------|
| `.L1`, `.L2` | Logical/Arithmetic | 32/40-bit integer add/sub, floating-point add (DP/SP), logical AND/OR/XOR |
| `.S1`, `.S2` | Shift/Branch | 32-bit shifts, bit-field operations, branches, floating-point reciprocal |
| `.M1`, `.M2` | Multiply | 16×16, 32×32 integer multiply, IEEE floating-point multiply (SP/DP) |
| `.D1`, `.D2` | Data | Load 32-bit, store 32-bit, address generation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each functional unit can perform a **32-bit operation in one cycle**. The floating-point units execute SP ops in 1 cycle (L, S) or 4 cycles (M, reciprocal), and DP ops in 2 cycles (L, S) or 7-8 cycles (M, reciprocal).

## 2.4 Register File Organization

The C67xx contains **two register files (A and B)**, each with **32 registers of 32 bits**.

- **Registers A0–A15** (Data path A) and **B0–B15** (Data path B) — General purpose
- **Registers A16–A31** and **B16–B31** — Used for **predicates, conditions, and special use**
- A register may be used as **two 16-bit values** (high/low) or **four 8-bit values** in some operations

### Conditional Registers

- **A1, A2, B0, B1, B2** are *conditional registers* — their value (0 or non-zero) determines whether certain instructions execute.

## 2.5 Pipeline Architecture (8-Stage)

The C67xx uses a **non-blocking 8-stage pipeline** allowing multiple instructions to be in different stages simultaneously.

| Stage | Name | Function |
|-------|------|----------|
| PG | Program Generate | Generates fetch address |
| PW | Program Address Send | Sends address to memory |
| PR | Program Address Ready | Waits for memory response |
| PF | Program Fetch | Receives instruction packet |
| DP | Dispatch | Decodes and dispatches to functional units |
| DC | Decode | Reads register file |
| E1 | Execute 1 | First execute phase |
| E2 | Execute 2 | Second execute phase (for multi-cycle ops) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Pipeline Matters:** A throughput of **1 instruction per cycle per functional unit** is achieved, meaning up to **8 instructions can complete in one cycle** at peak.

## 2.6 Memory Organization

The C67xx uses a **modified Harvard architecture** with separate program and data spaces, but unified on-chip memory.

### Memory Map (Typical for C6713):

| Address Range | Memory Type |
|---------------|-------------|
| 0x0000 0000 – 0x0000 FFFF | Internal RAM (L2) — 192 KB |
| 0x0001 0000 – 0x0001 3FFF | Internal ROM |
| 0x6000 0000 – 0x6000 FFFF | EMIF CE0 (External Memory) |
| 0x8000 0000 – 0x8000 FFFF | EMIF CE1 |
| 0x9000 0000 – 0x9000 FFFF | EMIF CE2 |
| 0xA000 0000 – 0xA000 FFFF | EMIF CE3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **L1/L2 Cache Architecture:** The C6713 has 4 KB L1P (program cache), 4 KB L1D (data cache), and 192 KB unified L2 cache/RAM.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table

| Concept | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---------|-----------------|--------------|
| Instruction word size | $W_{inst} = 256$ | bits per fetch packet |
| Instructions per packet | $N = 8$ | 32-bit instructions |
| Total registers | $R_{total} = 2 \times 32 = 64$ | 32-bit each |
| SP float precision | $p_{sp} = 24$ | mantissa bits |
| DP float precision | $p_{dp} = 53$ | mantissa bits |
| SP float range | $\pm 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | IEEE-754 |
| DP float range | $\pm 1.8 \times 10^{308}$ | IEEE-754 |
| Pipeline depth | $D = 8$ | stages |
| Functional units | $F = 8$ | (.L, .S, .M, .D) × 2 |
| Throughput (peak) | $T_{peak} = F \times f_{clk}$ | MIPS/MFLOPS |
| Latency (SP add) | $L_{add} = 1$ | cycle |
| Latency (SP multiply) | $L_{mul} = 4$ | cycles |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Trap:** When asked "How many instructions execute per cycle?", the correct answer is **up to 8 per cycle** (one per functional unit), NOT 1.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Application | Why C67xx is Used |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| Audio Processing | MP3, AAC codecs | Floating-point FFT, filter precision |
| Biomedical | ECG/EEG analysis | High dynamic range signals |
| Communications | Channel equalizers, OFDM | Adaptive filters |
| Industrial Control | Motor control, robotics | Fast real-time response |
| Image Processing | Edge detection, DCT | High-throughput arithmetic |
| Defense / Radar | Pulse compression, beamforming | Floating-point FFT kernels |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Throughput and Performance Calculation

### Problem Setup
A TMS320C6713 DSP operates at $f_{clk} = 225 \text{ MHz}$ with 8 functional units. Calculate:
(a) The peak instruction throughput
(b) The peak floating-point performance in MFLOPS (SP)
(c) Time to execute an FFT of $N = 1024$ points requiring $5N \log_2 N$ operations

### Step 1: Peak Instruction Throughput

$$T_{peak} = F \times f_{clk}$$

where $F = 8$ is the number of functional units and $f_{clk}$ is the clock frequency.

$$T_{peak} = 8 \times 225 \times 10^{6} = 1.8 \times 10^{9} \text{ instructions/sec}$$

### Step 2: Peak Floating-Point Performance (SP)

Each functional unit can produce one SP floating-point result per cycle (in best case), but multiply takes 4 cycles, while add takes 1 cycle. For peak MFLOPS:

$$P_{peak} = F \times f_{clk} = 8 \times 225 = 1800 \text{ MFLOPS (SP)}$$

### Step 3: Time for 1024-Point FFT

The number of complex operations for a radix-2 FFT:

$$N_{ops} = 5 N \log_2 N$$

$$N_{ops} = 5 \times 1024 \times \log_2 1024 = 5 \times 1024 \times 10 = 51200 \text{ operations}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Since $\log_2 1024 = 10$ (because $2^{10} = 1024$), this directly multiplies with the other factors.

Time taken (assuming peak throughput):

$$t_{FFT} = \frac{N_{ops}}{P_{peak}} = \frac{51200}{1800 \times 10^{6}} = 28.4 \text{ μs}$$

> **Interpretation:** The C6713 can complete a 1024-point FFT in under **30 microseconds** — a typical benchmark for real-time audio processing.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pipeline Latency vs. Throughput

### Step 1: Compute Single-Instruction Latency

The latency for an SP floating-point multiplication is 4 cycles (pipeline stages involved). However, because of pipelining, **a new multiply instruction can be started every cycle** after the first.

### Step 2: Throughput Expression

For an $n$-stage pipeline executing $N$ instructions:

$$T_{total} = (n - 1) + N = (D - 1) + N \text{ cycles}$$

where $D$ is the pipeline depth.

For $N = 8$ SP multiplies on C67xx:

$$T_{total} = (8 - 1) + 8 = 7 + 8 = 15 \text{ cycles}$$

But effective throughput:

$$\eta = \frac{N}{T_{total}} = \frac{8}{15} \approx 0.533 \text{ instructions/cycle/functional unit}$$

This converges to 1 instruction/cycle as $N \to \infty$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Fixed-Point vs. Floating-Point DSP

| Parameter | C54xx (Fixed) | C64xx (Fixed) | **C67xx (Floating)** |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|------------------------|
| Data type | 16-bit fixed | 16/32-bit fixed | 32/64-bit float |
| Dynamic range | $\sim 10^3$ | $\sim 10^9$ | $\sim 10^{\pm 38}$ (SP) |
| Precision | Limited | Better | Best |
| Power | Low | Low | Higher |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Ease of algorithm development | Difficult | Moderate | Easy |
| Application | Voice codecs | Video, wireless | Audio, biomedical, radar |

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Memory Bandwidth Analysis

### Given:
- External Memory Interface (EMIF) clock: 100 MHz
- Bus width: 32 bits

### Peak Memory Bandwidth

$$BW_{peak} = f_{bus} \times W_{bus}$$

$$BW_{peak} = 100 \times 10^{6} \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 400 \text{ MB/s}$$

> **Practical Note:** Sustained bandwidth is typically **60-80%** of peak due to bus protocol overheads, giving $\approx 240\text{–}320 \text{ MB/s}$.

This bandwidth is critical for memory-bound DSP algorithms (e.g., matrix multiplication, large FFTs).

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Speedup Due to Pipelining

Amdahl's Law for pipelined execution:

$$S = \frac{T_{non-pipelined}}{T_{pipelined}} = \frac{N \times t_{cycle}}{(D - 1) \cdot t_{cycle} + N \cdot t_{cycle}}$$

For $N \gg D$:

$$S \to \frac{N \cdot t_{cycle}}{N \cdot t_{cycle}} \cdot \frac{N}{(D-1)+N} \approx 1 \text{ (per unit)}$$

But for **parallel units**:

$$S_{parallel} = \frac{N \times t_{cycle} \times F}{(D - 1) t_{cycle} + N t_{cycle}} = F \text{ (peak)}$$

> **Takeaway:** With 8 functional units in C67xx, theoretical speedup is **8× over single-unit execution**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Architecture of TMS320C67xx

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU[C671xx CPU Core]
        PFU[Program Fetch Unit]
        IDU[Instruction Dispatch Unit]
        RFA[Register File A - 32 x 32 bit]
        RFB[Register File B - 32 x 32 bit]
        LSUB[.L1 .S1 .M1 .D1 - Data Path A]
        RSUB[.L2 .S2 .M2 .D2 - Data Path B]
        CRF[Control Register File - CSR AMR etc]
    end

    subgraph MEM[Memory Subsystem]
        L1P[L1P Program Cache 4KB]
        L1D[L1D Data Cache 4KB]
        L2[L2 Unified Cache RAM 192KB]
        EMIF[External Memory Interface EMIF]
    end

    subgraph PER[On Chip Peripherals]
        MCBSP[Multichannel Buffered Serial Port]
        TIMER[Timers 0 1 2]
        DMA[Direct Memory Access Controller]
        GPIO[General Purpose IO]
        HPI[Host Port Interface]
    end

    PFU --> IDU
    IDU --> LSUB
    IDU --> RSUB
    LSUB --> RFA
    RSUB --> RFB
    CRF --> LSUB
    CRF --> RSUB

    PFU --> L1P
    L1D --> LSUB
    RFA --> L1D
    RFB --> L1D
    L1P --> L2
    L1D --> L2
    L2 --> EMIF

    EMIF --> MCBSP
    EMIF --> TIMER
    EMIF --> DMA
    EMIF --> GPIO
    EMIF --> HPI
```

## 4.2 Pipeline Flow Diagram (8-Stage)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PG - Program Generate] --> B[PW - Program Address Send]
    B --> C[PR - Program Address Ready]
    C --> D[PF - Program Fetch]
    D --> E[DP - Dispatch]
    E --> F[DC - Decode]
    F --> G[E1 - Execute Stage 1]
    G --> H[E2 - Execute Stage 2]
    H --> I[Result Writeback]
```

## 4.3 Functional Unit Allocation in Data Paths

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DA[Data Path A]
        A1[.L1 Logical Arithmetic Unit]
        A2[.S1 Shift Branch Unit]
        A3[.M1 Multiply Unit]
        A4[.D1 Data Load Store Unit]
        RFA[Register File A - 32 x 32 bit]
    end

    subgraph DB[Data Path B]
        B1[.L2 Logical Arithmetic Unit]
        B2[.S2 Shift Branch Unit]
        B3[.M2 Multiply Unit]
        B4[.D2 Data Load Store Unit]
        RFB[Register File B - 32 x 32 bit]
    end

    RFA --> A1
    RFA --> A2
    RFA --> A3
    RFA --> A4

    RFB --> B1
    RFB --> B2
    RFB --> B3
    RFB --> B4

    A1 -.cross.-> B1
    A3 -.cross.-> B3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Path Connections:** Data Path A registers can supply operands to Data Path B units (and vice versa) via 1X and 2X cross paths, providing flexibility in instruction scheduling.

## 4.4 Memory Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU[CPU Core] --> L1P[L1 Program Cache 4KB]
    CPU --> L1D[L1 Data Cache 4KB]
    L1P --> L2[L2 Unified Cache RAM 192KB]
    L1D --> L2
    L2 --> EMIF[External Memory Interface]
    EMIF --> SDRAM[External SDRAM]
    EMIF --> SRAM[External SRAM]
    EMIF --> FLASH[External Flash]
```

## 4.5 Application Flow — DSP Algorithm Execution on C67xx

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Analog Input Signal] --> B[Anti Aliasing Filter]
    B --> C[ADC Converter]
    C --> D[Buffer in L2 Memory]
    D --> E[DSP Core - Performs FFT FIR etc]
    E --> F[Processed Data in Memory]
    F --> G[DAC Converter]
    G --> H[Reconstruction Filter]
    H --> I[Analog Output Signal]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define VLIW architecture. How is it implemented in the TMS320C67xx processor?**

#### Model Answer:
VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word) is a CPU architecture where **multiple instructions are packed into a single long instruction word** and executed in parallel by multiple functional units, with parallelism determined at compile time.

In TMS320C67xx, the instruction word is **256 bits wide**, containing up to **8 parallel 32-bit instructions**. These are dispatched every cycle to **8 functional units** (2 sets of `.L`, `.S`, `.M`, `.D`), enabling very high throughput. The compiler is responsible for scheduling — the hardware does not dynamically detect parallelism.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**List any three advantages of floating-point DSPs over fixed-point DSPs.**

#### Model Answer:
1. **Wider dynamic range** — supports values from $\sim 10^{-38}$ to $\sim 10^{+38}$ (SP) without scaling.
2. **Higher precision** — 24-bit mantissa (SP) avoids quantization noise in iterative algorithms.
3. **Easier software development** — no need for manual scaling or saturation logic, reducing development time.
4. **Better numerical stability** for recursive algorithms (e.g., IIR filters, LMS adaptive filters).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the CPU architecture of TMS320C67xx with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the two register files and the eight functional units.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the 8-stage pipeline of TMS320C67xx. What is the difference between latency and throughput in this pipeline?

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — CPU Architecture Explanation:**

The CPU of TMS320C67xx consists of:

1. **Program Fetch Unit** — Generates the program counter, fetches 256-bit instruction packets.
2. **Instruction Dispatch Unit** — Decodes the instruction packet and distributes individual instructions to the appropriate functional units.
3. **Two Register Files (A and B)** — Each file contains **32 registers of 32 bits** ($32 \times 32$ bits). Data Path A uses Register File A; Data Path B uses Register File B. Data can be exchanged via cross-paths (1X and 2X).
4. **Eight Functional Units** organized into two data paths:
   - **Data Path A:** `.L1`, `.S1`, `.M1`, `.D1`
   - **Data Path B:** `.L2`, `.S2`, `.M2`, `.D2`
5. **Control Register File** — Contains special registers (CSR, AMR, etc.) for system control.

| Functional Unit | Primary Operations |
|------------------|---------------------|
| `.L1`, `.L2` | 32/40-bit add/sub, logical ops, SP/DP float add |
| `.S1`, `.S2` | Shift, branch, bit manipulation, SP reciprocal |
| `.M1`, `.M2` | 16×16, 32×32 fixed multiply, SP/DP float multiply |
| `.D1`, `.D2` | 32-bit load, store, address generation |

> **Key Architectural Distinction:** The C67xx uses a **load-store architecture** — all memory operations go through `.D` units; computational units operate only on registers.

**Part (b) — Pipeline and Latency vs. Throughput:**

The 8-stage pipeline of C67xx:

| Stage | Operation |
|-------|-----------|
| PG | Program address generate |
| PW | Program address send |
| PR | Program address ready |
| PF | Program fetch |
| DP | Instruction dispatch |
| DC | Instruction decode |
| E1 | Execute phase 1 |
| E2 | Execute phase 2 |

**Latency** = number of cycles from when an instruction enters the pipeline until it produces a result.
Example: SP float multiply has **4-cycle latency** (E1 takes 2 cycles, E2 takes 2 cycles).

**Throughput** = number of cycles between two consecutive instructions of the same type producing results.
Example: SP float multiply has **1-cycle throughput** (a new instruction can be initiated every cycle).

> **Why This Matters:** Throughput is the key to high performance. Although multiply has 4-cycle latency, the throughput of 1 cycle means **8 multiplies per cycle** are possible across 8 functional units.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the memory organization of TMS320C67xx. What are L1 and L2 caches?

**(b) [7 Marks]** What are the salient features of TMS320C67xx? Compare it with the fixed-point TMS320C54xx family.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Memory Organization:**

The C67xx uses a **modified Harvard architecture**:

1. **L1 Program Cache (L1P)** — 4 KB, direct-mapped, dedicated to instructions
2. **L1 Data Cache (L1D)** — 4 KB, dual-ported, dedicated to data
3. **L2 Unified Cache/RAM** — 192 KB, shared between program and data
4. **External Memory Interface (EMIF)** — Connects to external SDRAM, SRAM, Flash

> **Cache Operation:** When the CPU requests data/instruction, L1 is checked first. On miss, L2 is consulted. On L2 miss, external memory is accessed via EMIF.

**Part (b) — Salient Features & Comparison:**

| Feature | C67xx (Floating Point) | C54xx (Fixed Point) |
|---------|-------------------------|----------------------|
| Arithmetic | 32-bit SP / 64-bit DP float | 16-bit fixed |
| Architecture | VLIW, 8 functional units | Enhanced Harvard, single MAC |
| Pipeline | 8 stages | 6 stages |
| Registers | 64 (32-bit) | 16+ auxiliary |
| Power | Higher | Very low |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Clock speed | Up to 300 MHz | Up to 160 MHz |
| Application | High-precision DSP | Battery-powered, voice |
| Throughput | 2400 MIPS / 1800 MFLOPS | ~160 MIPS |

**Salient Features of C67xx:**
- 256-bit instruction word (VLIW)
- 8 functional units
- IEEE-754 single & double precision
- Rich peripheral set (McBSP, EMIF, Timers, HPI)
- Suitable for high-end DSP applications

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing functional units with multipliers:** Students often say "C67xx has 8 multipliers" — **WRONG.** It has 8 functional units, of which only 2 are multipliers (`.M1`, `.M2`).
> 2. **Forgetting latency vs. throughput:** When asked pipeline performance, mention **both** latency and throughput.
> 3. **Mixing C64xx and C67xx:** C64xx is **fixed-point VLIW**; C67xx is **floating-point VLIW**. Both have VLIW but different data types.
> 4. **Ignoring cross-paths:** Mentioning cross-paths (1X, 2X) between data paths scores extra marks.
> 5. **Not stating VLIW word size:** Always mention the **256-bit instruction word** explicitly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — TMS320C67xx**
>
> - **Architecture:** VelociTI™, VLIW-based
> - **Instruction word size:** 256 bits (8 × 32-bit instructions per packet)
> - **Number of functional units:** **8** (2 sets of `.L`, `.S`, `.M`, `.D`)
> - **Register files:** 2 (A and B), each 32 × 32-bit
> - **Total registers:** 64 (32-bit each)
> - **Data path units per side:** `.L1` `.S1` `.M1` `.D1` in A; `.L2` `.S2` `.M2` `.D2` in B
> - **Multipliers:** 2 (`.M1` and `.M2`)
> - **Pipeline stages:** 8 (PG, PW, PR, PF, DP, DC, E1, E2)
> - **Arithmetic types:** 32-bit fixed, 32-bit SP float, 64-bit DP float
> - **Float standard:** **IEEE-754**
> - **SP precision:** 24-bit mantissa, 8-bit exponent
> - **DP precision:** 53-bit mantissa, 11-bit exponent
> - **L1 cache:** 4 KB L1P + 4 KB L1D
> - **L2 memory:** 192 KB unified cache/RAM
> - **Cross-paths:** 1X (to B), 2X (to A) — for inter-data-path operand sharing
> - **EMIF:** External Memory Interface — supports SDRAM, SRAM, Flash
> - **Peripherals:** McBSP, Timers, HPI, DMA, GPIO
> - **Peak throughput:** 8 instructions per cycle (peak)
> - **Latency (SP mul):** 4 cycles; **Throughput:** 1 cycle
> - **Load-store architecture:** All memory ops via `.D` units
> - **VLIW feature:** Compiler-driven parallelism (no dynamic hardware scheduling)
> - **Family members:** C6701, C6711, C6713, C6727 (varying speeds)
> - **Comparison with C54xx:** Floating-point vs fixed-point, higher power, higher precision
> - **Application areas:** Audio codecs, biomedical, radar, communications, image processing

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Block Diagram; Finite Word Length Effects in DSP Systems - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT504 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Functional Block Diagram; Finite Word Length Effects in DSP Systems - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Real-World DSP System

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
A **Functional Block Diagram** of a Digital Signal Processing (DSP) system is the systematic representation of the sequential hardware/functional blocks involved in converting a real-world analog signal into a digitally processed analog output. It describes the complete signal path — from the physical transducer, through anti-aliasing, sampling, quantization, digital processing, reconstruction, and finally back to the analog domain.

The canonical DSP system is composed of the following blocks (in order):

1. **Input Transducer / Sensor** — converts physical phenomena (sound, temperature, pressure) into an electrical analog signal $x(t)$.
2. **Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)** — a low-pass analog filter that band-limits the input signal to satisfy the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**, i.e., removes frequency components above $f_s/2$.
3. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit** — samples the band-limited analog signal at discrete time instants and holds each value constant for the duration of the A/D conversion.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — performs both **sampling** and **quantization**, producing a binary-coded discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital signal $x(n)$.
5. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP Chip/Processor)** — the heart of the system; executes algorithms (FIR, IIR, FFT, filtering, etc.) on $x(n)$ to produce $y(n)$.
6. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** — converts the processed digital signal $y(n)$ into a staircase analog waveform $y(t)$.
7. **Reconstruction / Anti-Imaging (Smoothing) Filter** — a low-pass filter that smooths the staircase output, removing spectral images, to deliver the final analog output $y(t)$.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Purification Plant"
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of a DSP system exactly like a municipal water purification plant.
> - The **raw river water** is your noisy analog input signal.
> - The **coarse sand filter (AAF)** removes large debris (high-frequency noise above Nyquist).
> - The **sample-and-hold unit** fills measurement cups at fixed intervals (sampling).
> - The **chemical test kit (ADC)** measures and assigns a number to each cup's water level (quantization).
> - The **central processing tank (DSP chip)** adds chlorine/purifies (algorithm execution).
> - The **bottling machine (DAC)** returns water to physical form.
> - The **final fine filter (Reconstruction filter)** gives you clean drinking water (smooth analog output).
>
> Every "measurement" and "treatment" is done with **finite precision** (cup markings, dosage pumps) — this is precisely the source of **Finite Word Length Effects**.

### Standard Key Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical/Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sampling frequency | $f_s$ | $\geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | Hz |
| Number of bits in ADC | $B$ | 8, 12, 16, 24 | bits |
| Quantization step size | $\Delta$ | $\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^B}$ | Volts (V) |
| Maximum quantization error | $e_{max}$ | $\pm \Delta/2$ | Volts (V) |
| SQNR (full-scale sine) | $6.02B + 1.76$ | dB | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, students must be able to (i) draw and label the functional block diagram, (ii) state the function of each block, and (iii) identify the sources of word-length errors — the **ADC quantizer** is the FIRST and most dominant source of quantization noise.

---

## 1.2 Finite Word Length Effects in DSP Systems — Introduction

### Formal Definition
**Finite Word Length (FWL) Effects** refer to the cumulative set of errors, distortions, and performance degradations that occur when a digital signal processing system — which is mathematically defined over continuous-valued, infinite-precision arithmetic — is actually implemented using a **fixed number of bits** $B$ for representing signal samples, filter coefficients, and arithmetic products.

In an ideal mathematical model, we have:
- Continuous amplitude signals $x(n) \in \mathbb{R}$ (infinite precision).
- Exact filter coefficients $h(k)$, $a_k$, $b_k$ (infinite precision).
- Exact arithmetic operations (no round-off).

In the real digital hardware, however:
- Each signal sample is held in a $B$-bit register.
- Each coefficient is stored in a $B$-bit register.
- The result of multiplication is a $(2B)$-bit product, which must be **re-quantized** to $B$ bits.

This unavoidable mismatch between mathematical idealization and physical realization introduces **errors at three distinct stages** of the DSP system.

### Three Principal Sources of FWL Effects (KTU Mandated Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Module-4 syllabus explicitly groups all FWL effects into **three categories**:

1. **ADC Quantization Error (Input Quantization Error, $e_q(n)$)**
   - Occurs at the A/D converter when the continuous-amplitude sampled signal is rounded/truncated to the nearest representable discrete level.
   - This error is **injected at the input** of the DSP processor.

2. **Coefficient Quantization Error**
   - Occurs because the designed (theoretical) filter coefficients $h(k)$, $a_k$, $b_k$ must be stored in a finite-length word in the DSP memory.
   - This causes the actual frequency response to **deviate** from the ideal response (pole-zero displacement, passband ripple, etc.).

3. **Arithmetic Round-Off Error (Product Quantization Error)**
   - Occurs inside the DSP chip when multiplying two $B$-bit numbers produces a $2B$-bit product, which must be rounded or truncated back to $B$ bits.
   - In recursive (IIR) systems, these errors get **fed back and accumulated**, sometimes producing **limit cycle oscillations** and **overflow**.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Calculator with Limited Digits"
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a basic pocket calculator that displays only **8 digits**.
> - If you compute $1 \div 3 = 0.33333333\ldots$, the calculator truncates to $0.33333333$ — a **rounding error**.
> - If you compute $\pi \approx 3.14159265\ldots$ and store only $\pi \approx 3.1415927$ — a **coefficient quantization error**.
> - If you multiply two 8-digit numbers and the result has 16 digits, the calculator keeps only 8 — a **product round-off error**.
>
> Now, imagine doing this **a million times in a chain of multiplications and additions** (an IIR filter) — tiny errors snowball, poles drift, and the output can oscillate or even become unstable. This is exactly how FWL effects corrupt real DSP systems.

### Why FWL Effects Matter in Practice
> [!WARNING]
> **Real-world consequence:** A 16-bit audio CD player has $B=16$. The theoretical SQNR is $6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 \approx 98.08$ dB. A 24-bit professional system achieves $\approx 146$ dB. Drop to 8 bits (telephone-grade), and the noise floor is only $\approx 50$ dB — clearly audible hiss. **More bits = more fidelity, but more cost, power, and memory.** Mastering FWL analysis is the key to designing cost-effective DSP hardware without sacrificing performance.

### Key Standard Quantities (Universal in KTU Board Papers)

- **Full-scale range:** $V_{FS}$ (in Volts)
- **Number of bits:** $B$ (dimensionless, integer)
- **Quantization step / resolution:** $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^B}$
- **Quantization error range:** $-\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q(n) \leq +\dfrac{\Delta}{2}$
- **Mean Square Quantization Noise (MSQN):** $\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-by-Block Theoretical Analysis of the DSP Functional Diagram

### Block 1 — Input Transducer
- Converts physical energy (acoustic, optical, mechanical, thermal) into a voltage or current signal.
- The output is a **continuous-time, continuous-amplitude** signal $x(t)$.
- **Why needed:** DSP processors cannot directly process physical phenomena; a transducer is the universal "translator" into the electrical domain.

### Block 2 — Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)
- A low-pass analog filter (typically Butterworth, Chebyshev, or Bessel) with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$.
- **Why needed (Critical):** Real-world signals contain unlimited bandwidth. Sampling without band-limiting causes **aliasing** — high frequencies fold back into the baseband, irreversibly corrupting the signal. The Nyquist theorem strictly requires $x(t)$ to be band-limited to $f_s/2$ **before** sampling.
- **Real-world engineering utility:** Audio systems use AAF at 20 kHz before 44.1 kHz CD sampling. ECG/EEG medical monitors use AAF at 40–250 Hz.

### Block 3 — Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit
- Samples the input at instants $t = nT_s$ where $T_s = 1/f_s$ and holds the value constant for the ADC conversion time.
- **Why needed:** ADCs require a finite "aperture time" to perform conversion. If the input changes during conversion, the output is erroneous. The S/H freezes the signal to guarantee a stable, accurate conversion.

### Block 4 — Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)
- Performs two operations simultaneously:
  - **Sampling:** $x(t) \to x(nT_s) = x(n)$, an infinite-precision discrete-time signal.
  - **Quantization:** $x(n) \to x_Q(n)$, a finite-precision discrete-time signal.
- The **quantization error** $e_q(n) = x_Q(n) - x(n)$ is the **first** and most important FWL effect.
- **Why finite precision?** A $B$-bit register can only encode $2^B$ distinct levels. Any sample that falls between two adjacent levels must be rounded (or truncated) to the nearest level.

### Block 5 — Digital Signal Processor
- Executes the difference equation (FIR or IIR) on $x_Q(n)$ to produce $y(n)$.
- Internally performs:
  - Multiplications $x_Q(n) \cdot h(k)$, producing $2B$-bit products.
  - Additions/accumulations, producing wider sums.
  - **Re-quantization** back to $B$ bits at each node (or at the output).
- **Why this matters:** This is where **coefficient quantization** and **product round-off** errors are generated.

### Block 6 — Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)
- Converts the digital $y(n)$ into a staircase analog waveform $y_{\text{stair}}(t)$.
- Holds each digital value for one sampling period $T_s$, producing a **zero-order hold (ZOH)** output.

### Block 7 — Reconstruction / Anti-Imaging Filter
- A low-pass analog filter that removes the high-frequency spectral images (centered at $k \cdot f_s$, $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$) created by the ZOH.
- Recovers a smooth, continuous approximation $\hat{y}(t)$ of the original analog signal.
- **Why needed:** Without this filter, the output would be a harsh staircase, unsuitable for audio/sensor applications.

### Complete Signal Chain (KTU Board Sketch Standard)
$$x(t) \xrightarrow{\text{AAF}} x_{BL}(t) \xrightarrow{\text{S/H}} x(n) \xrightarrow{\text{ADC (Quantize)}} x_Q(n) \xrightarrow{\text{DSP}} y(n) \xrightarrow{\text{DAC}} y_{ZOH}(t) \xrightarrow{\text{Recon. Filter}} \hat{y}(t)$$

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Analysis of the Quantization Error Model

### Assumptions for the Standard Quantization Noise Model (KTU Mandated)
1. The quantization error $e_q(n) = x_Q(n) - x(n)$ is a **stationary** random process.
2. $e_q(n)$ is **uniformly distributed** over $\left[-\dfrac{\Delta}{2}, +\dfrac{\Delta}{2}\right]$.
3. $e_q(n)$ is **uncorrelated** with the signal $x(n)$ (reasonable for fine quantization, $B \geq 8$).
4. $e_q(n)$ is **white noise** — the error sequence is uncorrelated from sample to sample:
$$E[e_q(n)\, e_q(m)] = 0 \quad \text{for } n \neq m$$

### Mathematical Form of the Quantization Error
For **mid-tread** quantizers (zero is a representable level):
$$x_Q(n) = \Delta \cdot \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{x(n)}{\Delta}\right)$$

For **mid-rise** quantizers (zero is not a representable level):
$$x_Q(n) = \Delta \cdot \text{floor}\!\left(\dfrac{x(n)}{\Delta}\right) + \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$$

In both cases:
$$e_q(n) = x_Q(n) - x(n), \quad -\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q(n) \leq +\dfrac{\Delta}{2}$$

### Power (Mean Square) of the Quantization Noise
The variance of a uniform distribution on $[-a, +a]$ is $\sigma^2 = a^2/3$. Here $a = \Delta/2$:
$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \dfrac{(\Delta/2)^2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1} = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

The mean of the error is zero: $\mu_{e_q} = 0$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board Exam Lifesaver)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastery of these formulas guarantees 80% of Module-4 marks.** Memorize the derivations — KTU board examiners frequently ask "Derive the expression for SQNR."

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampling frequency | $f_s = 1/T_s$ | Hz |
| 2 | Nyquist criterion | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz |
| 3 | Quantization step size | $\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^B$ | V (volts) |
| 4 | Normalized step size | $\Delta = 2^{-B}$ | dimensionless (if $V_{FS}=1$) |
| 5 | Quantization error bound | $-\Delta/2 \leq e_q(n) \leq +\Delta/2$ | V |
| 6 | Mean of error | $\mu_{e_q} = 0$ | — |
| 7 | Variance (power) of error | $\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \Delta^2/12$ | $V^2$ |
| 8 | RMS quantization noise | $e_{q,\text{rms}} = \Delta / \sqrt{12}$ | V |
| 9 | Signal power (full-scale sine) | $\sigma_x^2 = V_{FS}^2/8$ | $V^2$ |
| 10 | Signal power (uniform distribution) | $\sigma_x^2 = V_{FS}^2/12$ | $V^2$ |
| 11 | SQNR (general) | $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_{e_q}^2}\right)$ | dB |
| 12 | SQNR (full-scale sine) | $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\, B + 1.76$ | dB |
| 13 | SQNR (uniform signal) | $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\, B$ | dB |
| 14 | SQNR per bit | $6.02$ dB/bit (sine), $6.02$ dB/bit (uniform) | dB/bit |
| 15 | Coefficient sensitivity (linear) | $\Delta H = \sum_k \dfrac{\partial H}{\partial h_k} \Delta h_k$ | — |
| 16 | Pole-radius sensitivity | $\dfrac{\partial \vert r \vert}{\partial a_k} \approx \vert r \vert^{n-1}$ | recursive filter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal KTU Rule of Thumb:** *Every additional bit of ADC resolution gives approximately **6 dB** of SQNR improvement.* This is the most-cited number in KTU board papers.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of FWL Analysis

| Domain | Typical Word Length $B$ | Consequence of FWL Errors |
|---|---|---|
| **Telephony (PCM)** | 8 bits ($\mu$-law) | Audible hiss; SQNR $\approx 50$ dB |
| **Audio CDs (Red Book)** | 16 bits | Hi-fi quality; SQNR $\approx 98$ dB |
| **Professional Audio (DVD-A)** | 24 bits | Studio quality; SQNR $\approx 146$ dB |
| **Medical Imaging (MRI, CT)** | 12–16 bits | Diagnostic accuracy; minor round-off tolerated |
| **Military Radar / SDR** | 14–16 bits | Dynamic range critical for weak target detection |
| **Low-power IoT / MCUs** | 8–12 bits | Cost-driven; FWL analysis vital to choose minimum $B$ |
| **Aerospace Control Loops** | 16–32 bits floating-point | Stability margins must account for round-off |

> [!NOTE]
> The **engineering trade-off** is universal: increase $B$ → more fidelity, more cost, more power, more silicon area. FWL analysis is the **mathematical tool** that lets the designer find the **minimum $B$ that meets the SQNR specification**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Variance of the Quantization Error $e_q(n)$

### Given
- $e_q(n)$ is uniformly distributed on $\left[-\dfrac{\Delta}{2}, +\dfrac{\Delta}{2}\right]$.
- The probability density function (PDF) is:
$$p(e_q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{\Delta}, & -\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### Step 1 — Verify the PDF is normalized
$$\int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} p(e_q)\, de_q = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} \dfrac{1}{\Delta}\, de_q = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \Delta = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 2 — Compute the mean $\mu_{e_q}$
$$\mu_{e_q} = E[e_q] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q \cdot p(e_q)\, de_q = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q \cdot \dfrac{1}{\Delta}\, de_q$$

$$\mu_{e_q} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \left[\dfrac{e_q^2}{2}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \left(\dfrac{\Delta^2}{8} - \dfrac{\Delta^2}{8}\right) = 0$$

Hence, the quantization error is **zero-mean** — a critical KTU assumption.

### Step 3 — Compute the second moment $E[e_q^2]$
$$E[e_q^2] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot p(e_q)\, de_q = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2\, de_q$$

$$\int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2\, de_q = \left[\dfrac{e_q^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \dfrac{(\Delta/2)^3}{3} - \dfrac{(-\Delta/2)^3}{3} = \dfrac{\Delta^3}{24} + \dfrac{\Delta^3}{24} = \dfrac{\Delta^3}{12}$$

Therefore:
$$E[e_q^2] = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta^3}{12} = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the variance
Since $\mu_{e_q} = 0$:
$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = E[e_q^2] - \mu_{e_q}^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12} - 0 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

### Step 5 — Express in terms of $B$
Since $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^B$:
$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \dfrac{1}{12}\left(\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^B}\right)^2 = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 4^B}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $\boxed{\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12} = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 2^{2B}}}$. This shows that the noise power **doubles for every 1-bit decrease** in $B$ (since $2^{2B} = 4^B$, halving $B$ multiplies noise by 4 = **6 dB**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: SQNR for a Full-Scale Sinusoidal Input

### Given
- Input signal: $x(n) = A \sin(2\pi f n T_s)$, with $A = V_{FS}/2$ (full-scale amplitude).
- Variance of a sinusoid of amplitude $A$: $\sigma_x^2 = A^2/2$.
- Variance of quantization noise: $\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \Delta^2/12$ with $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^B = 2A/2^B$.

### Step 1 — Write the signal power
$$\sigma_x^2 = \dfrac{A^2}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Write the noise power
$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \dfrac{1}{12}\left(\dfrac{2A}{2^B}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{12} \cdot \dfrac{4A^2}{2^{2B}} = \dfrac{A^2}{3 \cdot 2^{2B}}$$

### Step 3 — Form the SQNR ratio
$$\text{SQNR} = \dfrac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_{e_q}^2} = \dfrac{A^2/2}{A^2/(3 \cdot 2^{2B})} = \dfrac{3 \cdot 2^{2B}}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to decibels
$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3 \cdot 2^{2B}}{2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(3) - 10 \log_{10}(2) + 20 B \log_{10}(2)$$

Using the standard values:
- $10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3.0103 \approx 3.01$
- $10 \log_{10}(3) \approx 4.7712 \approx 4.77$
- $20 \log_{10}(2) \approx 6.0206 \approx 6.02$

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 4.77 - 3.01 + 6.02 B = 1.76 + 6.02 B$$

### Step 5 — Final boxed result
$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\, B + 1.76 \text{ dB (full-scale sinusoidal input)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Special Note:** The "**1.76 dB**" comes from $10\log_{10}(1.5) = 1.76$. Many students mistakenly write $10\log_{10}(3/2)$ — both are correct, just different forms. The **6.02 dB per bit** is the universally accepted "rule of thumb."

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SQNR for a Uniformly Distributed Signal

### Given
- $x(n)$ is uniformly distributed on $[-V_{FS}/2, +V_{FS}/2]$ (uses the full scale, no clipping).
- Variance of a uniform distribution on $[-a, +a]$ is $a^2/3$. So:
$$\sigma_x^2 = \dfrac{(V_{FS}/2)^2}{3} = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2}{12}$$

### Step 1 — Form the ratio
$$\text{SQNR} = \dfrac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_{e_q}^2} = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2/12}{V_{FS}^2/(12 \cdot 2^{2B})} = 2^{2B}$$

### Step 2 — Convert to decibels
$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(2^{2B}) = 20 B \log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02 B$$

### Final boxed result
$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\, B \text{ dB (uniformly distributed input)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** For a uniform signal, the constant term **1.76 dB is absent**, because a sinusoid's peak-to-RMS ratio is worse than a uniform signal's. Engineers use the **+1.76 dB version** as a more conservative (pessimistic) estimate of system performance.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Board Numerical Problems

### Problem Type 1: Given $B$, find $\Delta$ and noise power
- $B = 12$, $V_{FS} = 10$ V.
- $\Delta = 10 / 2^{12} = 10/4096 \approx 2.44$ mV.
- $\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \Delta^2/12 = (2.44 \times 10^{-3})^2/12 \approx 4.97 \times 10^{-7} V^2$.

### Problem Type 2: Find $B$ for a target SQNR
- Required SQNR $\geq 80$ dB, full-scale sine input.
- $6.02 B + 1.76 \geq 80 \Rightarrow B \geq 13.0$. So $B = 14$ bits (always round up).

### Problem Type 3: Find $B$ given allowable RMS noise
- Maximum $e_{\text{rms}} = 1$ mV, $V_{FS} = 5$ V.
- $e_{\text{rms}} = \Delta/\sqrt{12} = V_{FS}/(2^B \sqrt{12}) \leq 10^{-3}$.
- Solving: $2^B \geq 5/(10^{-3} \cdot \sqrt{12}) \approx 1443.4$.
- Hence $B \geq \lceil \log_2(1443.4) \rceil = \lceil 10.49 \rceil = 11$ bits.

### Problem Type 4: Total input-referred noise power for $N$-sample IIR filter
- For a cascade of $N$ independent quantizers (white noise assumption), total noise power = $N \cdot \sigma_{e_q}^2 = N \Delta^2/12$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Complete Functional Block Diagram of a DSP System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Signal\nSound, Light, Pressure] --> B[Transducer\nSensor / Microphone]
    B --> C[Analog Signal\nx of t]
    C --> D[Anti-Aliasing Filter\nLow-pass, fc less than fs/2]
    D --> E[Sample and Hold\nSampled at fs]
    E --> F[ADC\nSampling plus Quantization]
    F --> G[Digital Signal\nxQ of n with B bits]
    G --> H[DSP Processor\nFIR, IIR, FFT, Algorithm]
    H --> I[Digital Output\ny of n with B bits]
    I --> J[DAC\nZero Order Hold]
    J --> K[Staircase Analog\nyZOH of t]
    K --> L[Reconstruction Filter\nLow-pass Smoothing]
    L --> M[Clean Analog Output\ny hat of t]
    M --> N[Output Actuator\nSpeaker, Display, Motor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block introduces its own potential errors. The **red-flag blocks** (where FWL effects originate) are the **ADC** (Block F), the **DSP Processor** (Block H), and the **DAC** (Block J). All other blocks are analog-domain or interface blocks and are not directly affected by digital word length.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Sources of Finite Word Length Effects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Finite Word Length Effects in DSP] --> S1[1. ADC Input Quantization Error]
    R --> S2[2. Coefficient Quantization Error]
    R --> S3[3. Arithmetic Product Round Off Error]
    
    S1 --> S1a[Error eq at ADC output]
    S1 --> S1b[Modeled as additive white noise]
    S1 --> S1c[Power Delta squared over 12]
    S1 --> S1d[Affects SNR of digitized signal]
    
    S2 --> S2a[Designed coefficients truncated to B bits]
    S2 --> S2b[Actual poles and zeros shift from ideal locations]
    S2 --> S2c[Frequency response deviates from specification]
    S2 --> S2d[Can cause instability in IIR filters]
    
    S3 --> S3a[B by B multiplication gives 2B bit product]
    S3 --> S3b[Re-quantization back to B bits introduces error]
    S3 --> S3c[In IIR, errors get fed back and accumulated]
    S3 --> S3d[Can cause limit cycle oscillations]
    S3 --> S3e[Can cause overflow and saturation]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Quantization Error Model (Additive Noise)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Continuous Amplitude x of n] --> Q[Quantizer B bits]
    X -.->|+ eq n .-> SUM(( Summation Junction ))
    Q --> XQ[Discrete Amplitude xQ of n]
    Q --> EQ[Quantization Error eq n]
    EQ --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Modeled Output]
    
    style EQ fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
    style SUM fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The **quantization model** replaces the complex, nonlinear quantizer with a simple **additive white noise** source $e_q(n)$. This linearization is the foundation of the entire SQNR analysis taught in KTU Module 4. It is valid **only when** the input signal is "busy" (many levels are exercised) and $B$ is reasonably large ($\geq 8$).

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Hierarchical View of the DSP System (Module-4 Mind Map)

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((DSP System Block Diagram and Finite Word Length))
    Functional Blocks
      Transducer
      Anti Aliasing Filter
      Sample and Hold
      ADC
      DSP Processor
      DAC
      Reconstruction Filter
    Finite Word Length Sources
      ADC Quantization
        Step size Delta
        Error bound Delta over 2
        Noise power Delta squared over 12
      Coefficient Quantization
        Pole zero shift
        Frequency response error
      Arithmetic Round Off
        Product truncation
        Limit cycles
        Overflow
    Key Performance Metric
      SQNR in dB
        6.02 B plus 1.76 for sine
        6.02 B for uniform
        6 dB per bit rule
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List the functional blocks of a typical DSP system. State the purpose of the anti-aliasing filter.**

**Model Answer:**

A typical DSP system consists of the following functional blocks in sequence:

1. **Input Transducer** — converts the physical signal (sound, light, pressure) into an electrical analog signal.
2. **Anti-Aliasing Filter** — a low-pass analog filter that band-limits the input signal.
3. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit** — samples the band-limited signal at instants $nT_s$.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — performs sampling and quantization; outputs a binary digital signal.
5. **Digital Signal Processor** — executes the DSP algorithm (filtering, FFT, etc.) on the digital input.
6. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** — converts the processed digital output back into a staircase analog waveform.
7. **Reconstruction (Anti-Imaging) Filter** — a low-pass filter that smooths the DAC output and removes spectral images.

**Purpose of the Anti-Aliasing Filter:**
The anti-aliasing filter is a low-pass analog filter placed **before** the sampler. Its purpose is to remove all frequency components of the input signal that lie above the **Nyquist frequency** $f_s/2$. This is essential to satisfy the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**:
$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$
If high-frequency components are not removed **before** sampling, they will **fold back (alias)** into the baseband $[0, f_s/2]$, producing an irreversible distortion of the signal. The AAF thus prevents aliasing and ensures faithful digital representation of the analog signal.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define finite word length effects. List any three sources of finite word length effects in DSP systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
**Finite Word Length (FWL) Effects** refer to the quantization errors and performance degradations that occur when a digital signal processing system, which is mathematically designed assuming infinite-precision arithmetic, is physically implemented using registers and arithmetic units of a fixed, finite number of bits $B$.

**Three sources of FWL effects:**

1. **ADC Input Quantization Error:**
   The continuous-amplitude sampled signal $x(n)$ must be rounded to one of $2^B$ discrete levels. This introduces the quantization error:
$$e_q(n) = x_Q(n) - x(n), \quad -\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q(n) \leq +\dfrac{\Delta}{2}$$
   with noise power $\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \Delta^2/12$.

2. **Coefficient Quantization Error:**
   The designed filter coefficients $h(k)$, $a_k$, $b_k$ are stored in $B$-bit memory. The quantized coefficients differ from the ideal ones, causing the actual frequency response to deviate from the specification and possibly shifting poles/zeros into unstable regions (in IIR filters).

3. **Arithmetic Round-Off (Product Quantization) Error:**
   Multiplication of two $B$-bit numbers produces a $2B$-bit product, which must be re-quantized to $B$ bits for storage. In recursive (IIR) structures, these round-off errors are fed back and can accumulate, leading to **limit cycle oscillations** and **overflow**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, Q4(a)-or-(b) pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Draw the functional block diagram of a real-time DSP system and explain the function of each block in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a suitable model, derive the expression for the signal-to-quantization noise ratio (SQNR) of a $B$-bit ADC for a full-scale sinusoidal input. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to (a): Functional Block Diagram and Block Functions

**Diagram (re-sketched for answer script):**

```
Physical Signal -> Transducer -> Anti-Aliasing Filter -> Sample-and-Hold
                                                          |
                                                          v
                          [DSP Processor] <- ADC (Quantizer)
                                |
                                v
                              DAC -> Reconstruction Filter -> Output
```

**Functions of each block:**

1. **Transducer / Sensor:** Converts the physical signal (acoustic, optical, mechanical, thermal) into an electrical analog signal $x(t)$ — a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude voltage or current.

2. **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A low-pass analog filter with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$. It band-limits the input to satisfy the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem and prevents aliasing.

3. **Sample-and-Hold Circuit:** Samples the band-limited signal at $t = nT_s$ and holds each value for the duration of A/D conversion. The output is a discrete-time, continuous-amplitude (PAM) signal.

4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter:** Performs both sampling and quantization; outputs a binary-coded, discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital signal $x_Q(n)$ in $B$-bit words.

5. **Digital Signal Processor:** Executes the desired algorithm (FIR, IIR, FFT, convolution) on $x_Q(n)$ to produce $y(n)$. Performs multiplications, additions, and re-quantization internally.

6. **Digital-to-Analog Converter:** Converts the digital $y(n)$ into a staircase analog waveform $y_{\text{ZOH}}(t)$ using zero-order hold.

7. **Reconstruction (Anti-Imaging) Filter:** A low-pass filter that smooths the staircase output, removing spectral images centered at multiples of $f_s$, to produce a clean analog output $\hat{y}(t)$.

#### Solution to (b): Derivation of SQNR for Full-Scale Sine Input

**Step 1 — Quantization step size:**
$$\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^B}$$

**Step 2 — Signal model:**
Full-scale sinusoid: $x(n) = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2} \sin(2\pi f n T_s)$.

**Step 3 — Signal power:**
$$\sigma_x^2 = \dfrac{A^2}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2}{8}$$

**Step 4 — Quantization noise power (uniform distribution model):**
$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12} = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 2^{2B}}$$

**Step 5 — Form the SQNR ratio:**
$$\text{SQNR} = \dfrac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_{e_q}^2} = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2/8}{V_{FS}^2/(12 \cdot 2^{2B})} = \dfrac{12 \cdot 2^{2B}}{8} = \dfrac{3 \cdot 2^{2B}}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to dB:**
$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3 \cdot 2^{2B}}{2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20 B \log_{10}(2)$$

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.76 + 6.02 B \quad \text{dB}$$

**Step 7 — Final boxed answer:**
$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\, B + 1.76 \text{ dB}}$$

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, alternative pattern]**

**Q. (a)** What is meant by quantization error? Derive the expression for the mean square value of the quantization error of a $B$-bit uniform quantizer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 12-bit ADC with full-scale range $\pm 5$ V, compute (i) the quantization step size, (ii) the RMS quantization noise, (iii) the SQNR in dB for a full-scale sinusoidal input, and (iv) the SQNR in dB for a uniformly distributed input. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to (a): Mean Square Value of Quantization Error

**Definition:**
The **quantization error** $e_q(n)$ is the difference between the quantized value $x_Q(n)$ and the true continuous-amplitude sample $x(n)$:
$$e_q(n) = x_Q(n) - x(n)$$

For a $B$-bit uniform quantizer, the error is bounded by:
$$-\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q(n) \leq +\dfrac{\Delta}{2}, \quad \Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^B}$$

**Step 1 — Model:** Assume $e_q(n)$ is uniformly distributed on $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$. The PDF is:
$$p(e_q) = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \quad \text{for } e_q \in [-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$$

**Step 2 — Mean:**
$$\mu_{e_q} = E[e_q] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q \cdot \dfrac{1}{\Delta}\, de_q = 0$$

**Step 3 — Mean Square Value:**
$$E[e_q^2] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\Delta}\, de_q = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left[\dfrac{e_q^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta^3}{12} = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**
$$\boxed{\sigma_{e_q}^2 = E[e_q^2] = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12} = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 2^{2B}}}$$

#### Solution to (b): Numerical Problem

**Given:** $B = 12$, $V_{FS} = 10$ V (full range is $\pm 5$ V, so $V_{FS} = 10$ V).

**(i) Quantization step size:**
$$\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^B} = \dfrac{10}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{10}{4096} \approx 2.4414 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 2.44 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) RMS quantization noise:**
$$e_{q,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{2.4414 \times 10^{-3}}{3.4641} \approx 7.047 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \approx 0.7047 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) SQNR for full-scale sine:**
$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 B + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

**(iv) SQNR for uniform input:**
$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 B = 6.02 \times 12 = 72.24 \text{ dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $V_{FS}$ with peak amplitude:** When the problem says "$\pm 5$ V full-scale", students often use $V_{FS} = 5$ V. The **correct** value is $V_{FS} = 10$ V (peak-to-peak range).
> 2. **Forgetting to round up the number of bits:** When asked "find $B$ for SQNR $\geq 80$ dB", students compute $B = 13.0$ and write $B = 13$. The **correct** answer is $B = 14$ (always round up; $B = 13$ gives only $79.02$ dB, which is **less** than 80 dB).
> 3. **Mixing up SQNR formulas:** For full-scale sine, use $6.02B + 1.76$. For uniform input, use $6.02B$. Many students write $6.02B + 1.76$ for both, losing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the units:** $\Delta$ in volts, $\sigma_{e_q}^2$ in $V^2$, $\text{SQNR}$ in dB. Examiners explicitly deduct for missing units.
> 5. **Sign error in $e_q$ bound:** Writing $0 \leq e_q \leq \Delta$ instead of $-\Delta/2 \leq e_q \leq +\Delta/2$ — this completely changes the variance result from $\Delta^2/12$ to $\Delta^2/3$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Bank — Glance at this 2 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] A DSP system has **7 blocks**: Transducer, AAF, S/H, ADC, DSP, DAC, Reconstruction filter.
- [ ] **Anti-aliasing filter is mandatory before sampling**; cutoff $\leq f_s/2$ (Nyquist rule).
- [ ] **Sample-and-Hold** freezes the signal during A/D conversion to prevent aperture error.
- [ ] **Three sources of FWL errors:** (1) ADC quantization, (2) Coefficient quantization, (3) Product round-off.
- [ ] Quantization step: $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^B$.
- [ ] Error bound: $-\Delta/2 \leq e_q(n) \leq +\Delta/2$.
- [ ] Noise power: $\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \Delta^2/12$ (uniform distribution, white noise assumption).
- [ ] **SQNR (sine) = $6.02B + 1.76$ dB**; **SQNR (uniform) = $6.02B$ dB**.
- [ ] **Every additional bit adds 6 dB of SQNR** — universal KTU "rule of thumb."
- [ ] Quantization noise is modeled as **additive white noise**, **zero-mean**, **uniformly distributed**, and **uncorrelated with the signal** (4 KTU assumptions).
- [ ] For $V_{FS} = \pm 5$ V, use $V_{FS} = 10$ V (not 5 V) in formulas.
- [ ] For target SQNR, **always round up** the bit count to the next integer.
- [ ] IIR (recursive) filters are **more sensitive** to FWL effects than FIR filters, due to feedback of round-off errors.
- [ ] Limit cycles and overflow are critical FWL concerns **only in IIR** filters.
- [ ] For SNR problems, remember to convert **power ratios to dB** using $10 \log_{10}(\cdot)$.
