- [ ] $10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3.01$; $20 \log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02$; $10 \log_{10}(1.5) \approx 1.76$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closing remark:** Mastery of this module is foundational for VLSI design, real-time embedded DSP, and audio/speech processing careers. The 6 dB-per-bit rule is the most-cited single number in any DSP interview. **Practice at least 5 numerical problems** from KTU previous-year papers covering the three formula categories (sine SQNR, uniform SQNR, target-$B$ problems) before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed-point and Floating-point DSP Arithmetic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT504 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fixed-point and Floating-point DSP Arithmetic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Fixed-Point and Floating-Point DSP Arithmetic

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fixed-Point Arithmetic** in DSP refers to a method of representing real numbers using a **fixed number of digits** (bits) after the binary (or decimal) point, where the scaling factor is **predetermined and static** throughout the computation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Floating-Point Arithmetic** refers to a numerical representation where each real number is expressed as a **mantissa (significand) multiplied by a base raised to an exponent**, allowing the decimal/binary point to "float" — i.e., the dynamic range is determined dynamically by the exponent at run-time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to analyze the trade-offs between fixed-point and floating-point implementations, evaluate quantization noise, scaling techniques, dynamic range, and precision. The board examiner expects direct definitions of Q-format, IEEE-754 single precision, and SNR derivations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are measuring the length of a table using two different rulers:

**Fixed-Point Ruler (Static Precision):**
Think of a ruler marked from **0.00 cm to 99.99 cm** — a total range of only **2 meters**, but you can always measure with **precision of 0.01 cm**. The position of the decimal point **never moves**. If the table is 2.5 meters long, this ruler simply **overflows** and tells you "ERROR". This is exactly how fixed-point arithmetic behaves: high **predictable precision**, but limited **dynamic range**.

**Floating-Point Ruler (Adaptive Precision):**
Now imagine a scientific caliper. If the object is tiny (say 0.0025 m), it auto-shifts to read **2.5 mm × 10⁻³ m**. If the object is huge (say 2500 m), it auto-shifts to read **2.5 km × 10³ m**. The decimal point **floats** based on the magnitude. This is floating-point arithmetic: massive **dynamic range** but the relative precision (% error) remains roughly constant.

| Property | Fixed-Point | Floating-Point |
|----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Decimal Point** | Static | Dynamic (floating) |
| **Dynamic Range** | Small | Very Large |
| **Precision** | Uniform & Predictable | Proportional to magnitude |
| **Hardware Cost** | Cheap, fast | Expensive, complex |
| **Power Consumption** | Low | High |
| **Used in** | Real-time embedded DSPs, mobile phones | Desktop computing, prototyping |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to remember:**
> - **Word Length (B)** = total bits in a register (e.g., 16-bit, 32-bit)
> - **Fractional Bits (b)** = bits after the binary point in Q-format
> - **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** measured in **dB** is the figure of merit
> - **1 LSB (Least Significant Bit)** = the smallest representable value
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fixed-Point Number Representation

### 2.1.1 Q-Format Notation (Q15, Q31)

The Q-format `Qm.n` means `m` integer bits (including sign) and `n` fractional bits.
- **Total Word Length:** $B = m + n$ bits
- **Range:** $[-2^{m-1}, \; 2^{m-1} - 2^{-n}]$
- **Resolution (1 LSB):** $\Delta = 2^{-n}$
- **Q15 (16-bit):** $m=1$, $n=15$ → range $[-1, \; 1 - 2^{-15}]$
- **Q31 (32-bit):** $m=1$, $n=31$ → range $[-1, \; 1 - 2^{-31}]$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two's Complement** is the universal standard for fixed-point DSP hardware because it uniquely represents zero and simplifies adder/subtractor hardware.

### 2.1.2 Two's Complement Conversion

For a B-bit signed number $N$ in two's complement:

$$
N = -N_{B-1} \cdot 2^{B-1} + \sum_{i=0}^{B-2} N_i \cdot 2^i
$$

The negative of a number is found by:
1. **Invert** all bits (one's complement)
2. **Add 1** to the LSB (two's complement)

### 2.1.3 Fixed-Point Addition

When two Q-format numbers of the same `m.n` format are added, the result requires `m+1` integer bits to avoid overflow:

$$
\text{Result Range} = [-2^m, \; 2^m - 2 \cdot 2^{-n}]
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Overflow** occurs when the sum exceeds the representable range. In two's complement, overflow wraps around causing a **catastrophic sign change** (e.g., $0x7FFF + 1 = 0x8000$, a small positive becomes a large negative). KTU examiners frequently test this!

### 2.1.4 Fixed-Point Multiplication

Multiplying two Q-format numbers produces a result with the fractional bits **summing**:

$$
Q_{m_1.n_1} \times Q_{m_2.n_2} = Q_{(m_1+m_2).(n_1+n_2)}
$$

The product requires **double the word length** to retain full precision. For example, $Q15 \times Q15 = Q30$ (a 32-bit product from two 16-bit operands).

> [!NOTE]
> **Truncation** discards LSBs (fast but introduces **downward bias**).
> **Rounding** to nearest LSB is unbiased but slightly more expensive in hardware.

## 2.2 Floating-Point Number Representation

### 2.2.1 IEEE 754 Single Precision (32-bit)

A real number $x$ is represented as:

$$
x = (-1)^S \times 1.M \times 2^{E - 127}
$$

| Field | Bits | Range |
|-------|------|-------|
| Sign (S) | 1 | 0 or 1 |
| Exponent (E) | 8 | Biased by **+127** |
| Mantissa (M) | 23 | Implicit leading 1 |

- **Largest value:** $\approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$
- **Smallest normal value:** $\approx 1.18 \times 10^{-38}$
- **Machine epsilon ($\varepsilon$):** $2^{-23} \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-7}$ (relative precision)

### 2.2.2 IEEE 754 Double Precision (64-bit)

| Field | Bits | Range |
|-------|------|-------|
| Sign (S) | 1 | 0 or 1 |
| Exponent (E) | 11 | Biased by **+1023** |
| Mantissa (M) | 52 | Implicit leading 1 |

Machine epsilon: $2^{-52} \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-16}$

### 2.2.3 Floating-Point Multiplication

$$
x_1 \cdot x_2 = (1.M_1)(1.M_2) \times 2^{(E_1 - \text{bias}) + (E_2 - \text{bias})}
$$

1. Multiply mantissas (with implicit 1 restoration).
2. **Add exponents**, subtract bias once (because it was added twice).
3. Normalize result (shift mantissa, adjust exponent).

### 2.2.4 Floating-Point Addition

Floating-point addition is **inherently more complex** than multiplication. The smaller number must first be **right-shifted** (denormalized) until exponents match, then mantissas are added, followed by renormalization.

## 2.3 Quantization Noise & SNR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantization Error** $e_q = x_q - x$ is the difference between the quantized value and the true value.
> - For **rounding**, $e_q \in [-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ where $\Delta = 2^{-n}$.
> - For **truncation** in two's complement, $e_q \in [-\Delta, 0]$ (always negative bias).

**Assumption (uniform white noise model):**

$$
E[e_q] = 0, \quad \sigma_e^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{2^{-2n}}{12}
$$

### 2.3.1 SNR for Fixed-Point Arithmetic

For a sinusoidal input of amplitude $A$, the **signal power** is $A^2/2$, and the **quantization noise power** is $\Delta^2/12$. The Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) is:

$$
\text{SQNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A^2/2}{\Delta^2/12}\right) = 6.02\,n + 1.76 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The famous 6-dB rule:** Each additional bit of precision (n) improves SQNR by **6.02 dB**. This formula is a KTU-favorite derivation.

### 2.3.2 SNR for Floating-Point Arithmetic

Floating-point quantization error is **proportional to the magnitude** of the signal, not a fixed $\Delta$:

$$
\sigma_e^2 = \frac{\varepsilon^2}{12} \cdot \sigma_x^2
$$

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{float}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{12}{\varepsilon^2}\right) \approx 6.02\,m + 1.76 \text{ dB}
$$

where $m$ is the number of mantissa bits. The SNR is **independent of signal level** — this is the killer advantage of floating-point.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` in all equations to avoid markdown breakage. The cheat sheet below mirrors what KTU examiners expect you to memorize.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Q-format value | $x = \sum_{i=-n}^{m-1} b_i \, 2^i$ | Two's complement |
| 2 | Q-format range | $\vert x \vert_{\max} = 2^{m-1} - 2^{-n}$ | One's complement varies |
| 3 | Q-format resolution | $\Delta = 2^{-n}$ | Smallest step |
| 4 | Quantization noise variance | $\sigma_e^2 = \Delta^2 / 12$ | Uniform rounding model |
| 5 | Fixed-point SQNR | $6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB | $n$ = fractional bits |
| 6 | Floating-point SQNR | $6.02\,m + 1.76$ dB | $m$ = mantissa bits |
| 7 | IEEE 754 exponent bias (single) | $\text{bias} = 127$ | $2^{8-1} - 1$ |
| 8 | IEEE 754 exponent bias (double) | $\text{bias} = 1023$ | $2^{11-1} - 1$ |
| 9 | Dynamic range (fixed) | $20 \log_{10}(2^{B-1})$ dB | B-bit word |
| 10 | Dynamic range (float, single) | $\approx 1536$ dB | From $10^{-38}$ to $10^{38}$ |
| 11 | Product word length | $B_{\text{product}} = B_1 + B_2$ | Q-format multiplication |
| 12 | Sum word length | $B_{\text{sum}} = \max(B_1, B_2) + 1$ | Q-format addition |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fixed-point DSPs** (e.g., TMS320C55x, SHARC fixed-point variants) dominate **mobile audio codecs, hearing aids, automotive ECUs, and IoT edge devices** due to low power, deterministic latency, and small silicon area.
- **Floating-point DSPs** (e.g., SHARC ADSP-21489, modern Cortex-M with FPU) are preferred for **radar, sonar, medical imaging, and adaptive filtering** where large dynamic range and easy prototyping are critical.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fixed-Point SQNR for a Sinusoidal Input

### Given
- A sinusoidal input $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$ quantized using an $n$-bit ADC (rounding).
- Quantization step $\Delta = 2^{-n}$ (assuming the signal is scaled to fill the full range $[-1, +1]$).

### Step 1 — Compute the Signal Power

For a sinusoid of amplitude $A$ with zero mean:

$$
P_s = E[x^2(t)] = \frac{A^2}{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Model Quantization Error as Uniform White Noise

Assume $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ with zero mean. The variance of a uniform distribution on $[a, b]$ is $\frac{(b-a)^2}{12}$:

$$
\sigma_e^2 = \frac{(\Delta/2 - (-\Delta/2))^2}{12} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

### Step 3 — Form the SNR Ratio

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{P_s}{\sigma_e^2} = \frac{A^2/2}{\Delta^2/12} = \frac{6 A^2}{\Delta^2}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Decibels

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{6 A^2}{\Delta^2}\right)
$$

Expanding the logarithm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} &= 10 \log_{10}(6) + 10 \log_{10}(A^2) - 10 \log_{10}(\Delta^2) \\
&= 7.78 + 20 \log_{10}(A) - 20 \log_{10}(\Delta)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $\Delta = 2^{-n}$ for Full-Scale Sinusoid (A ≈ 1)

When the signal uses the full-scale range ($A \approx 2^{n-1} \cdot \Delta$), the $A$ and $\Delta$ terms collapse. The canonical KTU result:

$$
\boxed{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 \text{ dB}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical check:** For $n = 16$, SNR ≈ $6.02(16) + 1.76 = 98.08$ dB. A 16-bit CD-quality audio achieves this dynamic range.

## 3.2 Derivation: Floating-Point SQNR (Relative Error Model)

### Step 1 — Express the Quantized Value

A floating-point number $\tilde{x}$ approximates $x$ such that:

$$
\tilde{x} = x (1 + \delta), \quad \vert \delta \vert \leq \varepsilon / 2
$$

where $\varepsilon = 2^{-m}$ is machine epsilon for an $m$-bit mantissa.

### Step 2 — Compute the Error Variance

Assuming $\delta$ is uniformly distributed in $[-\varepsilon/2, +\varepsilon/2]$:

$$
E[\delta] = 0, \quad \sigma_\delta^2 = \frac{\varepsilon^2}{12}
$$

The quantization error of $\tilde{x}$ is:

$$
e_q = \tilde{x} - x = x \delta
$$

So the noise power is signal-dependent:

$$
\sigma_e^2 = E[x^2 \delta^2] = \sigma_x^2 \cdot \sigma_\delta^2 = \sigma_x^2 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon^2}{12}
$$

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_x^2 \cdot \varepsilon^2/12} = \frac{12}{\varepsilon^2}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Decibels

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{12}{\varepsilon^2}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}(12) - 20 \log_{10}(\varepsilon) \\
&= 10.79 + 20 \log_{10}(2^m) \\
&= 10.79 + 6.02\,m
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB, float}} \approx 6.02\,m + 10.8 \text{ dB}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Floating-point SQNR depends **only on the number of mantissa bits $m$**, not on the input signal magnitude. This is why a 24-bit mantissa yields roughly 154 dB SNR — a figure impossible in fixed-point with any practical word length.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Operation-by-Operation Comparison

| Operation | Fixed-Point | Floating-Point |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Add/Subtract** | Simple integer adder, risk of overflow | Align exponents → add mantissas → renormalize |
| **Multiply** | Integer multiplier, **double word length** needed | Multiply mantissas, add exponents |
| **Cycle Count (typical 16-bit)** | 1 cycle (MAC) | 4–10 cycles |
| **Energy per MAC** | $\approx 1$ pJ | $\approx 10$–$50$ pJ |
| **Overflow Handling** | Saturate or wrap (KTU: prefer saturation for safety) | Soft saturation via exponent clipping |
| **Underflow** | Becomes zero | Gradual (denormals) or flush-to-zero |
| **Quantization Noise** | **Additive**, signal-independent | **Multiplicative**, signal-proportional |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Q15 Multiplication Overflow

Multiply $x = 0.75$ (Q15) and $y = 0.90$ (Q15).

**Step 1:** Convert to 16-bit integers.

$$
x = 0.75 \times 2^{15} = 24575, \quad y = 0.90 \times 2^{15} = 29491
$$

**Step 2:** Integer multiply (32-bit product):

$$
P = 24575 \times 29491 = 724{,}781{,}325
$$

**Step 3:** Convert product back to Q30:

$$
P_{\text{Q30}} = \frac{724781325}{2^{30}} = 0.6750
$$

**Step 4:** Truncate to Q15 for storage:

$$
P_{\text{Q15}} = \lfloor 0.6750 \times 2^{15} \rfloor = 22118 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{22118}{32768} \approx 0.6750
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The fractional part survives the Q15 × Q15 multiplication because the true product $0.75 \times 0.90 = 0.675$ lies in $[-1, +1]$ — no overflow. The upper 16 bits of the 32-bit product would give the Q16 value for use in accumulator chains (FIR filtering).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Fixed-Point MAC Unit (Multiply-Accumulate)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[16-bit X Input] --> MUL[16x16 Hardware Multiplier]
    B[16-bit Y Input] --> MUL
    MUL --> P[32-bit Product]
    P --> SHIFT[Barrel Shifter / Q-Format Scaler]
    SHIFT --> Q[Truncated or Rounded 16-bit Value]
    Q --> ACC[40-bit Accumulator]
    ACC --> SAT[Saturate to 16-bit Range]
    SAT --> OUT[16-bit Output Y]
    ACC --> FB[Feedback to ACC]
    FB --> ACC
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: IEEE 754 Floating-Point Multiplier

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    XA[X A: 32-bit operand] --> EXA[Extract Sign A, Exponent A, Mantissa A]
    XB[X B: 32-bit operand] --> EXB[Extract Sign B, Exponent B, Mantissa B]
    EXA --> SUMS[Sign Bit XOR S = SA XOR SB]
    EXB --> SUMS
    EXA --> ADDE[Adder for Exponents E_A + E_B - bias]
    EXB --> ADDE
    EXA --> MULM[24x24 Mantissa Multiplier with implicit 1]
    EXB --> MULM
    MULM --> NORM[Normalize and Round]
    ADDE --> NORM
    NORM --> PACK[Repack into IEEE 754 32-bit Word]
    SUMS --> PACK
    PACK --> OUT[Result F]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Floating-Point Adder (Exponent Alignment)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operand A: 32-bit] --> EA[Extract Exp A and Mant A with implicit 1]
    B[Operand B: 32-bit] --> EB[Extract Exp B and Mant B with implicit 1]
    EA --> CMP[Compare Exponents]
    EB --> CMP
    CMP --> SHIFT[Right-Shift Smaller Mantissa by Diff in Exponents]
    SHIFT --> ADD[Add or Subtract Mantissas]
    EA --> ADD
    EB --> ADD
    ADD --> RENORM[Renormalize: shift mantissa and adjust exponent]
    RENORM --> ROUND[Round to 24 mantissa bits]
    ROUND --> PACK2[Pack into IEEE 754 Format]
    PACK2 --> RES[Result]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Flowchart: Choosing Fixed vs. Floating Point

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: New DSP Design] --> Q1{Need Battery or Low Power?}
    Q1 -- YES --> FX[Choose Fixed-Point DSP]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Dynamic Range > 120 dB?}
    Q2 -- YES --> FL[Choose Floating-Point DSP]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Rapid Prototyping Priority?}
    Q3 -- YES --> FL2[Choose Floating-Point DSP]
    Q3 -- NO --> FX2[Choose Fixed-Point DSP]
    FX --> END1[Use Q15 or Q31 formats]
    FL --> END2[Use IEEE 754 single or double]
    FL2 --> END2
    FX2 --> END1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Differentiate between fixed-point and floating-point arithmetic in DSP. Mention two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fixed-Point | Floating-Point |
|--------|-------------|----------------|
| Decimal point position | Static | Dynamic |
| Word length | Typically 16 or 32 bits | 32 (single) or 64 (double) bits |
| Dynamic range | Limited (e.g., 90 dB for Q15) | Very high (≈1536 dB for single) |
| Hardware | Simple, low power | Complex, higher power |
| Precision | Uniform absolute precision | Uniform relative precision |

**Two advantages of fixed-point:** (1) Lower power consumption and faster execution, suitable for real-time embedded systems. (2) Deterministic behavior, simpler hardware, lower cost.

**Two advantages of floating-point:** (1) Vast dynamic range without manual scaling, ideal for algorithms with large amplitude variations. (2) Easier algorithm development and reduced risk of overflow/underflow.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the Q15 format? Write the range and resolution of a Q15 number.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Q15 format** is a 16-bit signed two's-complement fixed-point representation with 1 sign/integer bit and 15 fractional bits.

- **Range:** $[-1, \; 1 - 2^{-15}] = [-1, \; 0.999969]$
- **Resolution (1 LSB):** $\Delta = 2^{-15} = 1/32768 \approx 3.05 \times 10^{-5}$

The general value of a Q15 number stored as integer $N$ is:

$$
x = \frac{N}{2^{15}}
$$

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** forget to **normalize** floating-point results — failure to do so costs 2–3 marks.
> - **Do NOT** drop the implicit leading 1 when multiplying mantissas — it must be reinserted.
> - **For fixed-point products**, you must show the full 32-bit result before truncation/rounding.
> - **Always state assumptions** (uniform white noise, full-scale input) before applying the SNR formula.
> - Unit errors: dB ≠ linear ratio. Use $10 \log_{10}$ for power ratios, $20 \log_{10}$ for amplitude ratios.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

#### (a) Explain the IEEE 754 single-precision floating-point format with a neat block diagram. Convert the decimal number **−118.625** to IEEE 754 single-precision format. (7 marks)

#### (b) Derive the signal-to-quantization-noise ratio (SQNR) for a fixed-point uniform quantizer with a sinusoidal input. Show that each additional bit improves SQNR by approximately 6.02 dB. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**IEEE 754 Single Precision Layout (7 marks):**

| Field | Sign (S) | Exponent (E) | Mantissa (M) |
|-------|----------|--------------|--------------|
| Bits | 1 | 8 | 23 |

The number is represented as:

$$
x = (-1)^S \times 1.M \times 2^{E - 127}
$$

**Conversion of −118.625:**

**Step 1 — Sign bit:** Number is negative → $S = 1$.

**Step 2 — Convert integer part 118 to binary:**

$$
118 = 64 + 32 + 16 + 4 + 2 = 1110110_2
$$

**Step 3 — Convert fractional part 0.625 to binary:**

$$
0.625 \times 2 = 1.25 \rightarrow 1, \quad 0.25 \times 2 = 0.5 \rightarrow 0, \quad 0.5 \times 2 = 1.0 \rightarrow 1
$$

So $0.625_{10} = 0.101_2$.

**Step 4 — Combined binary:**

$$
118.625_{10} = 1110110.101_2
$$

**Step 5 — Normalize to $1.M \times 2^E$ form:**

$$
1110110.101 = 1.110110101 \times 2^6
$$

So the **mantissa** is $M = 110110101$ (followed by zeros to fill 23 bits) and the **unbiased exponent** is 6.

**Step 6 — Biased exponent (add 127):**

$$
E_{\text{biased}} = 6 + 127 = 133 = 10000101_2
$$

**Step 7 — Final 32-bit word:**

| Sign | Exponent | Mantissa |
|------|----------|----------|
| 1 | 10000101 | 11011010100000000000000 |

Hexadecimal: $\text{C2ED4000}_{16}$.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Let $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$ be the input sinusoid. Its mean square value (signal power) is:

$$
P_s = E[x^2(t)] = \frac{A^2}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Quantization error $e_q$ is modeled as a uniform random variable in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ with zero mean. Variance:

$$
\sigma_e^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}, \quad \text{where } \Delta = 2^{-n}
$$

**Step 3:** The signal-to-noise ratio (linear) is:

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{P_s}{\sigma_e^2} = \frac{A^2/2}{\Delta^2/12} = \frac{6 A^2}{\Delta^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Take $10 \log_{10}$:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{6 A^2}{\Delta^2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(6) + 20 \log_{10}(A) - 20 \log_{10}(\Delta)
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $A = 2^{n-1} \cdot \Delta$ (full-scale sinusoid where amplitude uses all $n$ bits):

$$
20 \log_{10}(A) - 20 \log_{10}(\Delta) = 20 \log_{10}(2^{n-1}) = 20 (n-1) \log_{10}(2) = 6.02(n-1)
$$

**Step 6:** Combine terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} &= 10 \log_{10}(6) + 6.02(n-1) \\
&= 7.78 + 6.02 n - 6.02 \\
&= 6.02 n + 1.76
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 \text{ dB}}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:** Differentiating with respect to $n$:

$$
\frac{d(\text{SNR})}{dn} = 6.02 \text{ dB per bit}
$$

This proves that every additional fractional bit adds approximately **6.02 dB** of SQNR — the famous 6-dB-per-bit rule.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Retest Model Paper]**

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the fixed-point MAC (Multiply-Accumulate) unit used in DSP processors. Discuss how overflow is handled in two's-complement fixed-point arithmetic. (7 marks)

#### (b) A 14-bit ADC is used to digitize a full-scale sinusoid. Compute the theoretical SQNR in dB. If the same system is implemented in IEEE 754 single-precision floating-point, what is the SQNR? Comment on the difference. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Fixed-Point MAC Unit (7 marks):**

The **Multiply-Accumulate (MAC)** unit is the heart of every DSP processor. It performs $Y = \sum_{i} a_i \cdot x_i$ in a single cycle.

**Block diagram description:**

1. **Input registers** $X$ and $A$ hold 16-bit operands.
2. **Hardware multiplier** computes a 32-bit product $P = X \times A$.
3. **Barrel shifter** aligns $P$ to the accumulator format (Q-format scaling).
4. **Accumulator (typically 40-bit wide)** adds the shifted product to its current value.
5. **Saturator** clips the accumulator output to the 16-bit range $[-32768, +32767]$ to prevent overflow wrap-around.
6. The accumulator is **fed back** to itself for the next accumulation cycle.

**Overflow Handling in Two's Complement:**

Two's-complement overflow causes the sign bit to **incorrectly flip**, leading to large erroneous magnitudes. Two solutions are employed:

- **Saturation arithmetic:** When overflow is detected (the carry into the sign bit differs from the carry out), the result is clamped to the maximum positive ($+2^{B-1} - 1$) or minimum negative ($-2^{B-1}$) value. This is the **safer** choice for control systems and audio.
- **Wrap-around (modular):** The result simply overflows and the extra bits are discarded. This is **mathematically correct in modular arithmetic** but produces large sign errors if the true sum exceeds the range.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern DSPs (e.g., TMS320C67xx) have a **SAT bit** in the control register to switch between saturation and wrap modes. The KTU examiner expects you to mention both strategies.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**SQNR Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Fixed-point SQNR with n = 14 bits:**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 \times 14 + 1.76 = 84.28 + 1.76 = 86.04 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Floating-point SQNR (IEEE 754 single, mantissa bits m = 23):**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB, float}} \approx 6.02 \times 23 + 10.8 = 138.46 + 10.8 \approx 149.3 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Difference and Commentary:**

$$
\Delta \text{SNR} = 149.3 - 86.04 \approx 63.3 \text{ dB}
$$

**Comments:**

1. The floating-point implementation provides **roughly 63 dB more dynamic range**, equivalent to about 10.5 extra bits of precision.
2. Floating-point SNR is **independent of signal level** — a tiny signal or a near-clipping signal both achieve the same SQNR. Fixed-point SQNR degrades by 6 dB for every **halving** of the input amplitude (because $\sigma_e^2$ stays the same while $P_s$ halves).
3. However, floating-point MAC units consume **5–10× more power** and **3–5× more silicon area**.
4. For a 14-bit ADC, **fixed-point arithmetic is sufficient** because the quantizer itself already limits SNR to ≈86 dB. Using floating-point here would be **over-engineering** with no quality benefit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical rule of thumb:** Match the word length of the fixed-point processor to the ADC resolution. A 14-bit ADC feeding a 16-bit DSP (with a 2-bit headroom for filter gain) is the standard design choice.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Fixed & Floating Point DSP Arithmetic**

- **Fixed-point** = static decimal point, predictable, low power, limited dynamic range; uses **Q-format (Qm.n)** with two's complement.
- **Floating-point** = dynamic decimal point, huge dynamic range (~1500 dB), uses **IEEE 754 single (32-bit, bias 127) or double (64-bit, bias 1023)** format.
- **Q-format conversion:** Integer value $N$ represents real $x = N \cdot 2^{-n}$ for Q-format `*.n`.
- **Fixed-point product word length:** $B_{\text{product}} = B_1 + B_2$ (e.g., Q15 × Q15 = Q30 in 32 bits).
- **Fixed-point sum word length:** $B_{\text{sum}} = \max(B_1, B_2) + 1$ bit to prevent overflow.
- **Quantization step (resolution):** $\Delta = 2^{-n}$ for $n$ fractional bits.
- **Quantization noise variance (uniform rounding):** $\sigma_e^2 = \Delta^2 / 12$.
- **Fixed-point SQNR:** $6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB; the **6.02 dB-per-bit rule** is a derivation target.
- **Floating-point SQNR:** $6.02\,m + 10.8$ dB; depends only on mantissa bits $m$.
- **Floating-point multiplication:** Add exponents (subtract bias once), multiply mantissas (with implicit 1), normalize.
- **Floating-point addition:** Align exponents by right-shifting the smaller mantissa, then add, then renormalize.
- **Overflow in two's complement** flips the sign bit; mitigated by **saturation arithmetic**.
- **Underflow** in floating-point produces denormals or flush-to-zero (gradual degradation).
- **Dynamic range (single precision float):** roughly 1536 dB — far beyond human hearing (~120 dB).
- **MAC unit** = multiplier + accumulator + (optional) saturator; the workhorse of every DSP chip.
- **Truncation** introduces downward bias; **rounding to nearest** is unbiased and preferred.
- **Engineering rule:** Match fixed-point word length to ADC resolution + headroom for filter gain.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ADC Quantization Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT504 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ADC Quantization Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** is the process of transforming a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog signal into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital signal. This transformation is performed in three fundamental steps: **Sampling** (discretization in time), **Quantization** (discretization in amplitude), and **Coding** (binary representation of quantized levels).

**Quantization** is the irreversible, non-linear process of mapping a continuous-amplitude sample $x(n)$ to one of $2^B$ discrete amplitude levels, where $B$ is the word-length (number of bits) of the ADC. The difference between the actual analog sample and its quantized representation is termed **Quantization Error** $e_q(n)$.

**Quantization Noise** is the unwanted random disturbance introduced into the digital signal as a direct consequence of the amplitude discretization (rounding/truncation) performed by the quantizer. In a well-designed system, this noise is modeled as an **additive white noise source** superimposed on the input signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The quantizer is treated as a deterministic non-linear system, but its error is statistically analyzed assuming the input is a "busy" signal that rapidly traverses many quantization levels. This is the foundation of the **Stochastic (Statistical) Model of Quantization Error**.

### 1.2 The Three Core Assumptions of the Quantization Noise Model

For a B-bit quantizer, the following assumptions form the bedrock of KTU-level analysis:

1. **Uniformity Assumption:** The quantization step size $\Delta$ is constant across the entire dynamic range of the quantizer (mid-rise or mid-tread type).
2. **Statistical Independence Assumption:** The quantization error $e_q(n)$ at sample $n$ is statistically independent of the input sample $x(n)$ and of all other error samples $e_q(m)$ for $m \neq n$.
3. **Uniform Distribution Assumption (White Noise Model):** The Probability Density Function (PDF) of the quantization error is **uniformly distributed** in the interval $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Staircase vs. Slope Analogy:**
> Imagine a smooth, continuous ramp (your analog signal) and a child trying to draw it on grid paper using only blocky, stair-step shapes. The child cannot draw a smooth diagonal line; they can only fill whole squares. The "error" is the small unfilled triangular area between the ideal smooth slope and the jagged staircase. As the grid gets finer (more bits $\rightarrow$ smaller step size $\Delta$), the staircase hugs the ramp more tightly, and the "error" (noise) becomes visually and audibly negligible.

**A more vivid analogy:** Think of a digital weighing scale that rounds to the nearest 100 grams. If you place a 4,750-gram object, it reads **5,000 g**. The error is +250 g. ADC quantization is the exact mathematical equivalent of this rounding operation performed billions of times per second on a continuous voltage waveform.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Step Size (Resolution):** $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^B}$
- **Quantization Error Bound:** $\vert e_q(n) \vert \leq \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$
- **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR):** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$
- **Dynamic Range:** $\text{DR} = 20 \log_{10}(2^B) = 6.02B \text{ dB}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Quantization Process

The quantization operation can be understood in distinct logical steps:

- **Step 1: Input Conditioning.** The continuous analog input $x(t)$ is bounded to a finite Full-Scale (FS) range $[-V_{FS}/2, +V_{FS}/2]$ to prevent quantizer saturation/clipping.
- **Step 2: Level Definition.** The dynamic range is partitioned into $2^B$ equally-spaced intervals. Each interval has a width equal to the step size $\Delta$.
- **Step 3: Decision and Rounding.** For every input sample $x(n)$, the quantizer selects the discrete level $x_Q(n)$ nearest to $x(n)$. Mathematically, $x_Q(n) = Q[x(n)] = \Delta \cdot \text{round}\left(\dfrac{x(n)}{\Delta}\right)$.
- **Step 4: Error Generation.** The quantization error is computed as the algebraic difference: $e_q(n) = x_Q(n) - x(n)$.
- **Step 5: Binary Coding.** The selected level index is mapped to a B-bit binary word for storage or transmission.
- **Step 6: Noise Modeling.** For analysis, the actual quantizer block is replaced by an equivalent additive noise model: $x_Q(n) = x(n) + e_q(n)$, where $e_q(n)$ is a zero-mean, uniformly distributed random process.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why is it irreversible?** Because two infinitely close analog values (e.g., 1.4999 V and 1.5001 V) get mapped to the *same* discrete level (1.5 V). The original information is fundamentally lost.
- **Why uniform distribution?** Because for a high-resolution quantizer with a complex (busy) input signal, the input sample is equally likely to fall anywhere within the $\Delta$-wide interval around the quantization level, making the error PDF rectangular.
- **Why is it modeled as white noise?** Because the error samples are uncorrelated (statistically independent), which mathematically is the discrete-time definition of a white process.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Step Size (Resolution) | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^B}$ | Volts (V) per LSB |
| 2 | Quantization Error | $e_q(n) = x_Q(n) - x(n)$ | Volts; $\vert e_q \vert \leq \Delta/2$ |
| 3 | Error Mean | $\mu_{e_q} = E\{e_q(n)\} = 0$ | Volts; zero-mean property |
| 4 | Noise Variance (Power) | $\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | V²; fundamental formula |
| 5 | RMS Noise Voltage | $V_{n,rms} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}$ | Volts |
| 6 | Noise Power Spectral Density | $P_{e_q}(f) = \dfrac{\sigma_{e_q}^2}{f_s} = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12 f_s}$ | V²/Hz; flat (white) spectrum |
| 7 | Sinusoidal Signal Power | $P_s = \dfrac{A^2}{2}$ | V²; for $x(t) = A\sin(2\pi f t)$ |
| 8 | Full-Scale Sinusoid Power | $P_{s,FS} = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2}{8}$ | V²; amplitude $A = V_{FS}/2$ |
| 9 | SQNR (Linear) — Full-Scale Sine | $\text{SQNR} = \dfrac{P_s}{P_n} = \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot 2^{2B}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 10 | SQNR (dB) — Full-Scale Sine | $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02B + 1.76$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 11 | Peak SQNR (dB) | $\text{SQNR}_{peak} = 6.02B$ | dB; when $P_s$ replaces with $V_{FS}^2/2$ |
| 12 | Dynamic Range | $\text{DR} = 6.02B$ | dB; theoretical maximum |
| 13 | Bit Resolution from SNR | $B = \dfrac{\text{SNR}_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Bits; engineering design formula |
| 14 | Loading Factor Adjustment | $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02B + 1.76 + 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A}{V_{FS}/2}\right)$ | dB; for non-full-scale inputs |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Engineering (CD Standard):** The 16-bit CD audio standard is derived directly from this analysis. It yields $\text{SQNR} = 6.02(16) + 1.76 \approx \mathbf{98.08 \text{ dB}}$, which exceeds the dynamic range of human hearing (~96 dB).
- **Telecommunications (PCM):** The 8-bit $\mu$-law and A-law companding codecs used in digital telephony rely on quantization noise analysis to ensure that voice quality meets ITU-T standards.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT):** The bit-depth of ADC converters in MRI machines (typically 12-16 bits) is chosen using these exact formulas to ensure diagnostic image fidelity.
- **Seismic and Radar DSP:** Oversampling followed by digital filtering reduces quantization noise by $3 \text{ dB}$ per octave of oversampling—a direct engineering application of the white-noise assumption.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Quantization Error Variance (Mean-Square Noise Power)

**Problem Statement:** Derive the variance $\sigma_{e_q}^2$ of the uniformly distributed quantization error in the interval $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.

**Step 1 — Define the Uniform Probability Density Function.**

Since the error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed with zero mean within the interval $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$, the PDF is:

$$p(e_q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{\Delta} & \text{for } -\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Total Probability Condition.**

The integral of the PDF over the entire real line must equal 1:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} p(e_q) \, de_q = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \, de_q = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \Delta = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms our PDF is correctly normalized.

**Step 3 — Compute the Mean (Expected Value).**

The first moment of the error distribution is:

$$\mu_{e_q} = E\{e_q\} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q \cdot p(e_q) \, de_q = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q \cdot \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \, de_q$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral.**

By the symmetry of the integrand (an odd function over a symmetric interval), the integral evaluates to zero:

$$\mu_{e_q} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \dfrac{e_q^2}{2} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \left( \dfrac{\Delta^2}{8} - \dfrac{\Delta^2}{8} \right) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Mean-Square Value (Second Moment).**

$$\overline{e_q^2} = E\{e_q^2\} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot p(e_q) \, de_q = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, de_q$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the Second Moment Integral.**

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \dfrac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} \left( \dfrac{\Delta^3}{8} - \left(-\dfrac{\Delta^3}{8}\right) \right) = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta^3}{12}$$

**Step 7 — Conclude the Variance.**

Since the mean is zero, the mean-square value equals the variance:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{e_q}^2 = P_n = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12} \text{ (V}^2\text{)}}$$

This is the **fundamental quantization noise power formula** in the KTU syllabus.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: SQNR for a Full-Scale Sinusoidal Input

**Problem Statement:** A B-bit ADC with full-scale range $V_{FS}$ quantizes a sinusoidal input $x(t) = A\sin(2\pi f_0 t)$. Derive the SQNR in decibels for the case where $A = V_{FS}/2$ (full-scale sinusoid).

**Step 1 — Express the Step Size.**

$$\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^B}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Signal Power.**

For a sinusoid of peak amplitude $A = V_{FS}/2$, the average power over a full period is:

$$P_s = \dfrac{A^2}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2} \right)^2 = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2}{8}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Noise Power.**

Using the result from Derivation 1:

$$P_n = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12} = \dfrac{1}{12} \left( \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^B} \right)^2 = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 4^B} = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 2^{2B}}$$

**Step 4 — Form the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Linear).**

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{linear}} = \dfrac{P_s}{P_n} = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2 / 8}{V_{FS}^2 / (12 \cdot 2^{2B})} = \dfrac{12 \cdot 2^{2B}}{8} = \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot 2^{2B}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Decibels.**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot 2^{2B} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Expand the Logarithm.**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right) + 10 \log_{10}\left(2^{2B}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the Constants.**

$$10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(0.75) \approx -1.249 \approx 1.76 \text{ (with sign correction as } +1.76\text{)}$$

$$10 \log_{10}\left(2^{2B}\right) = 20B \log_{10}(2) = 20B \cdot 0.30103 = 6.02B$$

**Step 8 — Final Combined Result.**

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02B + 1.76 \text{ dB}}$$

This is the **"6 dB-per-bit rule with 1.76 dB offset"** that forms the cornerstone of KTU quantization noise problems.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Quantization Noise Power Spectral Density

**Problem Statement:** Show that the Power Spectral Density (PSD) of the quantization error is white and equals $\Delta^2 / (12 f_s)$.

**Step 1 — Define the Autocorrelation Function.**

For a zero-mean white noise process with variance $\sigma_{e_q}^2$, the autocorrelation is an impulse:

$$r_{e_q}(k) = E\{e_q(n) e_q(n+k)\} = \sigma_{e_q}^2 \cdot \delta(k)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Wiener-Khinchin Theorem.**

The PSD is the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of the autocorrelation:

$$P_{e_q}(\omega) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} r_{e_q}(k) e^{-j\omega k} = \sigma_{e_q}^2 \cdot \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(k) e^{-j\omega k} = \sigma_{e_q}^2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Variance.**

$$P_{e_q}(\omega) = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12} \quad \text{for } \vert \omega \vert \leq \pi$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Frequency Variable $f$.**

Using the substitution $\omega = 2\pi f / f_s$, and integrating PSD over $[-f_s/2, f_s/2]$ to get total power:

$$P_{e_q}(f) = \dfrac{\sigma_{e_q}^2}{f_s} = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12 f_s} \quad \text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation.**

The PSD is **flat (constant)** across the entire Nyquist band $[-f_s/2, +f_s/2]$. This means quantization noise is uniformly spread across all frequencies—a property that enables the use of oversampling + digital filtering to reduce in-band noise by trading off sampling rate against resolution.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix (Worked Examples)

#### Example Problem A (Part-A Style, 3 Marks)

**Q:** A 10-bit ADC has a full-scale range of $\pm 5$ V. Calculate the quantization step size and the RMS noise voltage.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Step size calculation.

$$\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^B} = \dfrac{10 \text{ V}}{2^{10}} = \dfrac{10}{1024} = 9.766 \text{ mV/LSB}$$

Step 2: Noise power.

$$P_n = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12} = \dfrac{(9.766 \times 10^{-3})^2}{12} = 7.948 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}^2$$

Step 3: RMS noise voltage.

$$V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{P_n} = \sqrt{7.948 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.819 \text{ mV}$$

---

#### Example Problem B (Part-B Style, 14 Marks)

**Q:** A 12-bit ADC is used to digitize an audio signal. The input is a sinusoid with peak amplitude $A = 2$ V, and the full-scale range is $\pm 5$ V. Compute (a) the SQNR in dB assuming a full-scale input, and (b) the actual SQNR for the given input amplitude.

**Solution Part (a) — Full-Scale SQNR:**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02B + 1.76 = 6.02(12) + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = \mathbf{74.0 \text{ dB}}$$

**Solution Part (b) — Actual SQNR with Loading Factor:**

The loading factor is the ratio of actual amplitude to full-scale amplitude:

$$\text{Loading Factor} = \dfrac{A}{V_{FS}/2} = \dfrac{2}{5} = 0.4$$

The penalty in dB is:

$$20 \log_{10}(0.4) = -7.958 \text{ dB}$$

Therefore, the actual SQNR is:

$$\text{SQNR}_{actual} = 6.02(12) + 1.76 + 20\log_{10}(0.4) = 74.0 - 7.96 = \mathbf{66.04 \text{ dB}}$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Quantization Noise Modeling Flow

The diagram below maps the complete signal-flow architecture of an ADC system, showing where the quantization error is injected and how it propagates through the digital signal chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    analogIn["Analog Input Signal x(t)"] --> sampleHold["Sample and Hold Circuit"]
    sampleHold --> quantizer["B-bit Quantizer Q[.]"]
    quantizer --> codedOut["Binary Coded Output yB(n)"]
    quantizer --> errorGen["Error Generator e_q(n) = xQ(n) - x(n)"]
    quantizer --> noiseModel["Additive Noise Model xQ(n) = x(n) + e_q(n)"]
    errorGen --> pdf["Uniform PDF over -Delta/2 to +Delta/2"]
    pdf --> stats["Zero Mean, Variance Delta squared over 12"]
    stats --> sqnr["SQNR Calculation: 6.02B + 1.76 dB"]
    noiseModel --> sqnr
    codedOut --> reconDAC["Reconstruction DAC"]
    reconDAC --> lpFilter["Low Pass Reconstruction Filter"]
    lpFilter --> analogOut["Reconstructed Analog Signal y(t)"]
```

The figure below depicts the canonical uniform probability density function of the quantization noise. The horizontal axis represents the error magnitude in volts, and the vertical axis represents the probability density.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    origin --> negHalf["-Delta/2"]
    origin --> posHalf["+Delta/2"]
    pdfBlock["Uniform PDF p_eq = 1/Delta for -Delta/2 to +Delta/2, else 0"]
    pdfBlock --> area["Total Area under PDF = 1"]
    pdfBlock --> msq["Mean Square Value = Delta squared / 12"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define quantization error. Explain the statistical model of quantization error with its key assumptions.**

**Model Answer:**

Quantization error is the difference between the actual analog sample and its quantized digital representation. For a sample $x(n)$ and its quantized version $x_Q(n)$, the error is defined as:

$$e_q(n) = x_Q(n) - x(n)$$

The **statistical (stochastic) model** treats the quantizer as a deterministic non-linear system, but the *error* $e_q(n)$ is modeled as an additive, zero-mean, white random process. The three key assumptions are:

1. The error $e_q(n)$ is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.
2. The error sequence $e_q(n)$ is stationary, white, and uncorrelated with the input signal $x(n)$.
3. The input signal $x(n)$ is "busy" — it rapidly traverses many quantization levels, so the error samples are statistically independent.

The model allows us to replace the quantizer with a linear additive-noise model: $x_Q(n) = x(n) + e_q(n)$.

---

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Derive the expression for the mean-square value of quantization noise.**

**Model Answer:**

Assuming a uniform distribution of the error $e_q$ in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$, the PDF is $p(e_q) = 1/\Delta$ within this interval and zero elsewhere.

The mean-square value is the second moment of this distribution:

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = E\{e_q^2\} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \, de_q$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \dfrac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \dfrac{1}{3\Delta} \cdot \dfrac{2\Delta^3}{8} = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

Therefore, the **mean-square quantization noise power is** $\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ V².

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

#### Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (14 Marks)

**Q.** (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the statistical model of quantization error in an ADC. List the assumptions involved. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An 8-bit ADC has a full-scale range of $\pm 5$ V. Calculate the quantization step size, quantization noise power, RMS noise voltage, and the SQNR in dB for a full-scale sinusoidal input. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Statistical Model and Assumptions:**

The block diagram consists of the analog input $x(n)$ entering an adder. The quantizer $Q[\cdot]$ is replaced by a parallel path where $x(n)$ is added directly to the quantization error $e_q(n)$ to produce $x_Q(n)$. The assumptions are:

1. The quantization error $e_q(n)$ is uniformly distributed.
2. The error is statistically independent of the input $x(n)$.
3. The error samples are mutually uncorrelated (white noise).
4. The input is sufficiently complex (busy) for these assumptions to hold.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculations:**

Step 1: Step size.

$$\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^B} = \dfrac{10}{2^8} = \dfrac{10}{256} = 0.03906 \text{ V} = 39.06 \text{ mV}$$

Step 2: Quantization noise power.

$$P_n = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12} = \dfrac{(0.03906)^2}{12} = \dfrac{1.526 \times 10^{-3}}{12} = 1.272 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}^2$$

Step 3: RMS noise voltage.

$$V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{P_n} = \sqrt{1.272 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01128 \text{ V} = 11.28 \text{ mV}$$

Step 4: SQNR in dB.

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02B + 1.76 = 6.02(8) + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = \mathbf{49.92 \text{ dB}}$$

---

#### Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.** (a) Derive the expression for the signal-to-quantization noise ratio (SQNR) of a B-bit ADC for a full-scale sinusoidal input. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A music company wants to digitize audio signals with an SQNR of at least 90 dB. The input is a full-scale sinusoid. Determine the minimum number of bits required for the ADC. Also, calculate the actual SQNR achieved for a non-full-scale input with peak amplitude 70% of the full-scale range. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of SQNR for Full-Scale Sine Input:**

For a B-bit quantizer, the step size is $\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^B$. The signal power for a full-scale sinusoid ($A = V_{FS}/2$) is $P_s = A^2/2 = V_{FS}^2/8$. The noise power is $P_n = \Delta^2/12 = V_{FS}^2/(12 \cdot 2^{2B})$. Therefore:

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{linear}} = \dfrac{P_s}{P_n} = \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot 2^{2B}$$

In decibels:

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{3}{4} \cdot 2^{2B}\right) = 20B \log_{10}(2) + 10 \log_{10}(3/4) = 6.02B + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

**Part (b) — Bit Calculation for Required SQNR:**

Step 1: Solve for B.

$$B = \dfrac{\text{SQNR}_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02} = \dfrac{90 - 1.76}{6.02} = \dfrac{88.24}{6.02} \approx 14.66$$

Step 2: Round up to the next integer.

$$B_{\min} = 15 \text{ bits}$$

Verification: $\text{SQNR} = 6.02(15) + 1.76 = 92.06 \text{ dB}$ (exceeds 90 dB ✓)

Step 3: Calculate SQNR for 70% loading factor.

$$\text{Loading Factor} = 0.70$$

Penalty term:

$$20 \log_{10}(0.70) = -3.098 \text{ dB}$$

Actual SQNR for 15-bit ADC:

$$\text{SQNR}_{actual} = 6.02(15) + 1.76 + 20\log_{10}(0.70) = 92.06 - 3.10 = \mathbf{88.96 \text{ dB}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget the **+1.76 dB offset** in the SQNR formula and write only $6.02B$. This costs **2 full marks** in a 14-mark question.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** When the input is *not* full-scale, the loading factor penalty $\left(20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A}{V_{FS}/2}\right)\right)$ **must** be added. Neglecting it loses another **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In bit-resolution problems, students compute $B$ as a decimal (e.g., 14.66) and forget to **round up** to the next integer, since a fractional bit is physically impossible. Always round UP.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing the **signal power** formula for a sinusoid ($A^2/2$) with that for a square wave ($A^2$). Read the input waveform specification carefully.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — ADC Quantization Noise**

- [ ] **Step Size Formula:** $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^B$ — *Always convert full-scale range to peak-to-peak before division.*
- [ ] **Noise Power Formula:** $P_n = \Delta^2/12$ — *Applies only for uniform mid-rise or mid-tread quantizers.*
- [ ] **RMS Noise Voltage:** $V_{n,rms} = \Delta/\sqrt{12}$ — *Useful when given as a circuit parameter.*
- [ ] **The Golden Formula:** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02B + 1.76$ dB — *Memorize and apply in every problem.*
- [ ] **The 6 dB Rule:** Each additional bit of resolution improves SQNR by **6.02 dB**, equivalent to a 4× improvement in linear noise power.
- [ ] **Dynamic Range:** $\text{DR} = 6.02B$ dB — *Equals the peak SQNR, achieved only for full-scale square-wave-like inputs.*
- [ ] **White Noise PSD:** $P_{e_q}(f) = \Delta^2/(12 f_s)$ V²/Hz — *Uniform across Nyquist band, enabling oversampling benefits.*
- [ ] **Loading Factor Penalty:** A 50% amplitude input loses **6.02 dB** of SQNR; a 10% input loses **20 dB**.
- [ ] **Statistical Assumptions:** (1) Uniform PDF, (2) Zero mean, (3) White spectrum, (4) Independent of input.
- [ ] **Real-World Benchmarks:** CD audio = 16-bit (98 dB); Studio Pro = 24-bit (146 dB); Telephone PCM = 8-bit (50 dB).
- [ ] **Design Formula:** $B = (\text{SQNR}_{dB} - 1.76)/6.02$ — *Always round UP to the next integer bit.*
- [ ] **Oversampling Benefit:** Each 2× increase in $f_s$ reduces in-band noise by **3 dB** (equivalent to 0.5 bits of resolution).
- [ ] **Failure Condition:** If the input exceeds $V_{FS}/2$, **clipping** occurs — this is non-linear and invalidates the statistical model entirely.
